I’ve improved to two pair (Q♦ on the board + my Q♣T♦ → Qs & Ts). Elon’s flop check‑raise to 1,000 polarized his range to sets (33/22), very strong Qx, some two‑pair combos and occasional bluffs. After the Ten on the turn I’m ahead of most of that range (Q3/Q2 and single‑pair Qx are now behind me); the only realistic hands that beat me are sets (and an unlikely pocket Ten turning trips). This is a very dry board, so I should extract value and deny free cards. I’ll bet about half the pot to get called by worse Qx and thin value hands while keeping the pot manageable against a polarized raise. If Elon reacts with a large, polarized raise I’ll re‑evaluate (lean to folding to a shove that commits me given sets are in his check‑raise range, but will call reasonable single raises).

Pocket 66 remains a strong overpair relative to the lower board cards, ahead of Minni's likely calling range (Qx, lower pairs, draws like J9/KJ for gutshots/straights now on Ts turn, spade flush draws). Her line—defending wide preflop vs button raise, leading flop into position (often thin value or probes), calling my flop raise, and now turn leading ~1/3 pot—suggests thin value (QJ/QT), float turning into value with 10x or draw. Board brings straight draws (KJ, J9), backdoor spade flush, but no strong completed hands beyond Q10/set. Raising extracts value from worse Qx/draws, protects vs overcards/draws improving, and leverages fold equity given her 6200 stack (cannot call off light). Flat call risks giving free river or facing larger river bet; check-fold too weak. History shows Minni check-calls passively but folds to turn aggression when beat (e.g., Hand 20 river fold). Raise sizing to ~2.7x her bet (3500 total) builds pot without overcommitting, leaving her ~2700 behind into ~9200 pot for river fold equity/value. Position allows control.

I’ve turned two pair (Q♦ on board + my Q♣T♦ = Qs & Ts). Elon’s line — check‑raise flop and a large raise on the turn to 3,500 — is polarized toward sets (33/22), trips (QQ/rare TT) and some strong/creative value hands, but it will still contain Qx, turn‑missed bluffs and thin value that I beat. The pot is 7,450 and calling 2,200 requires ~22.8% equity; my two‑pair has enough equity against a polarized raising range to justify seeing the river. Min‑raising or shoving here is thin — it bloats the pot and will be called by the very hands that beat me, while folding now surrenders a lot of equity. I’ll call 2,200 more (3,500 total) to realize equity, keep the river decision open, and plan to fold to a shove or continue cautiously if the river is a scary card; extract value if he checks or gives a small river price.

Board: 3c 2h Qd Ts Th. My hand: Qc Td — that’s a full house (Tens full of Queens). With three tens accounted for (Td + Ts + Th), quads are impossible and the only hand that truly beats me is pocket Queens (only 1 combo left). Two QT combos (Qc is in my hand, so only QhTc/QsTc) would tie; most of Elon’s polarized check-raise/turn-raise range (sets, single-Qx, KQ/AQ, bluffs) now lose to my full house. Pot = 9,650 and I have 4,000 behind. Shoving maximizes value: if he folds I take the whole pot; if he calls I’m ahead vs almost his entire continuing range and only narrowly behind the tiny QQ combination. A small bet risks folding out some worse hands and enables him to raise; with my stack size an all‑in captures maximum value and prevents him from leveraging a check‑raise or river shove on a small sizing. I will shove all‑in for 4,000.

Pocket 6s entered as a speculative hand preflop from button, but the Q-high flop made it an underpair immediately, though raised for value/protection assuming wide defender range. Her donk-call flop, lead-call turn aggression, and now all-in river jam after board pairs 10s screams showdown value: likely Qx (top pair QQ or Q10 two pair), 10x (trips), or sets. Pair of 6s loses to all of that. No draws completed meaningfully, and her line is too strong for bluffs given passive history but stubborn value defense here. Pot odds ~3.4:1 effective (13650 pot +4000=17650 for 4000 call needs ~19% equity), but equity <10% vs her range. Tournament stage mid (hand 38/150), deep effective stacks (mine 18k), no need to hero call off 4000 with marginal pair into polarized jam. Preserve stack, fold.

I am on the button with 4h 2h, a weak suited gapper. While being in position is advantageous, the current table dynamics involve significant aggression from both Minni and Elon, who are the blinds. My stack is the shortest at 75BB, and 42s does not play well against 3-bets or high post-flop aggression. Folding is the most disciplined choice here to wait for a better spot.

I’m in the small blind with 5♦4♠ — a weak, disconnected offsuit hand and I’ll be out of position postflop. The extra 50 to see a flop (total commitment 100) is cheap and keeps the pot small; open‑raising to 200 risks a 3‑bet from an aggressive big blind and would commit too many chips with little postflop playability, while folding concedes only 50 but is overly tight here. I’ll complete to see the flop, plan to fold to a sizable 3‑bet, and otherwise proceed cautiously OOP — realizing equity on favorable, dry flops and avoiding bloated pots.

Preflop in BB facing a SB limp after BTN fold, heads-up. K7o is a marginal hand with a strong king blocker and decent high-card value. SB limps suggest a wide range, often weak or speculative hands. Raising light here applies pressure, builds the pot when ahead, and takes advantage of my position and deep stacks. My aggressive image from prior hands (e.g., raising A9o, 44 successfully) supports continuing to steal initiative. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is standard sizing to isolate or fold out junk. Checking risks multi-street pot control issues postflop with a gappy offsuit hand.

5♦4♠ is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand and I’m out of position against an aggressive big blind. Folding loses only 50 but is too tight here; a reraise to the min (500) would be an aggressive bluff that I don’t want to commit to OOP with little playability. Calling the extra 200 (total commitment 300) gives decent pot odds (need ~25% equity) to see a flop and try to realize equity on favorable, low or connected boards while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll call and proceed very cautiously postflop — check/fold to heavy action or continue only with clear improvement or strong draws.

I have 5♦4♠ on a 6♦-2♠-3♠ flop — that’s a made 6-high straight (2-3-4-5-6). Against Elon’s preflop raising range this is almost certainly the best hand on the flop (only exact 5-4 combos would tie). The board is low and coordinated and there are two spades, so spade draws and the remote possibility of a higher straight on later streets exist, but right now I should be extracting thin value from single pairs, overcards and spade draws. Betting for value and to charge draws is correct; I’ll use a controlled size to get called without bloating the pot too much OOP. Bet ~half-pot (300) to get value and to protect against free turn cards. If Elon raises significantly I’ll re-evaluate (call moderate raises given my current equity, but be wary if the action commits too much of my stack).

Preflop, I raised K7o light from the BB after SB limp to apply pressure with position and build a pot OOP when ahead against a wide limping range. Flop 6d2s3s is highly coordinated: two spades for flush draws, straight draws like 45, 4s5s, and low pairs/set mining. My Kc7h has complete air—no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), no flush draw, just a king high card with poor blockers. Minni's SB limp-call pre indicates a wide speculative range (suited connectors, Ax suited spades, low pairs, broadway), many of which connect here (e.g., 45, A3s, 62s, 32s, sets). Her 300 bet into 900 pot (1/3 pot) is standard for value, protection, or semi-bluff. OOP, calling gives up initiative, faces multi-street aggression with no equity, and pot odds (300 into 1200 = ~25% needed) are insufficient against her range where I have <30% equity typically (king high rarely good). Raising has fold equity but commits me deeper OOP with air on a drawy board, risking stacks to draws that call. Folding is disciplined GTO play to realize fold equity preflop and preserve stack for premium spots in tournament with deep stacks (18k effective). Minni's history shows some aggression but also folds to pressure; however, this spot exploits my preflop investment without overplaying.

4♠6♦ offsuit is a weak, non‑connecting hand with very little playability. I have positional advantage on the button, and the cost to see a flop is small (100 into a 150 pot), so I prefer to realize equity cheaply rather than open‑raise and risk getting called or 3‑bet by Elon (aggressive SB) or Flash (shorter stack who defends wide). Plan: call 100 to see the flop, fold to any sizable preflop 3‑bet, and proceed cautiously postflop — check/control the pot unless I hit a real hand (pair/two‑pair/strong draw) or get a clear spot to bluff.

AQs is a premium hand, suited connectors with ace-high and flush potential. Facing a limp from the button (Minni), which is typically a weak or speculative hand. As SB, I should raise to isolate against the button's wide limping range and potentially fold out the BB's blinds. Raising builds the pot OOP with a strong hand while denying equity to worse hands. Standard sizing: 3x BB (300) + 1x limp (100) = 400 total. Pot will be 250 + 400 -100 (since limp 100) wait, no: current pot 250, I raise to 400 (putting in 350 more), pot becomes 250+350=600. Minni and Flash face 300 to call into 600. Good odds but my range strong. Deep stacks allow postflop play.

I am in the Big Blind with Q6o. Minni limped from the button, and Elon raised to 400 from the Small Blind. Q6o is a weak hand that does not play well out of position, especially against an isolation raise from a player like Elon who has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Calling 300 more would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a marginal hand and poor visibility. Therefore, the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities is to fold.

I limped intending to see a cheap flop, but Elon’s raise to 400 converts this into a heads‑up spot where I must call 300 more into a ~600 pot. I hold 4♠6♦ — a weak offsuit, non‑connecting hand with very poor equity and limited playability postflop. Calling requires ~33% equity (300 into ~900 pot) which 4‑6o won’t have versus a typical raising range, and a 3‑bet bluff here would bloat the pot unnecessarily against an aggressive, capable opponent. Preserving my 13.85k stack for better spots is correct — fold.

K6s is a strong suited king-high hand from the button facing only the big blind after the small blind likely folded (pot 150, to call 100). Position is excellent for post-flop play. Minni defends wide from BB, but raising builds the pot when ahead and applies fold equity. Standard button open-raise sizing of 3x BB (300) steals blinds often or plays in position with initiative. Deep stacks support speculative value. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pots or getting raised off equity.

I am in the Small Blind with a small pocket pair (2d 2s). Elon has raised to 300 from the button. In a 3-handed game, a pocket pair has significant value, though 22 is the weakest. My stack size of 7350 (73.5 BB) allows for some set-mining, as the call of 250 is less than 4% of my stack and the implied odds are good if I hit a set against Elon's wide button-opening range. I will call to see the flop, keeping in mind that I'll likely need to fold if I don't improve and face heavy aggression, unless the board texture is very specific.

Facing a button raise to 300 and a small-blind call, my 8♦4♠ is pure garbage — offsuit, unconnected, no blockers and very poor multiway equity. Calling 200 more into the 700 pot (pot would be ~900) requires roughly ~22% equity to break even; against a raiser’s range plus a caller my actual equity with 8-4o is well below that and I’d be out of position postflop with little playability. A light 3-bet bluff here is also unattractive — Elon shows aggression and is likely to call/4-bet, while Flash’s call means I’m often facing two players. With a healthy stack (13,750) and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the higher EV play. Fold.

I have a pocket pair of deuces and missed my set on an Ace-King high flop. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Elon) range. I am out of position and have very little equity here. I will check and plan to fold to most bet sizes, as my hand is effectively just a weak bluff catcher at this point.

Preflop, raised to 300 from button with K6s, Flash called from SB (wide defending range), Minni folded, pot 700. Flop Kh3hAs hits top pair (kings) with weak 6 kicker, initiative, and position. Board has flush draw (hearts) and Ace blocker, but Flash's SB calling range includes Ax suited, suited connectors/draws, pairs. Standard continuation bet to deny equity to draws, protect hand, and take advantage of fold equity vs floats. SB checks suggest weakness. Bet ~50-60% pot (400) sizes well for deep stacks, builds pot when ahead, allows fold to raise. Avoid check-back as gives free cards to draws/overs. History shows passive check/calls, but c-bet exploits position and range advantage.

I have a pocket pair of deuces on a board of Ace-King-Three. This is a very unfavorable board for my hand as it hits the pre-flop aggressor's range (Elon) extremely hard. Elon's 400 bet into a 700 pot represents strength or at least a standard continuation bet on a board that favors him. I am out of position and have very few ways to improve my hand (only two deuces left in the deck). Continuing here would be a mistake against his range, so I will fold.

In 3-handed play, opening with T9o from the button is a standard procedure to capture the blinds or play a pot in position. T9o has decent post-flop structure and playability. A 3x raise to 300 is consistent with the established betting patterns at this table and puts immediate pressure on the small and big blinds.

Facing a 300 open from the button, I have Jc2c in the small blind — a weak, low suited hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling 250 to see a flop puts me OOP against the raiser (and possibly the big blind) with a hand that is often dominated and has limited playability beyond a backdoor/low flush. 3‑betting to isolate isn’t attractive either: a min‑raise or larger 3‑bet with J2s is a bluff that can be easily 3‑bet back or exploited, and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with such a marginal holding. With deep but not compelling implied odds and better spots ahead, I’ll fold and preserve my stack.

Facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB) in a 3-handed game with SB folding. My K5h suited has good equity (around 40-45% vs typical button opening range of 40-50% hands), flush and straight potential, and Kh blocker reduces opponent's strong king likelihood. Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 more into 450 pot (2.25:1, needing ~31% equity). Deep stacks (190 BBs effective), OOP but playable postflop with positionally aware play. Folding too tight; shoving too strong. Standard defend: call and realize equity.

On the flop of 9s As 3h, I hold Kh5h, which is king-high with minimal backdoor draws (hearts). This board is ace-high and spade-heavy, hitting villain's button opening range strongly with aces, flush draws, and pairs. I'm OOP with no made hand, no strong draws, and poor playability. Equity realization is low OOP on this wet board vs a wide button range (~50% hands). Standard play is to check, allow villain to c-bet their range, and fold to aggression unless small bet with folds equity. Donk betting is suboptimal with no equity. Deep stacks but villain shorter at 6800 (~68BB effective). Checking preserves stack and realizes equity when checked back.

I have flopped middle pair (Nines) on an Ace-high board. While I have some showdown value, betting into the Big Blind on this texture doesn't accomplish much; an Ace will never fold, and I'd prefer to keep the pot small with a marginal hand. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and potentially catch a bluff or a cheaper showdown on later streets. It also protects me from a check-raise by Elon, who has shown significant aggression in previous hands.

Facing a checked-through flop of 9s As 3h where I hold Kh5h (king-high with backdoor heart draw). The board is ace-high and spade-heavy, favoring villain's button raising range heavily, but villain's flop check-behind caps their range somewhat—no strong aces, top pairs likely betting for value/protection, so more likely medium strength pairs (e.g., 88, TT-JJ, some draws that missed), bluffs, or traps. Turn Jd is a relatively blank card: brings backdoor straight possibilities (e.g., 10-Q gutshot opens slightly) and diamond flush draw, but doesn't complete major draws and doesn't pair the board. No improvement to my hand; still just king-high. Pot is 650, effective SPR ~10x with villain at 6800. OOP, pot control is key with a marginal bluff-catcher—no strong value, thin value bets risk getting raised off equity or called by worse that we don't beat often enough. Betting now invites aggression from villain's polarized range (donk bet looks bluffy), while check allows free card realization or cheap showdown if villain checks again (common with weak hands post-flop checkback). If villain bets turn, excellent spot to fold king-high to likely value/c-bet range. Deep tournament phase (hand 42/150), preserve stack for better spots; no need to force action here vs aggressive button who checked flop.

The turn brings a Jack, which is an overcard to my pair of Nines. Elon has checked for the second time. While I have a pair, the board of A-9-3-J is quite coordinated, and my hand is primarily a showdown value hand at this point. Betting doesn't get many better hands to fold, and most hands that call me will have me beaten (Aces, Jacks, or better). By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see the river for free, maintaining the opportunity to win at showdown if the river is safe.

On the river Td completing a diamond flush draw (Jd Td) and adding straight draw completion possibilities (e.g., Q8 for 8-9-T-J-Q), with the board A<9s>9s Jd Td 3h. Holding Kh5h (king-high, backdoor heart draw missed), no pair or made hand. Preflop button raiser Flash checked behind flop and turn, capping their range significantly: unlikely strong Ax (would c-bet flop for value/protection), top pairs (9x/Jx on turn less likely post-checkback), more probable weak/medium pairs (e.g., 77-88, some Jx that slowed down), missed draws (spade flush draws missed, gutshots), or air planning pot control/showdown. King-high blocks strong broadway combos (KQ) and has good showdown equity vs this capped range OOP. Pot 650, villain eff. 6800 (~10 SPR). Betting thin value risky (polarized donk looks bluffy, invites calls/raises from better); bluffing suboptimal as villain passive (checked behind twice, likely showdowns weak hands). Checking realizes equity at showdown (wins vs bluffs/air/missed draws, loses to pairs but pot-controlled), preserves stack in tournament (hand 42/150, 3-handed). If villain bets, excellent fold equity spot. Standard pot control OOP with marginal bluff-catcher.

I have improved to two pair (Tens and Nines) on the river. While this is a strong hand, the board is highly coordinated, with any King-Queen making a straight and several combinations of better two pairs (like AJ, AT, A9, or JT) being within Elon's range. Elon has checked three times, which often indicates weakness, but he has shown a capacity for aggressive play and trapping in previous hands. Given the pot size and the potential for being behind to a straight or a superior two pair, checking back is the most prudent way to realize my showdown value without risking a check-raise.

I’m on the button with Q♣2♠ facing a raise (I must call 100). This hand is weak and frequently dominated by a raiser’s opening range; offsuit Q2 has poor playability and weak kicker, especially multiway or versus an aggressive opener. Pot is 150 — calling 100 only gets ~40% equity to break even, which Q2o won’t have against a typical raising range. 3-betting as a bluff is thin (no strong blockers and opponents have shown aggression), so the +EV, low-variance line is to fold and wait for a better spot to leverage position and deeper postflop play.

Preflop in SB vs BB after button fold. KJo offsuit is a strong hand in this spot with position advantage postflop and good equity (around 60% vs typical BB calling range). Raising steals dead money (150 in pot) and has fold equity against BB, who defends wide but not always. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300 total. My deep stack (188BB) vs opponent's 73BB allows flexibility. Folding or limping suboptimal.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Elon) opened with a standard raise. In a 3-handed game, AJ is a very strong hand, especially in a blind vs blind situation where the SB's opening range is typically very wide. I am in position for the rest of the hand. I will 3-bet to 900 to take the initiative, extract value from weaker Ax and Kx hands, and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Preflop OOP with KJo facing a 3-bet from BB after my open-raise. KJo has solid equity (~40%) against a typical BB 3-bet range (JJ+, AK, some suited broadways/Axs), blocks villain's top hands like AK/KQ. Calling OOP is poor due to positional disadvantage and difficult postflop play on paired boards or uncoordinated flops. Folding is too tight given dead money in pot (1200) and my deep stack vs villain's 65BB, which provides fold equity. 4-betting applies max pressure, leverages fold equity, and sets up for potential cooler shoves postflop. Sizing to 2400 (2.67x the 3-bet) is standard, balances value and bluffs, pot-committing villain lightly while leaving maneuverability.

Elon's 4-bet to 2400 represents significant strength, especially given the stack depths. While Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong hand in 3-handed play, it performs poorly against a standard 4-betting range, which typically consists of JJ+, AQ+, and some bluffs. If I call, I will be out of position with a stack-to-pot ratio of less than 1, making it nearly impossible to play post-flop without committing the rest of my chips. Shoving is also high variance and likely to be called only by hands that dominate me. Folding allows me to preserve my 65BB stack and wait for a better spot against an aggressive chip leader.

3-handed on the button with ATo offsuit, a strong hand with excellent playability, top of my raising range. Position allows me to realize equity well post-flop against calling ranges from blinds. Opponents have shown aggression but also folding tendencies in history. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) to build pot, take initiative, and potentially isolate or win blinds frequently. Deep stacks support standard sizing; avoids min-raise which invites multiway pots.

Kd 8d is a strong suited King in a 3-handed game, especially from the Small Blind. Elon has been very aggressive from the Button, and a 3-bet here allows me to take the initiative and potentially end the hand immediately. If called, the hand has decent playability and flush potential. Given my stack size relative to Elon's, I want to define his range now rather than playing a bloated pot out of position later.

I have A♣Q♠ in the big blind facing an open to 300 (button) and a 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. Folding is too weak with AQs; calling 900 keeps a bloated 3-way pot with reduced fold equity and allows the SB to realize equity. I want to take the initiative here: 4-betting isolates the SB's 3-bet (Flash has a 55BB stack and has shown willingness to fold to sizable 4-bets) and either wins the pot preflop or leaves me heads-up vs Elon's wide open-raising range, where AQs plays very well postflop.

Sizing: I choose a 4-bet to 3000 total. That is large enough to put serious pressure on Flash's 3-bet and avoid giving Elon extremely good odds to float with marginal hands, but it isn't so huge that I overcommit my entire stack if Elon flats or 5-bets. Plan: if I get folds I take the pot; if called by one or both I'll play aggressively on ace/high flops and proceed postflop for value and fold equity. If either opponent 5-bets shove I will re-evaluate based on who it is and the exact sizing (more inclined to fold to a polarized shove from Elon; willing to call a short-stack shove from Flash when pot odds and equities justify it). Overall a 4-bet to 3000 maximizes fold equity and puts me in the most profitable spots with AQs.

Facing a 3-bet from SB Flash (short stack ~5550) and a large 4-bet to 3000 from BB Minni after my button open-raise to 300 with ATo offsuit. ATo is a strong 3-handed button opening hand with great playability and position, but against a 4-bet, it's often dominated by premium pairs (JJ+) and AK/AQ in Minni's likely polarized range (tight value + some bluffs based on history). Equity realization drops multi-way if Flash continues (he needs to call 2000 more into ~7300 pot post my call, pot-committing his stack). Fold equity on a 5-bet exists vs Flash's wider 3-bet range, but Minni's sizing suggests strength, and shoving 19k deep into 7k pot or smaller raise risks bad SPR/call-off dynamics. History shows opponents aggressive but folding to pressure; however, with my large stack lead (19k vs their 5.5k/10.7k), folding marginal equity here preserves stack for superior post-flop edges later. Pot odds to call (~3.7:1 effective) are decent (~38% equity needed vs tight range), but position compromised multiway preflop, and ATo plays poorly OOP stacks deep. Standard fold to 4-bet in this squeeze spot; wait for better.

Minni's cold 4-bet from the Big Blind after I 3-bet the Button is a sign of immense strength, typically representing a range of JJ+, AK. While I have suited connectors with a King, Kd8d is significantly dominated by her likely range. With a stack of 4550 remaining after my 3-bet, calling 2000 more would commit nearly half my chips to a pot where I am likely a 3:1 underdog. Preserving my 45 big blinds to find a better spot is the higher-EV play here.

I am on the Button with Nine of Clubs and Deuce of Hearts (92o), which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While being on the button provides a positional advantage, this hand has no equity or playability. Given the stack sizes and the recent aggression shown by Minni and Elon in previous hands, attempting a steal or playing this hand is mathematically incorrect. I will fold.

I have 3c-4d in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind. This is a weak, offsuit, low connector — not worth a raise and not worth folding outright given the price. Calling the extra 50 (bringing my total committed to 100) is cheap to see a flop (good pot odds) and lets me realize equity without building a big OOP pot. Postflop plan: play straightforward — fold to heavy aggression if I miss, continue only with a pair or strong draw, and look to take free cards or small pots rather than bloating the pot OOP.

Preflop in the big blind with 3d5s, a speculative but weak offsuit hand with minimal playability. Flash has folded from the button, and Minni has limped from the small blind (effectively posting the blind and checking). Facing no bet with a pot of 200 and deep stacks (mine at 19,350, Minni 14,900, Flash out this hand), checking is optimal to realize implied odds on a cheap flop if I hit a straight draw or pair. Folding is impossible as there's nothing to call, and raising would be suicidal with this holding against a limper. From hand history, players often check passively postflop, so pot control and position out of position postflop is fine for a realization play in a tournament ICM context early (hand 45/150). Goal is long-term stack maximization, not forcing action with trash.

Board: A♣ T♠ 7♠. I hold 3♣4♦ — a complete miss with no real draws (only a very remote runner-runner straight). I’m out of position vs the big blind and pot is only 200 after a cheap preflop call. Best play is pot control: check and concede the initiative. If Elon checks back I get a free turn; if he fires a bet I’ll fold to any meaningful c-bet (especially >~1/3–1/2 pot). I might only consider calling a tiny probe to attempt a turn float, but I won’t bloat the pot OOP with no equity.

Holding 3d5s on a 7s Ac Ts flop gives me no pair, no straight draw (gutshot to 46 unlikely), and only backdoor spade potential with one spade. Complete airball with ~0% equity against any reasonable calling range. Minni limped preflop from SB and checked flop, indicating weakness or trap, but history shows passive checking postflop. Out of position with deep stacks (19k into 200 pot), betting as a bluff risks being called by ace, two pair, sets, or draws, bloating pot OOP with no equity. Optimal play is check to control pot, induce bluffs on later streets if board worsens, or realize thin equity cheaply if runner-runner miracle hits. Folding impossible, calling nothing to call; aligns with tournament stack preservation early (hand 45/150).

I have 3♣4♦ on A♣ T♠ 7♠ 2♣ — complete miss but I picked up a one-card wheel draw (need a 5 on the river; ~4 outs ≈ 8–9%). Pot is only 200 and I’m out of position vs the big blind. No reason to bluff or bloat the pot OOP with very little equity. Plan is to control the pot and see a free river: check here, fold to any meaningful turn bet (especially >~1/3–1/2 pot), but if Elon checks I’ll take my river card and decide then (value bet if I hit the wheel, consider a small bluff only if the texture and opponent weakness justify it).

Holding 3d5s on a 7s Ac Ts 2c board, I have complete air: no pair, no draws (gutshot to 46 straight is negligible ~4% equity, backdoor spade/flush minimal). Minni limped preflop from SB (weak range), checked flop and turn, showing passivity consistent with hand history where opponents frequently check postflop even with showdown value. OOP with deep stacks (19k into 200 pot, hand 45/150), betting/raising as a bluff is -EV: risks villain calling with Ax, Tx, sets, two-pair, or spade draws, bloating pot OOP where I have zero equity or fold equity against a station-like player. Checking preserves stack for better spots, induces bluffs on river if board scares, or cheap showdown if villain is trapping weakness. Optimal pot control in tournament preservation phase maximizes long-term stack growth.

I missed completely with 3♣4♦ on A♣ T♠ 7♠ 2♣ 2♠ and have no showdown value. The pot is only 200 and Elon (BB) checked the flop and turn, which shows a lot of weakness in his line — his river checking range will include many busted draws and weak A/T/X hands that can fold to pressure. A small half‑pot bluff only needs ~33% fold equity to be +EV. My stack is large relative to the pot so the risk is minimal. I’ll fire a small value‑steal: bet 100 to target folds from Kx/Qx/Jx and missed hands; if he raises I’ll fold (don’t want to bloat a pot OOP with no equity); if called I’ll concede at showdown (I’m behind to any real made hand).

Holding 3d5s (5-high, no pair, no draw) on 7s Ac Ts 2c 2s board facing 100 bet into 300 pot from Minni (SB limper, checked flop/turn). Board paired deuces, spade flush possible. Her range after passive play: weak Ax (A3-A9), Tx, 77, 22, missed draws now turning into thin value or bluffs. Pot odds ~4:1 (20% needed), but zero showdown equity vs value (any pair/A-high beats me); only beat air/missed draws, but history shows she bets value thinly. Raise -EV (no fold equity vs stationy profile). Call risks 100 chips (0.5% stack) for no equity gain. Fold preserves 19k stack early in tournament (hand 45/150) for premium spots, aligning with GTO pot control OOP with air.

Hand: T5s on the button, 3-handed with deep stacks. Facing a preflop amount to call 100 into a 150 pot (effective pot odds ~2.5:1) and with position on both opponents, this is a clear defend. T5s has decent playability (backdoor flush/straight potential and occasional two‑pair/top pair), and calling preserves flexibility — I can see a flop and apply pressure or fold to heavy aggression. A tiny raise here would bloat the pot and invite 3‑bets from the deep SB with little fold equity, so I’ll just call and play postflop in position.

A♥2♣ is a weak offsuit ace with poor playability out of position in the small blind. Facing a button limp from Minni (likely wide range), calling 50 into 250 offers decent pot odds but commits us OOP with a hand prone to domination (e.g., Ax where x>2) and limited equity. Raising risks playing a marginal hand post-flop or folding to a 3-bet. With deep stacks (193bb effective vs BB's 54bb), tournament stage (hand 46/150, 3 players), prefer folding to preserve stack for premium spots. Opponent history shows Minni mixes limps but aggressive; no need to speculate here.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Six offsuit. Minni, on the button, has simply called the blind (limped), and Elon in the Small Blind has folded. With a relatively weak hand and the opportunity to see a flop for free, there is no reason to risk chips by raising against a deep-stacked opponent. I will check and see if I can improve on the flop.

I have Jack high on a 9-4-3 rainbow board. I am out of position against Minni, who limped on the button. Since I have no made hand and no draws, I will check to see how the action develops. If she bets, I will likely fold, but there is no reason to bet into her here with no equity.

Flop 3h-4s-9c with Ts5s in position. I have a single overcard (T) to the board and a backdoor spade flush draw (one spade on board) but no made hand or clean straight draw. Both opponents checked, showing weakness. In position this is a good spot to stab a small bet to fold out worse unpaired hands and win the pot now while keeping the pot controllable if I’m called. I’ll bet the minimum-sized probe (100 into a 250 pot) — enough to generate fold equity but cheap enough to fold to meaningful resistance. If I’m raised heavily I’ll assume I’m beat and relinquish the pot; if called I’ll evaluate the turn and proceed cautiously (look to continue only with a pair, a ten/5/spade turn, or strong pot odds to chase).

I am currently holding Jack-high with no draws on a dry board of 9-4-3. Minni has made a small bet of 100 into a 250-chip pot. While the price to call is relatively cheap, my hand has very little equity and no potential to improve significantly on the turn. Against a large stack like Minni's, calling here would likely lead to being pushed off the hand on later streets anyway. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

3-handed on the button with 78o, facing a small raise to 200 (100 to call) into a 150 pot, giving excellent pot odds of 1.5:1. 78o has strong playability for straights and two-pair potential, suited enough with one high card. Likely facing a wide SB opening range from Flash (54BB stack), who limps and folds frequently based on history. BB Minni is deep but checks often. Calling maintains position, realizes equity multiway if BB calls, and allows postflop aggression on favorable boards. Folding too tight this deep in position; 3-betting risks short-stack shove from SB or cold-call from BB. Plan: call, see flop in position, bet/raise if hits flush/straight draws or pair+draw.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Elon has limped from the button, and I only need to commit 50 more chips to see the flop. However, T4o plays very poorly post-flop, and I will be out of position against two players with much larger stacks who have shown significant aggression in previous hands. Even with 5-to-1 pot odds, it is better to fold this trash hand and wait for a more advantageous spot, especially as the short stack in a 3-handed game.

Elon limped from the button and I’m in the big blind with 6♥4♥. Deep stacks but I’m out of position — the hand has some backdoor equity (suit/connectivity) but is otherwise weak and easily dominated. No raise was made, so there’s no need to bloat the pot preflop; I’ll check to see a flop for free and evaluate draws or a pair. Plan: continue only with clear equity (pair, two pair, flush/straight draws) and fold to heavy aggression on dry missed flops. If Elon shows weakness on the flop I can consider a small bluff or float; otherwise keep pot control.

I have 6♥4♥ on J♦ A♣ 8♣ — no pair, no meaningful draw (only runner-runner hearts), and the flop heavily favors a limper’s range (Ax/Jx/club draws). I’m out of position and the pot is small, so I want to keep the pot under control and avoid bloating it with a weak, easily-dominated hand. Check to see Elon’s action: fold to meaningful aggression, but if he checks back I’ll reevaluate the turn and consider a controlled bluff only on very favorable cards.

Flop of Ac-8c-Jd hits my 78o for bottom pair with a weak kicker and a gutshot straight draw to the 9. The board is draw-heavy with a flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., T9). Minni limped preflop from the BB and checked the flop, representing a wide, weak range including Ax, Jx, bluffs, and draws. In position heads-up with a deep stack vs her 15k, I should bet for thin value against her worse pairs/draws, protection against clubs/spades/9s/Ts, and to deny equity. Checking back risks giving free cards to draws or letting her realize she can bluff. Half-pot bet sizes well (pot 250, bet 125-175); 150 extracts value from her calling range while maintaining fold equity. Plan: c-bet 150, barrel turn if ahead or improves.

I have 6♥4♥ on J♦ A♣ 8♣ — this flop completely misses me. There are no hearts on board so a flush is impossible; my only realistic outs are three 4s and three 6s (6 outs). The chance to improve to at least a pair by the river is only ~24%, while calling 150 into the 550 pot requires ≈27% equity to break even. I’m out of position, Elon’s small bet still represents many hands that already beat me (A, J, club draws) and calling invites difficult multi-street decisions with very little fold equity. A raise would be too thin and reckless. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Ace-Seven of spades on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a premium opening hand in this position. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. Given the stack sizes, I want to play aggressively with my strong hands but be wary of the large stacks of Minni and Elon if they decide to play back.

Td2c is a very weak, poorly playable hand and I'm out of position in the small blind. Flash opened to 300; I must call 250 into a 450 pot, which requires roughly 36% equity to break even heads-up — Td2o rarely has that vs a button opening range. There's also a live big blind (Elon) left to act who can call or 3-bet, making multiway play likely and my equity even worse. A small squeeze/3-bet here is thin (BB can punish and I have no fold equity or postflop playability with this holding). With deep stacks and plenty of hands left, the +EV play is to preserve chips and fold.

73o is one of the worst hands in poker: offsuit, disconnected low cards with minimal playability. Facing a button raise from Flash (wide range possible), defending OOP is -EV. Pot odds (200 to call into 450) don't justify it as equity is too low (~25-30% vs button open). Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I have J♥2♠ on the button in a 3‑handed pot — a weak offsuit hand, but position is very valuable. The current raise only costs me 100 more (total 200) to see a flop into a relatively small pot with a deep stack (15,100). Folding would be too nitty; 3‑betting with J2o is unnecessary and speculative. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, see a flop in position, and then fold or pot‑control depending on the texture and opponent action (if the short big blind shoves, I’ll re‑evaluate).

85s is a trash hand from the small blind, acting first post-flop if I call. Button limp suggests weak range, but completing puts me OOP multiway (BB likely checks). Poor playability post-flop, low equity vs calling ranges, minimal fold equity if raising. Deep stacks early in tournament; preserve stack for premium spots. Pot odds ~5:1 insufficient for speculative hand OOP. Standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. The Button (Minni) has limped, and the Small Blind (Elon) folded. In a three-handed game, an Ace is a strong starting hand. I will raise to 400 to take initiative and attempt to win the pot immediately or isolate the limper. A5o is ahead of a typical button limping range, and raising allows me to define the hand better than checking.

Call to 400. J♥2♠ is a weak offsuit hand, but I’m on the button (position) and the extra 300 (3 BB) to see a flop is a small price with a deep stack (15,000). 3‑betting is unnecessary and speculative — I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with junk. Calling keeps the pot controllable and lets me use position post‑flop to realize equity or fold to aggression. If I miss the flop I’ll pot‑control and fold to significant pressure; if I hit a jack or pick up a backdoor draw I can apply pressure or extract value. I’ll re‑evaluate if Flash (shorter stack) shows strength (large 3‑bet/shove).

I was the pre-flop aggressor after raising Minni's button limp. The flop is 3d-Td-8h. While I haven't hit the board, I have Ace-high with the Ace of Spades (backdoor flush potential). C-betting here is a standard move to maintain the initiative and represent a range that includes overpairs or a Ten. Given that Minni limp-called pre-flop, her range is likely capped, and a half-pot bet should be enough to fold out her non-connecting cards or smaller pairs while keeping the pot manageable if I'm called.

I have J♥2♠ on a 3♦/T♦/8♥ flop with no pair and no real draw. Calling 425 into a 1,275 pot gives me ~25% pot‑odds, but my chance to improve to at least a pair by the river (~24%) is barely enough and is overstated because many of those pairs (a jack or a deuce) will still lose to any T/8/3, two‑pair, set or better hands Flash is representing with this continuation bet. Flash is short‑stacked (4,725) and betting/raising here — he can easily shove and force a difficult fold/call decision on later streets. Raising as a bluff risks a shove and committing a lot of chips with garbage. With position but no equity and a competent opponent showing strength, the correct play is to conserve chips and fold now.

3-handed on the button with J5d suited. SB (Flash) has folded, as evidenced by pot of 150 (SB dead 50 + BB 100). BB (Minni) has checked. Excellent position with initiative. J5s is a strong speculative hand in this spot: suited, connector-ish, plays well postflop multiway but especially heads-up in position. Raising steals the pot often (dead SB money), builds it when called, and allows control postflop. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300 given the dynamics and history of loose/passive play with frequent check-downs. BB defends wide but our position and hand equity vs calling range is solid. Avoids marginal call OOP postflop risk.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The Button (Elon), who is the current chip leader and has shown significant aggression in previous hands, has raised to 300. Jack-Seven offsuit is a weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given my stack size of approximately 60 big blinds, there is no reason to defend with a sub-optimal hand against a large stack. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing Elon's button raise to 300 as the big blind with K♦6♠. The pot is 450 and calling 200 (total commitment 300) is cheap relative to my 14.6k stack and I should defend a wide range heads-up. Kx has reasonable showdown value; a 3-bet with K6o is thin (weak kicker, out of position postflop) and folding is too nitty in this spot. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed straightforwardly — continue with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards where I’m clearly behind.

Flop Ac Kc Th. I hold Kd6s — top pair with a weak kicker. Elon was the preflop raiser and checked back, pot is 650 and there’s no bet to call. I’ll bet ~half pot (325). This size extracts value from worse kings and medium pairs, denies free cards to turn/river (reducing chances opponents with backdoor club/straight equity improve for free), and keeps the initiative heads-up. If Elon check-raises big I’ll assume sets/Ax/strong two‑pair and fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn to control the pot given my vulnerable kicker.

Preflop raise with J5dd was standard speculative in position vs BB limp-call, but flop AcKcTh completely misses. Board is extremely strong: two broadway cards plus Ace-King high with club flush draw possible. I have only a gutshot straight draw (4 queens for broadway nuts, ~8-9% equity) and backdoor diamond flush draw, no pair/draw showdown value. Minni's donk-lead of 325 into 975 pot (33% pot) suggests strength: Ax, Kx, Tx, QJc straight draw, club draw, or sets. Her range hits this board hard after limp-calling pre. Pot odds 3:1 require ~25% equity to call, but realized equity much lower vs her value-heavy range due to reverse implied odds on scary board and her likely continuation. Raising as bluff too thin OOP perception and position exploited later. Folding preserves stack for better spots; deepstack tournament, no need to float air here. History shows check-downs but aggression when betting this board warrants fold.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Ten-Eight suited, which is a strong opening hand in this position. My stack of 5,950 represents about 60 big blinds, giving me plenty of room to play. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play a pot in position if called.

I’m on the small blind with 5♦4♦ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need 250 more (I’ve already posted 50), so calling would commit 300 total. 5-4s is a speculative, playable hand with good backdoor/straight/flush potential and the opener (Flash) has an effectively deep stack (~56 BB), so there are implied odds to justify a call. A min 3-bet to 500 is unattractive here — the sizing gives little real fold equity and I’d be more committed out-of-position with a marginal hand. Folding is too tight given stack depths and the button’s wide opening range. Plan: call to see a flop cheaply (total 300). If I miss and face heavy action I’ll fold; if I hit a strong pair or strong draw I’ll continue/raise appropriately. If BB also calls and it becomes multiway I’ll play more cautiously.

Preflop in the big blind with T8o facing a button raise to 300 and a small blind call. T8o is offsuit and marginal at best, with poor playability multiway postflop. Pot odds are 200 to call into 700 (3.5:1), but equity against two players (likely wide button range and calling range) is insufficient, especially given opponents' aggressive tendencies from hand history. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament phase.

Flop: 9h 3s 4s. I hold 5♦4♦ — second pair (pair of 4s) with a weak 5 kicker. Heads-up vs the preflop raiser and I’m out of position; pot is 700 with no bet. This is a low, fairly dry board — I’m ahead of many of his missed overcards but behind any 9x/3x or pocket pairs (and vulnerable to sets). I don’t want to build a big pot OOP with a marginal made hand, so I’ll control the pot and keep ranges wide by checking. Plan: check and call a small-to-medium bet on the turn to see one more card; fold to large, committed aggression unless I improve (trip 4s or pick up strong backdoor equity).

I am the pre-flop aggressor on the button. The flop of 9h-3s-4s is relatively dry, although it does offer some spade draw possibilities. Minni, the small blind, has checked to me. My hand (Tc-8c) has no current pair or direct draw, but I have two overcards to the low cards and a backdoor club flush draw. Standard play here is to continuation bet to capitalize on the initiative and take down the pot, as this board likely missed a significant portion of the small blind's calling range. A half-pot bet of 350 is sufficient to fold out hands like KJ, QJ, or small pocket pairs that might feel vulnerable.

I have 5♦4♦ on a 9♥ 3♠ 4♠ flop — second pair (4s) with a weak 5-kicker, out of position versus the button who opened and now c-bet half‑pot (350 into 700). This is a very common continuation-bet size with a wide range (overcards, some backdoor draws, and some pairs). My hand is ahead of a lot of his missed overcards and some bluffs, but behind any 9x/3x or pocket pairs and vulnerable to spade draws. Calling 350 into a 1050 pot gives me ~25% break-even equity, which is reasonable vs a wide c-betting range. I don’t want to build a big pot OOP with a marginal hand by check-raising or leading; a raise here would be thin and invite difficult decisions on later streets. Plan: call to keep the pot under control and see the turn. If Flash barrels large on the turn (significant raise/overbet) I’ll fold unless I improve (trips, a 5 to pair the board, or a diamond to give a strong backdoor). If the turn checks or is a small bet, I’ll re-evaluate and can barreI for value or shove if I improve to trips/strong two‑pair.

Board: 9♥ 3♠ 4♠ A♣. I hold 5♦4♦ — second pair (4s) with a weak 5-kicker, out of position. Flash opened preflop and c-bet the flop, so his range is wide and includes many missed overcards, but the turn Ace is a scary card that improves a lot of his value combos (Ax) and keeps me behind 9x/3x and pocket pairs. I don’t want to build a big pot OOP with a marginal hand or face a raise that puts me in a commit/fold spot. Optimal line is pot control: check and allow him to bluff or show aggression. Plan: check — call a small-to-medium bet to see a river, but fold to large/committed aggression (overbet/raise/all-in) unless I improve on the river.

The turn brings the Ace of Clubs, which is an excellent card for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely doesn't have an Ace herself, as she would be wary of my ability to have many Ace-high hands. I will follow up my flop continuation bet with a second barrel to represent the Ace and pressure any marginal made hands like a pair of nines or small pocket pairs that she might have called with on the flop. A bet of 850 into a 1400 pot is a strong sizing that should force folds from most of her range that isn't a flush draw or an Ace.

I have 5♦4♦ — second pair (4s) with a weak 5‑kicker, out of position on 9♥ 3♠ 4♠ A♣. Flash opened BTN, c‑bet the flop and now barrels the Ace on the turn; his range includes many Ax, 9x/3x and pocket pairs but also floats/bluffs given the line and sizing. The turn bet is 850 into a 2250 pot — calling 850 gives ~27.4% break‑even equity (850/(2250+850)). I only have 5 clean outs (2 fours + 3 fives ≈ 5/46 ≈ 10.9% to improve), so my raw draw alone is weak, but folding now to a medium turn bet against a wide button opening range is too passive and a raise OOP is thin and invites a tough shove. I’ll call to keep his bluffs in and see the river, planning to fold to a large shove/overbet if I don’t improve. If I pair my 5 or hit a 4 on the river I’ll seek value; if the river checks I’ll re‑evaluate (small value bet vs checking down depending on the river card).

I have second pair (4♦) with a weak 5-kicker, out of position to the button who opened and barreled the flop (350) and the turn (850). His line and sizing weight his range toward Ax, 9x/3x and pocket pairs (value-heavy), though there are some bluffs. The river 6♥ is a relatively blank card for my hand — it helps some 6x but doesn’t meaningfully improve my relative equity. Betting OOP is thin: a value bet will get called by many hands that already beat me, and a bluff risks a raise that puts me in a very uncomfortable commit/fold spot. I prefer pot control and showdown with this marginal made hand. Action: check and see if he barrels; if he fires a small river I’ll consider a call versus a bluff-heavy line, but I’ll fold to large overbets/shoves from his value-heavy range.

On the river, the board is 9h-3s-4s-Ac-6h. I have Ten-high (Tc 8c) with no showdown value. I have been the aggressor throughout the hand, representing a strong range that includes many Ace-high hands and sets. Minni called my flop bet and, more importantly, called my turn bet after the Ace—the ultimate scare card—appeared. Her turn call strongly suggests she either has a pair of Nines, a weak Ace, or a spade flush draw. While the spade draw missed, she still likely holds a pair that beats my Ten-high. While I could attempt a third barrel to push her off a Nine or a missed high-card draw (like King-high spades), her stickiness on the turn suggests she is unlikely to fold for a price that makes the bluff mathematically profitable. In a tournament context with 44 big blinds remaining, I will check back and concede the pot rather than risking a significant portion of my stack on a low-probability bluff against a range that has already called the most threatening card on the board.

I'm on the button with QcJc — a strong, suited connector in position. With two players to act and the pot currently 150 (to call 100), I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and build a pot I can play post-flop in position. A standard 3x BB open to 300 gives good fold equity against the blinds, isolates the short-stacked BB when appropriate, and keeps pot control versus Elon's deeper stack. If I face a large 3-bet I will re-evaluate based on sizing and reads (fold to very large 3-bets, call or 4-bet against moderate ones).

T4o is a trash hand with no connectivity, no suitedness, and poor playability postflop, especially out of position in the SB facing a button raise from Minni, who raises frequently but still has a strong range here. Pot odds of calling 250 into 450 (~2:1) don't justify it given implied odds difficulties OOP with this hand. Flash in BB may act behind but unlikely to improve the spot significantly. Deep stacks allow waiting for premium spots; folding preserves chips for value.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit. In a 3-handed game, KTo is a very strong hand to defend against a Button open. Minni's raise to 3 units (300) is standard. I have 43.5 big blinds, which gives me enough room to play post-flop. Folding would be much too tight, and while a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to keep Minni's wider range in and evaluate on the flop without bloating the pot unnecessarily with a medium-strength holding.

On a dry flop of Qh 9h 4c, I have King-high with one overcard (King) to the highest card and two overcards to the others. In this situation, the standard play out of the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor is to check to the button. Leading out (donk betting) here doesn't make much sense as this board texture often hits a button's opening range well (containing many Qx, 9x, and pocket pairs). I will check and evaluate based on Minni's bet sizing.

I have top pair (Q♣) with a decent J kicker on a very dry flop Q♦–9♥–4♣, heads-up and in position against the BB who defended a wide preflop range. This is a good spot to c-bet for value and protection: many worse Qx, small pairs (9x/4x) and weaker holdings will call, while betting charges the few drawing combos. I choose a roughly 60% pot bet (400 into 650) — large enough to extract value and deny equity to turn cards, but not so large that I overcommit with a marginal one-pair hand. Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn and continue extracting on safe cards; if met with a large raise/shove, give credit to a very strong hand (set/two-pair) and fold/re-assess accordingly.

I have King-high on a Queen-high board with no direct straight or flush draws. While King-Ten had decent pre-flop equity, Minni's 60% pot continuation bet on this texture (Q-9-4) indicates strength or at least a standard C-bet that I cannot profitably call out of position with no equity to improve significantly on the turn. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

3-handed on the button with A8s, an excellent hand with position, suited ace for playability and nut flush potential. Standard to open-raise first in for value and to steal blinds. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is standard sizing to build the pot while keeping it balanced. Flash is shorter (~41BB), Minni deeper; this size pressures both while committing a small portion of my deep stack (18k). Folding or limping is suboptimal.

I have been dealt Ace-King suited, one of the strongest starting hands in poker. I am currently the short stack with 4,100 chips (41 big blinds). Elon opened from the button for 300. In a three-handed game, AKs is a monster. I want to 3-bet for value and to potentially end the hand right here or set up a situation where I can comfortably commit my stack on most flops. A raise to 1,000 is a standard 3-bet sizing that puts pressure on the opener and the big blind while building a pot for my premium holding.

Elon opened to 300 and Flash (SB) 3-bet to 1,000. I’m in the BB with 6♦7♦. Pot is 1,400 and I must put in 900 more (total commitment 1,000). 6-7s is marginal but has very good postflop playability (straights/flushes) and this call is a small fraction of my 16,900 stack (~5%). Flash is relatively short (3,150) so his 3-bet can be wide/isolation-oriented and if Elon folds I’ll be heads-up with decent equity; if Elon calls I may face a multiway pot but I can still realize equity and play cautiously out of position. 4-betting or shoving with 6-7s would be unnecessarily aggressive; folding gives up too much equity for a small amount. Plan: call 1,000 now. On the flop I’ll continue with made hands and strong draws, use pot control with marginal boards, and fold to heavy aggression when I miss completely.

Facing a 3-bet from short-stacked Flash in SB (31BB) over my button open with A8hh, a strong suited ace with great playability, nut flush draw potential, and position on both opponents. Minni's flat-call from BB (deep stack) suggests a speculative or trapping range, but multiway pots favor my hand's equity due to suited broadway playability. Pot odds are excellent: calling 700 into 2300 (effective pot odds ~4.3:1), low commitment (~4% of stack). Folding is too tight preflop IP with this hand. Raising risks bloating pot multiway or facing a jam from Flash/Minni 4-bet, isolating unnecessarily when postflop position and implied odds are strong. Flatting keeps pot manageable, realizes equity, and allows controlled aggression on favorable boards (hearts, aces, broadway). Consistent with GTO vs aggressive shortstack 3-bets.

I have hit top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on an Ace-high board. The pot is currently 3,000 and my remaining stack is 3,150, giving us a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of nearly 1:1. On a board with both flush and straight draws (As 8s Jd), and facing two opponents, it is crucial to protect my hand and extract value from weaker Aces or draws. Given the low SPR, I am effectively committed to the hand; checking risks giving a free card to a spade draw or a straight draw, whereas a smaller bet would make me pot-committed anyway. Moving all-in maximizes the pressure on drawing hands and ensures that I get my stack in while I am likely ahead.

Preflop I called 1,000 as a speculative play with 6♦7♦. On this flop (A♠ 8♠ J♦) Flash has shoved all‑in for 3,150. The pot is 6,150 so calling 3,150 requires ~34% equity to break even (3,150 / (6,150+3,150) ≈ 33.9%). My hand has no made pair and only weak backdoor outs (double‑diamond runner flush ≈ 4.2%, runner‑runner straights ≈ ~3%, and about a ~24% chance to pair one of my cards by the river — many of those still lose to an Ace/Jack). Realistic equity vs a shove/polarized 3‑bet shove range here is well below the ~34% needed. Elon still must act behind and could call or raise, which would put me in a much larger and uncomfortable side‑pot. Raising myself is unattractive (Flash is already all‑in; a raise only creates a side pot vs Elon and commits a large portion of my 16k stack with a very marginal hand). For stack preservation and to wait for a better spot, I fold and save my chips for more favorable situations.

Top two pair (aces and eights) with the nut heart flush draw on a As8sJd board, giving massive equity (flush draw 9 outs, ~35% equity standalone plus two pair ahead of most overpairs, draws, and combo draws in Flash's 3-bet shove range from SB). Flash's line (preflop 3-bet vs button open, then massive flop overbet all-in after Minni's flat and fold) is polarized: value like sets (JJ unlikely given board, AA impossible with my Ah), AJs, or bluffs/draws (spade draws, gutshots). Pot odds excellent (~2:1, calling 3150 into 6150), low commitment (~18% of deep stack), no fold equity needed since all-in. Calling realizes equity perfectly heads-up in position, maximizes EV vs shorter all-in stack. Folding too tight; raising impossible.

I am on the button with Queen-Trey offsuit. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, this hand is significantly below the opening range for a three-handed game against active opponents like Minni and Elon. Given the recent history of high aggression and my current stack size, there is no need to play such a weak hand from the button. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with K♣6♠: marginal but playable. I want initiative and to avoid letting the big blind see a cheap flop with a wide range, so I open-raise. A standard sizing of 300 total (≈3bb) balances fold equity and pot control with deep stacks — big enough to take down the pot often, small enough to preserve manoeuvrability against Elon's demonstrated 3-bet aggression. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet, call small/medium 3‑bets very selectively, and c-bet favorable flops to capitalize on position postflop.

Facing a standard 3x raise from the SB after BTN folds, in the BB with Qh6h suited. This is a playable defending hand vs a likely wide SB opening range, which is often stealing. Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 into a pot that will be 600 heads-up. Q6s has decent equity (~35-40% vs a 40% range), straight/flush potential, and position OOP but can realize equity multi-street. Folding too tight here; raising unnecessary with speculative hand. Plan: call, see flop, fold to c-bet if miss, continue if hit or good draw.

Check. I opened preflop with K♣6♠ and got one caller; on the flop T♣–9♥–8♦ I only have a single overcard (K) and no real draw. This board is very connected and hits a huge portion of Elon’s calling range (T/9/8, pairs, two‑pair/sets, broadway combinations and connected connectors), so a continuation bet will get called too often by better hands and thinly by worse hands I can’t profitably barrel later. I have position, so I prefer pot control and to see a turn for free: if Elon checks back I can evaluate the turn and potentially take a stab on a safe card (e.g. K/A) or check down; if he bets small (≈≤1/3 pot ≈200) I’ll call to see a turn, but I’ll fold to medium/large sizing because my equity is low. Checking preserves chips, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and lets me exploit bluffs or improve on later streets.

Preflop, I defended wide from the BB against SB's standard raise with suited Q6h, good pot odds and equity. Flop 9h8dTc is connected and somewhat drawy (T98 possible straights/draws), but I completely whiff: queen-high with only backdoor heart draw/flush potential and minimal straight outs. Minni (SB, preflop raiser) checks OOP, which could be weakness, trap with strong hand (sets, two-pair, str8), or air/indecision from wide SB range. I'm now IP. Betting risks getting check-raised or called by better; thin value unlikely as Q-high rarely best. Best play: check back for pot control, realize equity cheaply on turn if improves (heart for FD, runner-runner str8 unlikely), or showdown if she checks turn. Folding equity not needed; avoid bloating pot OOP effectively next street. Consistent with preflop plan: continue cautiously on miss, no c-bet from her.

I improved to top pair on the turn (K♣6♠ on 9♥‑8♦‑T♣‑K♠). Elon checked flop and checked turn, which shows weakness or a hand he’s pot‑controlling (many T/9/8 combos, underpairs, or broadway cards). Betting ~1/2 pot gives me thin value against tens, nines, eights and small pairs, while pricing out/charging many of the straight/overcard draws that can still outdraw me. Size: 300 into a 600 pot is large enough to win the pot now often but small relative to effective stacks so I keep future options. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I reevaluate the river and look to get value on safe rivers or pot‑control on dangerous ones; if he check‑raises big I will give credit to two‑pair/straights and fold rather than get badly committed with a weak kicker.

Facing a small 1/3 pot turn bet from Minni (SB preflop raiser) after mutual flop checks on a whiffed board for me (Q-high with Qh6h, only backdoor heart draw initially). Board 9h8dTcKs is coordinated with straight possibilities (JQ, QJ for broadway), and Ks could improve Kx or draws in her wide SB range. Her flop check OOP suggests possible weakness or trap, and small turn bet smells thin value (mid pairs, Kx), bluffs (missed draws like J7, 75), or probes rather than strong nuts. Pot odds excellent at 300 to call into 900 (~3.3:1, need ~23% equity), and my Q-high has decent showdown value vs her polarized range post-check, plus live Q blocker and any heart/improver outs (3-4% to hit runner-runner flush/straight unlikely but cheap). Folding too tight OOP effectively next; raising thin without fold equity or value. Plan: Call cheaply, pot control to ~1200, evaluate river showdown/value bet, consistent with cautious postflop play after wide defend/call.

The river 8♣ pairs the board (9♥-8♦-T♣-K♠-8♣). I have top pair K♣6♠ with a very weak kicker — a hand that was reasonable on the turn but is now vulnerable to any 8 (which makes trips) and to stronger Kx/two‑pair combinations. Elon’s line (called my 3bb open preflop, checked the flop, then check‑called a 300 turn bet) is a fairly sticky/calling line that contains lots of T/9/medium pairs, some Kx, and some 8x. Betting for thin value here risks getting raised or jammed by trips/two‑pair (and his past hands show he will raise for value), while the amount I’d realistically get called by (T/9/underpairs) is small relative to the risk. Pot control is the priority — I’d rather go to showdown and avoid folding out worse hands or getting stacked by a river raise. Plan: check to induce a bet or get to showdown cheaply. If Elon fires a small bet (roughly ≤600) I will call for value versus T/9/underpairs; if he fires a large river shove/overbet (>>600) I will fold to avoid committing to a heavily beaten hand.

River board 9h8dTcKs8c pairs the 8s, completing possible straights (e.g., QJ broadway, 67 for lower straight) and flush draws partially. Holding Qh6h (queen-high, missed heart draw, no pair/straight), showdown value is low against Minni's SB preflop raising range, which includes pairs (88+, 99, TT, KK trips possible), Kx (hit turn), two-pair combos, and straights. Her line: check flop OOP (weak/trap), small turn bet after Ks (value Kx/midpair or thin/bluff probe), check river (pot control with medium strength or giving up bluff). Preflop wide defend with suited connector played cautiously: called pre small, checked flop miss, called cheap turn pot odds (~33% equity implied). Now IP after her river check, betting thin value unlikely (Q high rarely best vs calling range), bluffing poor (her double-check range calls light or has showdown). Check-back maximizes: pot control on whiff, realizes any equity/showdown if she has total air (missed Jx/7x draws), avoids spew OOP effectively prior streets. Consistent plan, preserves stack (14100 deep) in tournament with 96 hands left.

I’m on the button with 7♥6♣, stacks are deep (~166bb). Facing an open to ~2x (to call 100) into a 150 pot. 7‑6o is a speculative, position‑dependent hand — too weak to 3‑bet for value but worth seeing a flop given the price and positional advantage. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives roughly 2.5:1 pot odds (~28.6% break‑even equity), plus strong implied odds to hit straights/two‑pair in position. Plan: call to see the flop; c‑bet small on coordinated/drawy flops or continue aggressively when I connect, otherwise check/control and fold to heavy sustained aggression if I miss.

Preflop in SB with 5c7c suited connectors against a button limp from Minni. Excellent speculative hand for multi-way pots due to potential straights, flushes, and implied odds. Completing for 50 more into a 250 pot offers great odds (~6:1 before BB acts), and the game history shows passive play with frequent checks and multi-way pots. BB Flash may check behind or raise light, but overall EV positive to see flop cheaply. Raising risks isolating with marginal hand and folding out equity.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey suited. Both Minni (Button) and Elon (Small Blind) have limped in, and I have the option to check to see a free flop. While K3s has some value, it is not strong enough to warrant a raise against two opponents who could be limping with a wide variety of hands or potentially trapping. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free and see how the board develops.

On the flop of Ts5h3c, I have top pair fives with a seven kicker (pairing my 5c with the 5h), plus a backdoor club flush draw (holding 7c and board 3c) and some overcard value with the 7. The board is somewhat coordinated with possible straight draws (e.g., 6-7, 2-4, 4-6), but no immediate strong draws completed. Pot is 300, I'm out of position in the SB facing the Button (Minni, who limped) and BB (Flash, who checked behind). History shows frequent checking and passive play in multi-way pots, so checking induces bluffs or free cards, controls the pot with a marginal top pair (weak kicker vulnerable to 5x, Tx, Ax), and realizes equity cheaply against likely weak ranges. Betting risks folding out better equity or getting raised off the hand multi-way OOP. Check to see turn and reassess.

I have bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker on a relatively dry board of Ts-5h-3c. In a three-way limped pot, it is generally best to keep the pot small with marginal showdown value. Since the Small Blind checked, I will check as well to see how the Button reacts. Betting here doesn't achieve much value and potentially invites a raise that I would be forced to fold to, whereas checking allows me to potentially see a cheap turn.

I’m on the button with 7♥6♣ on a dry, low flop Ts‑5h‑3c. I have no pair and only a gutshot to a 4 (4 outs, ~16.5% to hit by river), so my showdown equity is weak. Both opponents checked to me, and many preflop calling ranges have missed this board. With position and deep stacks I want to seize the initiative with a small c‑bet to fold out overcards/marginal hands and to build a pot only when I improve. I’ll bet small (~1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable — if I face substantial aggression I’ll fold; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue aggressively if I improve or get a favorable runout.

Facing a small 100 bet into a 400 pot from Minni on the button after both blinds checked. I have top pair (fives) with a mediocre 7 kicker, plus backdoor club flush draw and some straight potential (gutshot to 6 for wheel or broader draws). Pot odds are excellent at 4:1, and calling keeps the pot small multi-way with Flash still to act behind. History shows passive tendencies with frequent small bets and calls in multi-way pots. Raising risks isolating OOP with a vulnerable hand or folding out Flash's equity; folding is too tight given price and hand strength. Call to realize equity, control pot size, and reassess on turn.

I have bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker on a dry board of Ts-5h-3c. In this three-way limped pot, Minni (Button) has made a small bet of 100 into 300, and Elon (Small Blind) has called. I am getting excellent pot odds (6:1) to see the turn. While bottom pair is a marginal hand, it has significant equity in a limped pot where ranges are wide. Calling is the most appropriate action to maintain a small pot while realizing my equity, especially since I am closing the action on this street.

On the turn Js, the board is Ts 5h 3c Js, giving me a vulnerable pair of fives (my 5c pairing the 5h) with a weak 7c kicker. The Js adds a spade flush draw (two spades on board), possible straight draws (e.g., 7-8-9-T-J or Q-9), and pairs Js or Ts beat me. I'm first to act OOP in a 3-way pot of 600 against passive opponents who have shown frequent checking and small betting in multi-way pots. Betting risks folding out equity or facing raises from better hands/draws; checking controls the pot, realizes equity cheaply, induces bluffs from the button (Minni), and allows Flash (BB, shorter stack) to act. History supports checking through often, preserving stack for better spots while hand has showdown value.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades adds another overcard to the board. I currently hold bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker. In a three-way pot that was limped pre-flop, my hand has some showdown value but is very vulnerable. Elon has checked from the Small Blind, and Minni is still to act behind me. There is no reason to bet here, as I'm unlikely to be called by many worse hands and could easily be facing a better pair or a draw. Checking is the most prudent move to try and reach a cheap showdown or see how the action develops if Minni bets again.

Check. I c‑bet the flop small to fold out overcards and realize some equity; both called. The turn J changes the texture — it helps common calling ranges (Jx, Tx, two‑pair combos) and substantially reduces my fold equity. I only have a 4‑out gutshot (4 outs ≈ 8.7% to hit the river), which is too thin to profitably double‑barrel multiway with deep stacks. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize my small equity from position, and avoids committing a large chunk of my 16400 stack when many hands now beat me. Plan: if either opponent leads out sizable I’ll fold; if checked to river I can attempt a targeted bluff or value line only if I improve.

On the river Ac, the board is Ts5h3cJsAc. Holding 5c7c, I now have the ace-high flush (Ac-7c-5c-3c clubs), a very strong hand likely the nuts or close to it against passive opponents' ranges. Possible worse hands include pairs (Tx, Jx, Ax trips unlikely), two-pair combos, missed draws, and weaker flushes (lower clubs). History shows frequent check-calls and passive play in multi-way pots, so they may call with medium strength. Pot is 600, I'm first to act OOP. Checking risks giving free showdown or missing value; betting extracts from worse hands without scaring off passive callers. Sizing 500 (about 5/6 pot) balances value and fold equity while leaving room for calls from Flash (9100 stack) and Minni (16400). Larger risks folds, smaller underrepresents strength.

The board has run out extremely unfavorably for my pair of threes, with four overcards now present (A, J, T, 5). Elon has led out for 500 into a 600 pot on the river after an Ace appeared. In a three-way pot, this bet usually represents either a slow-played monster or, more likely, an Ace that was checking/calling and finally hit. Even if Elon is bluffing, I still have Minni behind me who could easily hold a Ten, a Jack, or a Five. My pair of threes has almost no showdown value in this spot against a near-pot sized lead. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

Fold. I missed my 4‑outer and have basically no showdown value on T♠‑5♥‑3♣‑J♠‑A♣. The pot is 1,100 and Elon bet 500; calling 500 to win a 1,600 final pot requires ~31.25% equity (500/1600). Given his line (called my small flop c‑bet, checked the turn, now leads the river) he is credibly representing value (A‑x, J‑x, T‑x, two‑pair) and I have essentially zero equity against that range. I also have no meaningful blockers to Ace or Jack, and a raise/bluff here is unattractive — I would be investing a large amount with no fold equity or showdown cards. With deep stacks it’s better to conserve chips in this spot and fold.

3-handed on the button with deep stacks (~145BB effective vs BB). Unopened pot (SB limped/completed, BB checked), pot 150, call 100. 9c6d is a playable hand in position for a raise due to position, fold equity, and ability to realize equity postflop if called. Standard button opening range includes this offsuit gapped connector-ish hand in short-handed play. Opponents from history (esp. Minni in BB) defend wide and check-call frequently, but raising takes initiative, builds pot when ahead, and steals blinds/limps often. Limp/call too passive; fold too tight. Raising ~3x BB (300 total) is balanced size for this spot: builds pot without overcommitting, good SPR if called. Maximizes EV over tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit (JTo), which is a strong enough hand to defend against a Button open in a 3-handed game. Elon has been quite active from the Button, and JTo has good playability post-flop. While 3-betting is an option to take the initiative, calling allows me to see a flop for a relatively small investment while keeping my range wide. I will call the 250 additional chips to see the flop, noting that I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand.

I’m in the big blind with 9♦7♥ facing a button open to 300 and the small blind calling. I already have 100 in and need to add 200 (total 300) — the immediate pot odds (~22%) plus deep stacks make defending reasonable. 9‑7o is marginal and has poor playability as a 3‑bet hand, especially multiway, so I’ll avoid 3‑betting or folding my blind for free. I’ll call to see a flop and then proceed cautiously: folding to significant aggression if I miss; playing aggressively only if I pair up, make two pair/straights, or get strong draws. Calling preserves stack flexibility and lets me realize equity against wide opening ranges.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker on a board of Queen-Jack-Five. In a three-way pot and acting first out of position, checking is the most standard play. This allows me to control the size of the pot and see how both the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) and the Big Blind (Minni) interact with this board. My hand is strong enough to likely call a single bet, but it is not strong enough to lead out into two opponents.

I defended the blind preflop with 9♦7♥ for pot odds; on this flop (Js Qc 5d) I completely missed. There’s no pair, no real draw (only remote runner‑runner straights) and the board is two overcards — plus it’s multiway with the SB still in, so bluffing or barreling is unlikely to succeed. With deep stacks I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand, so I’ll check and fold to any meaningful bet, only continuing if I improve or face an extremely small probe.

Multiway pot (3 players) on a Js5dQc rainbow flop that completely misses my 9c6d (no pair, no draws, pure bluff catcher at best). As pre-flop raiser checked to in position, but with zero equity and deep stacks (~140BB effective), c-betting air is low EV due to wide calling ranges from passive check/call tendencies observed in opponents' histories (e.g., frequent check/calls with marginal hands).
