Checking preserves stack, controls pot size, allows free turn card to potentially improve (e.g., 8/T for gutshot/OESD), induces bluffs from worse nothing, and maintains balance by not always c-betting. Bluffing multiway with no blockers/fold equity against station-y players is suboptimal; live to fight another hand in tournament with 94 hands left.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker. The flop checked around between all three players, which suggests that the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) likely doesn't have a strong Queen or better, and the Big Blind (Minni) is also likely weak. The Seven of Diamonds on the turn is mostly a blank. While I could bet for value against draws or smaller pairs, checking is the most prudent play out of position in a three-way pot. It allows me to control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff from Elon, who has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands. If someone bets, I have a relatively easy call given the strength of my hand relative to the checking sequence on the flop.

I improved to a weak bottom pair (7s with a 9 kicker) on a Js Qc 5d 7d board in a three‑way pot. Both opponents have been passive (checked the flop/turn), and most hands that will call a bet (Jx, Qx, 5x) are already ahead of me — betting here has very thin value and a multiway bluff is unlikely to work. I want pot control and to realize equity cheaply, so I’ll check and evaluate the river. Plan: fold to a large bet/raise on the turn, call small probe bets to see a river, and only invest further if I improve or get a clear, cheap showdown.

Multiway pot on turn (Js5dQc7d board) with complete air (9c6d: no pair, no draws, pure 9-high bluff catcher). Preflop raiser who checked flop, now facing two checks; opponents show passive check/call tendencies from history (frequent stations with marginal holdings/draws). Bluffing here low EV: multiway dynamics reduce fold equity, no blockers to villains' likely calling ranges (pairs like J/Q/5/7, diamond draws, 89/T8 str8 draws), deep stacks (~140BB me, ~88BB Flash) punish floats/mistakes. Checking optimal: controls pot size (900), takes free river card (3-4% equity to pair/improve), balances checking range (includes missed draws/value), induces bluffs from worse air on river, preserves stack for better spots with 94 hands left in tournament. Live to exploit later.

The board has paired on the river with a second Queen. I currently hold two pair, Queens and Jacks. Given the action so far (checked through the flop and turn), it is highly unlikely that any player holds a Queen or an overpair like AA/KK, as they almost certainly would have bet for value or protection on earlier streets. My Jack is likely the best hand, but betting into two opponents might only get called by better Jacks (AJ or KJ) or a slow-played Queen. By checking, I can potentially induce a bluff from Elon, who has shown aggressive tendencies, or get a cheap showdown to win the pot. I will check and plan to call most reasonable bet sizes.

I defended the blind with 9♦7♥ and checked through the streets. The river  Q♦ pairs the board (Js 5♦ Q♣ 7♦ Q♦), which upgrades my hand to two pair (Queens and Sevens, Q Q 7 7 9). That’s a meaningful improvement — strong versus lots of missed broadway/rag hands that checked earlier, but still vulnerable to any Queen (trips), any Jack (QJ two‑pair), a made diamond flush (would require both hole cards diamonds), or a full house/pocket 7s. Both opponents were passive, so their ranges include many one‑pair or weaker holdings that will call a small value bet. I’ll make a thin value/blocking bet to extract from worse hands and deny a cheap showdown: bet ~1/3 pot (300). If I get called I’ll go to showdown; if I face a sizeable raise I’ll fold (assuming trips/full house/flush).

River facing Minni's small 300 bet into 900 pot (~1/3 pot) on a Js5dQc7dQd board, multiway with Flash checking behind. My 9c6d is pure air (9-high, no pair/draw), loses to any pair (Q,J,5,7), two pair, better, or even completed diamond flushes/sets. Minni checked flop/turn passively then small bets river, which per history (passive check-calls, occasional thin value bets like hand 55) likely thin value with Qx (e.g., AT/KT for Q-pair), Jx, or hit draws rather than bluff (small size induces calls from worse). Pot odds ~4:1 but EV negative as calling range loses to vast majority of her betting range; Flash checked first (weak, unlikely to raise/call behind profitably). Raising air has no fold equity vs station-y Minni deep. Folding optimal: preserves deep 14200 stack (140BB), avoids spew with zero equity/showdown value, lives for 94+ hands in tournament. Consistent with prior checking strategy to control pot/defer decisions.

I have two pair, Queens and Jacks, with a Ten kicker. The action on the flop and turn was checked by all three players, which strongly suggests that no one has a Queen or a very strong hand like an overpair. On the river, the second Queen appears, and Minni leads for a small bet of 300 into a 900 pot. This 1/3 pot bet could be a thin value bet with a weaker Jack (like J9 or J8), a small pocket pair, or a total bluff. Given that I have middle pair and the pot odds are 5-to-1, calling is a mandatory play. I cannot raise because I'm unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I cannot fold given the passive nature of the hand so far and the strength of my two pair relative to the board's texture.

In a 3-handed game, Ten-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand to open from the button. While a wide opening range is generally preferred on the button, T7o sits at the bottom of that range. Given the aggressive nature of Minni and Elon in recent hands, and the fact that I am slightly out-stacked by both opponents, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening opportunity.

Ten-deuce offsuit is one of the worst playable hands — poor equity and very limited postflop playability. I’m in the small blind so I’ll be out of position for every postflop street, and Elon has shown a tendency to defend and play big pots rather than lay down to small steals. Raising here is a thin bluff that will often be called or 3‑bett, creating difficult, low-expected-value situations. Limping (calling the extra 50) simply commits me OOP with a dominated hand for a trivial price. With deep stacks and many hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a much better spot is the correct long-term play.

I’m on the button with J♣8♦ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Facing 100 to call into a 150 pot (min-raise to 200), a light 3-bet here is too risky because this hand is often dominated and vulnerable to a 4-bet. Calling keeps the pot controllable, leverages my positional advantage, and gives good odds to see a flop. Postflop I’ll proceed aggressively on favourable boards (top pair, good draws) and pot-control or fold to heavy action when I miss. If I face a large preflop 3-bet I’ll usually fold.

Td4s is a very weak offsuit hand with minimal playability or equity multiway. Facing a limp from Minni on the button, who often raises but here limped, and with Flash in BB likely to check or check-raise, completing for 50 into a 250 pot offers poor implied odds due to the hand's trash nature—no straight potential, flush draws unlikely, dominated by better tens or connectors. Deep stacks but folding preserves chips for premium spots in a tournament setting where survival and position matter more than marginal calls. Standard discipline: fold trash from SB after limp.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of fours. The Button (Minni) has limped into the pot, and the Small Blind (Elon) has folded. Since I am already in for the full big blind amount and there is no further bet to call, I have the option to check and see a flop for free or raise to build a pot. Given my stack size relative to the others and the fact that pocket fours are vulnerable post-flop, checking is a sound strategy to try and hit a set cheaply without bloating the pot out of position.

I have flopped a set of fours on a board with two diamonds. While the pot is small because the button limped and the small blind folded, I have a very strong hand. I want to build the pot and charge any draws or top-pair hands. Leading out here is a good way to start building the pot immediately, as checking risks a free card for a flush draw or a gutshot if the button checks behind.

I have J♣8♦ on the button and missed this dry flop (9♦-4♦-5♥). I do have one diamond (8♦) for a very weak backdoor flush and a couple of backdoor straight possibilities, but no pair. Flash checked preflop and now bets 150 into what was ~250 (pot 400), so calling 150 requires roughly 27% equity. I estimate ~24–25% to make a pair by the river from my 6 direct outs plus ~4% or so combined for backdoor back-ups — roughly borderline (~28–30%) equity. In position with a marginal but improvable hand, the prudent play is to call and keep the pot controlled: I can fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss, or continue/raise if I pick up a pair or a clear draw. A raise here would be a thin bluff that risks too much face of many made hands and possible 3-bet pressure. Therefore I will call to realize equity and use position to navigate later streets.

I have a set of fours on a relatively coordinated board featuring a flush draw and potential straight draws. After leading on the flop and being called by the button, the deuce of spades on the turn is a safe card that doesn't complete the primary flush draw. I need to continue betting to build the pot and extract value from hands like top pair (9x) or various draws (flush draws, 67, 36). A bet of 400 (approximately 73% of the pot) serves to charge those draws while getting value from a wider range of the button's limping/calling range.

I missed the turn with J♣8♦ and the backdoor diamond draw is now dead after the 2♠. I only have pair-outs (3 Jacks + 3 Eights = 6 outs) — ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity to improve on the river. Flash has bet 400 into a 950 pot; calling 400 to win 950 requires ~29.6% equity (400/(950+400) ≈ 29.6%). My equity is well below the required break-even, so a call is a clear -EV. A raise/bluff (min-raise would be to ~800) is too thin here — Flash led the hand and the sizing looks consistent with value, and risking a big portion of a deep stack with essentially zero fold equity against many value hands is unjustified. With a healthy stack and position preserved for better spots, folding and waiting for a better situation is the correct play.

Preflop on the button in an unraised pot (pot 150, to call 100 from BB), with 34o (offsuit connectors). Deep stacks (~140BB effective). Position is excellent for playing speculative hands postflop if called, or stealing the blinds (~1.5BB dead money). 34o has decent implied odds multiway or heads-up IP against loose defenders evident from hand history (many checkdowns and wide calling ranges). Opponents passive postflop. Raising a wide range from BTN maximizes EV by denying equity and building pot OOP for them. Size to ~3x BB (300 total) for value/steal balance. Folding too tight; limping suboptimal OOP for callers.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Spades and Deuce of Diamonds, which is a very weak hand. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. This hand has poor playability, and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand if I called. It is a standard fold.

Elon opened to 300 from the button; I’m in the big blind with J♥6♣. This is a weak offsuit jack with a poor kicker and very limited post‑flop playability out of position. Calling 200 gives me ~30.8% pot odds, but vs a BTN opening range I’m often dominated and will face difficult multi‑street decisions against an aggressive player who has shown the willingness to 3‑bet and barrel. A light 3‑bet is possible as a bluff, but J6o is a poor candidate — bad equity when called and awkward post‑flop. With deep stacks and many hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the prudent long‑term play. I will fold this marginal defense.

On the button in a 3-handed game, QTo is a strong hand that should be played aggressively. Raising to 300 allows me to take the lead and utilize my positional advantage over the Small and Big Blinds, who have both shown willingness to contest pots in previous hands.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the Button, I’m in the small blind with K♦6♣ — a marginal, easily-dominated holding. The pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call (I’d be committing 250 to win 700 right away — I need ~35.7% equity), and I’ll be out of position versus the opener (and possibly the big blind if he continues). 3-betting to the minimum (500) with K6o is a poor play — not enough value and not a strong enough blocker bluff. With a deep stack and many hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better, positionally-favorable spot is correct. Fold.

9s6s is a suited connector with good playability and implied odds. Facing a button raise from Flash, who opens wide from late position based on history. Minni folded SB, so heads-up potential vs loose range. Pot odds excellent: 200 to call into 450 pot (~29% odds, hand has ~35-40% equity vs wide button range). Prefer calling to realize equity multiway potential or OOP profitably, rather than folding too tight or 3-betting marginal hand. Deep stacks allow set-mining/straight/flush potential postflop.

On this flop of 8s-2s-Jd, holding 9s6s, I have a strong nut flush draw (9-high spade) plus a gutshot straight draw to the ten (J-T-9-8). Equity is excellent against Flash's wide button opening range (~35-45% depending on exact range). As OOP first to act in a 650 pot with deep effective stacks (min ~9950), checking is optimal to induce a continuation bet from Flash, who is likely to c-bet frequently with air, draws, or marginal made hands. This allows check-call or check-raise semi-bluff opportunities, realizing equity while controlling pot size. Betting OOP risks getting check-raised off equity or facing floats; checking disguises strength and balances our checking range including monsters and weak hands. Long-term stack maximization favors playing draws aggressively post-response rather than donk-betting.

On this flop, I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). The presence of the Queen of Spades also provides a backdoor flush draw and a blocker to some of my opponent's potential spade draws. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Elon checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play to maintain initiative and potentially take down the pot immediately. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot should put pressure on Elon's weaker ranges while allowing me to realize equity if I am called.

Holding 9s6s on 8s2sJd flop, with Flash's 400 bet into 1050 pot. This is the nut spade flush draw (9-high) plus gutshot straight draw to the T (J98 needs T), giving ~40-45% equity against Flash's wide button c-bet range (many Ax suited spades worse, broadway floats, Jx, weak pairs). Pot odds for call are favorable (400 into 1050 ~28% needed), but OOP calling risks turn discomfort: blank turn check-fold to bet or weak check-raise. Check-raising semi-bluff seizes initiative, denies equity to ~20-30% worse hands (lower FDs, backdoors, floats), extracts value from Jx/A-high/top-pair weak, and balances with our checking monsters (JJ, J8, 82s, sets). Sizing to 1200 (3x bet, ~pot-sized post-call) commits ~9% of stack, pressures Flash's 9550 stack (call leaves him ~8350), likely folds 50%+ of his range while allowing profitable calls/shoves from worse. Deep stacks favor aggressive draw play for fold equity + showdown value. Long-term EV+ per history of Flash's aggression but fold to pressure (e.g., Hand 49, 52).

Flash's hand, Qs Td, has only a gutshot straight draw and two overcards on a J-8-2 board with two spades. Elon's check-raise indicates significant strength, likely a Jack with a good kicker, a set, or a strong flush draw. While Flash holds the Qs as a blocker to some spade draws, the overall equity against a check-raising range is too low to justify calling the 800 chips. Folding preserves the stack for better opportunities in the tournament.

I have A♥K♥ on the button — the strongest non-pair hand. Action folded to me with pot = 150 (50/100 blinds). This is an ideal spot to take the initiative and build the pot from position. Min-raise is 200 but I’ll open to 300 total (3x the BB). That sizing gives good fold equity, pressures the blinds, and keeps worse hands and callers in so I can play postflop in position with a dominating hand. Stacks are deep (~150BB), so I can comfortably call or 4-bet a 3-bet if needed, but for now a 300 open is the optimal standard play.

Jh4c is a trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive in the past, from out of position in the SB. Pot odds are marginal (250 to call into 450, ~2:1), but postflop playability is terrible OOP with this hand. No reason to call, raise, or defend. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 4s. Minni raised to 300 from the button, which is a standard opening move in a 3-handed game. While my hand is weak, it is suited, and I am getting 2.25:1 odds to call (200 more to win a pot that will be 650). In 3-handed play, defending the big blind with suited hands against a wide button range is generally profitable or at least neutral compared to folding, despite being out of position.

In this three-handed game, I defended my Big Blind with a suited 9-4 against a button raise from Minni. The flop comes Queen of Hearts, Five of Spades, Jack of Spades. This board contains two high cards and two spades. I have a backdoor flush draw (needing two more spades to make a flush). Typically, as the Big Blind in a single-raised pot, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading into a board that likely connects well with the button's range (which includes many broadway cards) would be an inefficient use of chips. I will check and see how Minni acts.

I opened to 300 preflop and the BB cold‑called. Flop Q♥ 5♠ J♠ with A♥K♥ in hand gives me two overcards plus strong blockers (both top hearts, including the A♥). This is a textbook continuation‑bet spot: my raising range fares well on Q‑J‑5 and I have respectable equity to improve (A/K pairs, T for Broadway) while also denying equity to many of Flash’s calling hands. A bet of ~60% pot (400 into 650) applies pressure, folds out weaker pairs and many pure bluffs, charges draws, and preserves initiative for turn/river play. If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and barrel favorable cards (A/K/heart/T) or slow down against heavy resistance; if I get a check‑raise I’ll give credit to strong made hands and proceed cautiously.

I have a flush draw with the 9s and 4s on a board of Qh 5s Js. Minni has made a continuation bet of 400 into a 650 pot. With four spades already (two in my hand, two on the board), I have a roughly 36% chance of hitting my flush by the river. The pot odds are 400 to win 1050, requiring about 27.5% equity to continue profitably. Since my equity exceeds the required odds and this is a 3-handed game where ranges are wider, calling to see the turn is the most standard and theoretically sound play.

I have successfully completed my flush on the turn with the King of Spades. While a 9-high flush is not the nuts, it is a very strong hand in three-handed play. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) and allow her to continue betting her range, which now includes many straights, sets, two-pair, or semi-bluffs with a high spade. Leading out might scare off her bluffs or weaker made hands. If she bets, I will likely call or consider a check-raise depending on the size, keeping in mind that the Ace or Ten of spades would currently beat me.

Turn K♠ pairs my K and improves AhKh to top pair with the best kicker. Pot is 1,450 and Flash (BB) checked the turn after calling my 400 flop c‑bet — his range contains Qx, Jx, medium pairs, some Kx, and the dangerous Broadway straights (A‑T and T‑9) that the K completes. I want to extract value from Qs/Jx/pocket pairs, deny a free river to straight/spade draws, and use my A♥/K♥ blockers to reduce some strong combos. Betting around 2/3 pot keeps worse hands in and charges draws; it also maintains initiative and allows us to re‑evaluate a call versus a check‑raise. I will bet 1,000 chips into the 1,450 pot to accomplish that; if I get a large check‑raise I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing (calling reasonable raises for pot odds, folding to an overcommitment that polarizes to made straights/sets), and if called I’ll plan to extract or pot‑control on the river based on the card and opponent action.

I have completed a 9-high flush on the turn. While this is a very strong hand in 3-handed play, there are several higher flushes possible (As, Ts, and 8s are still out there). Minni has been an aggressive player, and her 1000 bet into 1450 suggests she either has a strong made hand (like a straight, two pair, or a flush) or a high spade draw. If I raise now, I likely scare off her bluffs and weaker made hands, while getting called or re-raised by better flushes. By calling, I keep her range wide, allow her to continue bluffing on the river, and minimize losses if she already has me beat with a higher spade. I will call and re-evaluate on the river.

The river Queen of Diamonds pairs the board, which is a dangerous card for my 9-high flush. This card brings full houses into play (like KQ, QJ, Q5, or even 55/JJ/KK). Additionally, since I only have a 9-high flush, I am still losing to any higher flush (As or Ts). Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand. By checking, I can see how she reacts to the board pairing. If she checks behind, my hand likely has enough showdown value to win against straights or trips. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the size of the bet and the likelihood of her having a boat or a higher flush versus a bluff or a worse made hand. Leading out here would likely only get called by hands that beat me.

River Q pairs the board: Qh 5s Js Ks Qd. With AhKh I currently have two pair (Ks & Qs) with the best kicker, but the river dramatically improves villain’s range — any Q now makes trips (or better), pocket J/K/5 become full houses, and completed spade flushes also beat me. Flash’s line (cold‑call preflop, check‑call my 400 flop bet and 1,000 turn bet) is consistent with Qx, Jx, medium pocket pairs, or a flush draw that can now be disguised as a check. Because the river helps villain’s range more than mine and a bet here can be punished by a check‑raise or called by many hands that beat me, I prefer pot control and avoid bloating the pot. I’ll check and go to showdown — if Flash fires a small value bet I’ll call; if he polarizes with a large shove I’ll give heavy weight to folding since many of those hands beat my two pair.

3-handed on the button with KTo offsuit, a strong hand with king-high blocker, straight potential, and good playability. Blinds have effectively limped (standard blinds posted), pot 150, calling 100 gives great odds but raising is better to take initiative, build pot with strong hand, or steal blinds. Opponents often fold to preflop raises based on history (e.g., frequent folds to 200-300 raises). Deep stacks (150BB effective), so standard 3x raise to 300. Min-raise is 200 but sizing larger denies equity and shows strength.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a premium starting hand. Elon has raised from the button, which is a wide opening position. In the Small Blind, I prefer to 3-bet rather than call to avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position against the Big Blind and to take initiative in the hand. A standard 3-bet size here is approximately 3x to 3.5x the original raise plus the blinds. Raising to 1000 puts pressure on the button and likely isolates the hand or wins the pot immediately.

I have pocket 3s (3c3h) in the big blind facing a small-blind 3-bet to 1000 after the button opened to 300. To call I must commit a total of 1000 (900 more) into a 1400 pot. With deep stacks this is a standard set-mining / calling spot: I get good implied odds to hit a set and remain playable postflop. A 4-bet as a bluff with a small pocket pair is thin and unnecessary; folding surrenders too much equity given the price. I will call, see the flop, and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy action on a dry board and extract value when I hit sets or favorable textures.

Facing a 3bet from SB (Flash) to 1000 after my BTN open to 300, and a flat call from BB (Minni) for 900 more. Pot is 2300, calling 700 more offers excellent odds (over 3:1). KTo offsuit plays well multiway in position with straight potential, king-high blocker reducing villain's AA/KK, and good postflop playability on deep stacks. Opponent history shows aggression but also frequent folds and calls with medium strength; calling realizes equity heads-up or multiway while keeping pot manageable. Folding too tight preflop in position; 4betting risks bloating pot OOP vs likely strong calling ranges (Minni's flat suggests QQ+/AK/speculative but committed). Prefer seeing flop in position to exploit imbalances.

I am in a three-way 3-bet pot on a low, disconnected flop of 5-2-6. While I have Ace-King, I have missed this flop entirely. Both opponents called a significant pre-flop 3-bet, which suggests they likely hold pocket pairs like 77-JJ or suited broadways. A continuation bet into two opponents on this board is risky, as it's highly likely one of them holds an overpair that won't fold. Checking is the most prudent play to see how the action develops and potentially see a free turn card if everyone else checks behind.

I called the SB 3-bet preflop as a set-mining play. The flop (5♦ 2♦ 6♣) missed my 3s — this is a relatively dry board and both opponents have shown aggressive/wide-strong ranges (Flash 3-bet, Elon opened-and-called). With a small pocket pair versus two players I want pot control: don’t inflate the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check and either call a small bet to see a turn or fold to heavy pressure; if checked around I keep the pot manageable and can take a free turn to try to hit my set.

Multiway pot on a dry flop (5d2d6c) with no hits to my KTo. Both opponents checked, showing potential weakness—Flash's 3bet preflop often misses this low, uncoordinated board, and Minni's flat-call suggests speculative hands like suited connectors or pairs that didn't connect. My king-high has good blocker value against villainous strong hands (blocks AK/KK), and position allows me to c-bet for thin value against worse (Ax, Tx, bluffs), deny equity to draws (diamond flush draw, gutshots like 43/74), and take down the pot frequently. Checking back risks giving free cards to draws or induces unnecessary showdowns; betting ~60% pot (1800 into 3000) balances value/protection/fold equity while keeping stacks deep. History shows opponents call light but fold to aggression postflop when checked to.

I am in a difficult spot in a three-way 3-bet pot. I missed the flop entirely with my Ace-King offsuit. The board is low and disconnected (5-2-6), which likely favors the calling ranges of my opponents (typically medium pocket pairs or suited connectors). After I checked the flop, Elon bet 1800 into a 3000 pot. With Minni still to act behind me and no real equity other than two overcards, calling would be too expensive and risky. It is highly likely one of my opponents holds a pair that they will not fold, and I do not want to bleed chips out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop I called the SB’s 3-bet with 3♣3♥ as a set-mining play given deep stacks. I checked the flop (5♦ 2♦ 6♣) to control the pot — I missed. Elon now bets 1,800 into a 4,800 pot and Flash folded, leaving me heads-up. Raising is a thin bluff with a small pocket pair and would need to commit a lot; folding here surrenders too much equity given the pot size and deep stacks. Although my raw chance to make a set by river (~8–9%) is small and immediate pot odds are weak, the bet is modest and the implied odds (if I hit) are large heads-up. I’ll call 1,800 to see the turn, plan to fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, and extract maximum value if I hit a set or a very favorable turn.

I called preflop and the flop as a set-mining play with 3♣3♥ and have not improved on the turn (Q♠). Elon has shown an aggressive/wide-strong line (open, called a 3-bet, c-bet), so his range contains many hands that currently beat a small pair (queens, overpairs, sets) as well as bluffs. With deep stacks and no improvement, pot control is the priority — I don’t want to inflate a pot I’m likely to fold later. I will check the turn to see how Elon reacts. If he bets a small amount that offers good pot odds I may call to see the river; I will fold to heavy turn aggression that strongly represents a made hand. If he checks back I’ll reevaluate the river with the same plan (extract value only if I hit a set or face weak turn/river action).

Heads-up on turn vs Minni after she checked. Pot 6600, effective stacks deep (~100BB). My KTs is king-high with no pair or draw on 5d2d6cQs board. Preflop 3bet flat-call by Minni suggests medium pairs (77-JJ), suited connectors (34s-78s, diamonds), suited aces/broadways that missed or semi-connected flop. She check-called flop c-bet (got Flash out), indicating medium strength without top pair or better likely, but turn Qs could improve QT/JQ/Qx in range. Continued aggression risks value-owning vs weaker (Ax/Tx bluffs/draws that fold) while getting called by better (pairs + Qx, sets, 2-pair combos like 65/25). Position allows pot control; checking realizes equity with king-high showdown value against bluffs/misses, induces bluffs on scary river cards, and avoids bloating pot OOP postflop strength reps. History shows Minni calls light but checks weak post-flop; free river exploits imbalances without overcommitting marginal hand this deep in tournament.

River: 5♦ 2♦ 6♣ Q♠ 3♠ — I flopped a small pair and rivered trips (3♣3♥ -> 3♠). Opponent (Elon) opened pre, faced a 3-bet and called, c-bet the flop for a standard protection/continuation bet (wide range: top pair, overpairs, sets, diamond draws, bluffs) then checked the turn. That line is consistent with a wide but often-medium-strength range that will call a reasonable river value bet (Qx, single pairs like 6x/5x, missed draws turned into thin calls). Checking to induce bluff has merit but Elon showed passivity on the turn and checking back likely yields less value than a value bet here. I want to extract value from hands that will call (top pair, second pair, missed draws) without pricing them out. A sizing around 2/3 pot is appropriate — big enough to get value from Qx/6x/5x and smaller pairs, but not so big it folds out most of villain’s calling range. I will bet ~2/3 pot (≈4,400). If he raises/shoves I’ll re-evaluate (my set is strong vs most of his range but rare higher sets are possible).

Facing a river lead of 4400 into 6600 from Minni after both checked turn. Pot 11000, calling 4400 (28% equity needed). K♠T♣ is king-high with spade blocker on 5♦2♦6♣Q♠3♠ board completing spades. Preflop: Minni flatted 3bet OOP multiway (speculative suited connectors/pairs 55-JJ suited aces diamonds/suits). Flop call 1800 large (~60% pot) indicates connection (67♦/♦ draws, 45♦, small pairs/sets unlikely). Turn Q♠ bricks most (no Qx in range likely), mutual check suggests medium strength. River 3♠ completes spades (possible Q♠x/3♠x/K♠x/A♠x), wheel (43), pairs+ (55/22/66 trips/set). Her lead polarized: value (spade flushes rare in range, 65/25/34 straights, trips) or bluffs (missed ♦draws/turn draws betting fear cards). King-high has little showdown value vs calling range, blocks some flushes but beat by pairs/improved hands often. History: Minni calls light but bets for value/protection (e.g., hand 12 river shove thin trips). Pot odds marginal, ICM/tourney deep (hand 62/150, stacks ~100BB), folding avoids spew, preserves stack for better spots. Check-fold turn plan executed; hero call too thin OOP rep from preflop.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wider, but J6o is still a weak hand to open with, especially considering the aggressiveness displayed by Minni and Elon in recent hands. Both blinds have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet. My stack is currently the shortest at the table, and I prefer to wait for a stronger hand or a better spot to steal rather than committing chips with a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop.

I have K♣5♠ in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind. Heads-up ranges should be wide and I want the initiative, so a standard 2xBB open (to 200 total) is appropriate — it gives me fold equity and lets me take control of the hand postflop. My stack is very deep so I can comfortably fold to a large 3‑bet, but if Elon flat-calls I'll c-bet favorable flops and manage the pot on coordinated boards. Raising keeps the pot under control while applying pressure to a wide defending range.

K3o is a marginal hand at best from the BB facing a min-raise from the SB. While pot odds are favorable (3:1, needing ~25% equity), K3o has poor postflop playability: weak kicker for top pair, no straight/flush potential, and acts first postflop. Against Minni's aggressive history (frequent preflop raises and continuation betting), her min-raise range, though wide, includes better kings and suited hands. Equity likely around 35% vs. a loose 50% range, but realizing it is tough deep-stacked. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this 3-handed tournament phase.

Fold. 9♦2♥ is one of the worst hands — very little showdown value, no suit or connectivity, and it’s heavily dominated by a standard open-raise range. Facing a 2xBB open (to 200) on the button I’ll defend only with broadways, suited connectors, or pocket pairs; 9‑2o doesn’t play well postflop and has poor reverse implied odds. With a healthy 175bb stack there’s no reason to gamble or bluff 3-bet with no blockers — preserve chips for much better spots.

Pocket 4s is a strong hand heads-up from the SB against the BB after button folds. Standard play is to raise for value and fold equity, as small pairs perform well in this spot 3-handed. Limping OOP is too passive and invites trouble. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is a good size: builds the pot when ahead, has fold equity against junk, and post-flop we have set-mining potential or continuation bet opportunities. History shows aggressive play works here (e.g., Hand 20 with 44 won a big pot). Deep stacks support this.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. The Small Blind (Elon) has raised to 300. While I would have position post-flop in this Small Blind vs. Big Blind battle, Q5o is a very weak hand that doesn't flop well and is frequently dominated by Elon's opening range. Given the current tournament stage and the relatively standard raise size, I will fold and wait for a better hand to commit chips.

3-handed on the button with Q6o, a playable hand for a wide opening range. Excellent position post-flop. Raising to steal the blinds or build pot with position. Standard sizing around 2.25x the BB. Minni in BB may defend wide but Flash SB likely to fold often. Deep stacks allow for flexibility.

I am holding AQo in the small blind, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. Elon raised from the button, a position from which players open with a wide range of hands. To take the lead and potentially win the pot pre-flop or isolate against the button, a 3-bet is the standard play here. I will raise to 850 chips to put pressure on Elon and the big blind.

Call 850 (750 more). KTs is a strong, playable hand 3‑handed and the 3‑bet to 850 is relatively small versus our deep stack, so calling is cheap and keeps the button’s wide opening range in. Immediate pot odds (~39%) justify a call and the hand plays well postflop: I can continue for value if I pair the K or T or pick up strong draws, and control the pot or fold to heavy action on dry boards. Folding is too nitty; 4‑betting as a bluff here risks committing too much with a marginal hand against a likely polarized 3‑bet range. Plan: see the flop, evaluate texture vs both opponents, bet for value when I connect, and fold to significant aggression if I miss.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash in the SB (about 3.8x my open) and a flat call from Minni in the BB, my Q6o is a marginal opening hand that plays poorly multiway. Flash's 3-bet range is strong: pairs, broadway, suited Ax, etc., where Q6o is often dominated. Minni's call OOP suggests decent strength like pairs or suited connectors. Equity is low (~25-30% vs 3-bet, worse multiway), and while position is good, pot odds (625 into 1925, ~3:1) don't justify calling with such a weak hand. Folding preserves my stack (12k, deep) for better spots in this 3-handed dynamic. No value in raising as a bluff given the strength shown.

I was the pre-flop 3-bettor and the board has come 6s-9c-6c. While this paired board doesn't technically favor my 3-betting range (mostly high cards and big pairs), I have a range advantage and should maintain aggression with a continuation bet. AQo has two overcards and can still be the best hand if Minni called with suited connectors that missed. A bet of roughly 40% of the pot (750 chips) allows me to represent overpairs like JJ-AA and forces Minni to fold her complete air, while keeping the pot manageable if she has a mid-pair and decides to continue.

Flop is 6♠ 9♣ 6♣; I hold K♠ T♥ — missed the flop but have two overcards (6 outs: 3 kings + 3 tens). Flash was the preflop 3‑better but his flop stab is very small (750 into a 2675 pot ≈ 28%), which indicates a fairly wide c‑bet range rather than exclusively huge hands. Calling 750 gives me pot odds of roughly 22% (break‑even), and my chance to pair by the river (~23–24%) roughly meets that. Raising as a bluff here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand versus a player who 3‑bet preflop — too high variance. Folding is too tight given the cheap price and my live outs. Plan: call to see the turn; if I pair a K or T I can bet/raise for value, but if I miss and face significant turn aggression I will fold to preserve my deep stack.

The turn 7s doesn't significantly help my range of high cards and overpairs, and it makes the board more connected. Minni has called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the flop, suggesting she likely has a piece of the board, a pocket pair, or a draw. Since I only have Ace-high, I'm checking here to control the size of the pot and see how Minni reacts. If she checks back, I might still have the best hand or can re-evaluate on the river. If she bets, I'll have to consider the sizing and whether my overcards still have enough equity to continue.

Check. I missed the flop and the turn (K♠ T♥ with board 6♠ 9♣ 6♣ 7♠) so I only have outs to improve: 3 kings + 3 tens + the four 8s that complete a 10-9-8-7-6 straight = ~10 outs (10/46 ≈ 21.7%) to get to a winning hand on the river. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, tiny c‑bet on the flop, then checking the turn) looks more like a wide c‑bet/bluff or pot‑control with medium strength rather than a hand I can reliably fold out. Betting here would bloat a pot I’m usually behind in (he can call with lots of hands that currently beat me: 9x, 6x, sets) and I don’t have enough fold equity to make a thin bluff profitable. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and a free river to realize equity. Plan: check and see the river; if I pair or make the straight I can extract value, and if he bets the river I can fold to large bets or call small ones depending on price.

The board has become very coordinated with the 5s on the river, completing potential straights (any 8) and backdoor flushes. My Ace-Queen high has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value, and I am unlikely to fold out a better hand with a bluff given Minni's call on the flop. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might have a medium-strength hand or a draw. By checking, I am hoping to get to a cheap showdown. If Minni bets, I will likely fold as the board favors her calling range from the big blind and she is unlikely to bluff into this texture without at least a pair.

I hold K♠T♥ on a 6♠ 9♣ 6♣ 7♠ 5♠ river — I have no pair, no straight and no flush (only K-high). Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, a small flop stab, then check on turn) is consistent with a wide range that still contains many hands that beat me (any 6, any 9, 7x, 8x straights, two‑spade combos that make a flush, plus medium pairs). I have almost no showdown value and low fold equity here — a river bet would be a very thin bluff and can be called by lots of hands that currently beat me. With a 3425 pot and deep stacks I prefer pot control and to preserve chips rather than bloat the pot with a marginal bluff. Plan: check and fold to meaningful river aggression (only consider calling a very small bet if the price is absurd).

In a three-handed game, Ace-Jack offsuit (AJo) is a very strong starting hand, especially from the button. I want to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to put pressure on the blinds while remaining balanced with my overall button opening range.

Facing a button open to 300 from the button with Q♥7♥ in the small blind and 159bb effective, this is a marginal but playable suited queen. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but I prefer to keep the pot controllable OOP and realize equity — Q7s plays well multiway and has flush/backdoor potential. Calling 250 more (300 total committed, since I already posted 50) keeps weaker hands in, avoids bloating the pot out of position, and lets me fold to a large 3-bet from the big blind. If we see a favorable flop I'll continue; if the big blind 3-bets heavily preflop I'll fold and preserve a deep stack for better spots.

AKo is a premium hand, top of my range in the big blind facing a button raise and small blind call. The button open from Flash is likely wide, and Minni's call could be speculative or medium strength. Calling gives poor post-flop position with two opponents and doesn't apply enough pressure. Folding is never correct. Raising for value thins the field, isolates against weaker hands, and builds the pot with the best hand. Sizing to 1000 (about 3.3x the raise over the call) is standard, denying equity to worse hands and setting up post-flop aggression. Effective stacks are deep, but this play maximizes EV.

In a three-handed game, Ace-Jack offsuit is a very strong hand. After my initial raise from the button, Elon 3-bet from the big blind. While AJo can be vulnerable to a 4-bet shove from premium hands like AK or pocket pairs (JJ+), it is too strong to fold to a single 3-bet in this format. Calling allows me to maintain position for the rest of the hand and evaluate the flop. Minni's presence in the small blind adds complexity, but calling the additional 700 is the most balanced play to see a flop without over-committing my stack pre-flop.

I already have 300 committed, but Elon’s reraise to 1000 with Flash flat-calling turns this into a multiway 3-bet pot out of position. Q♥7♥ is marginal and frequently dominated in 3-bet spots (and doesn’t have enough playability OOP multiway to justify calling). Calling 700 more for ~23% pot odds still leaves me behind most of the 3-bettor’s range and likely to face c-bets and pressure. A light 4-bet bluff is unattractive multiway. Preserve my deep stack (15650) and wait for a better, positionally favorable spot — fold.

Heads-up out of position on a dry flop 2s4cTd with AKo after 3-betting pre-flop. This board misses much of Flash's wide button calling range, which includes Ax, broadway, suited connectors, pocket pairs. AKo is the best ace-high, has backdoor equity, and blocks strong hands like AA/AK. Continuation betting thins the field, denies equity to floaters/draws (e.g., spade flush draw, gutshots like J9), and builds the pot when ahead. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards or draws and invites bluffs, but betting maintains aggression as the pre-flop raiser. Sizing to 1500 (about 2/3 pot) balances value from worse Ax/Kx/pairs while keeping fold equity against air.

On a very dry board of 10-4-2 rainbow, Elon's lead of 1,500 into a 2,300 pot (approximately 65% pot) is a standard continuation bet after his pre-flop 3-bet. My Ace-Jack offsuit has two overcards to the board and holds significant equity against his likely 3-betting range, which includes hands like Ace-King, Ace-Queen, and King-Queen that I currently beat. Given the pot odds (requiring roughly 28% equity to call) and the fact that I am in position, calling one street to see how the turn develops is the most sound approach. I am looking for an Ace or a Jack to improve my hand, while also being prepared to fold if Elon continues with heavy aggression on a blank turn.

Heads-up on turn with AKo (AhKd) on a 2s4cTdTh board after 3-betting preflop and c-betting flop into Flash's wide button calling range. Pot 5300, stacks deep at ~9500 effective. My hand is ace-high with no pair, straight draw, or flush draw, but blocks AA/AK and has backdoor potential. The flop (2s4cTd) was dry and missed much of villain's range, justifying the c-bet for value/denial. However, the turn pairs the Td (10h), improving hands like Tx (A10, K10, Q10, J10, T9), sets (TT/22/44 unlikely in call range), and two-pair combos. Villain's flop call likely includes Tx, overpairs (JJ+/99?), strong Ax, or floats/draws. Betting now risks getting raised by improved hands or called by better ace-high/medium strength, with poor implied odds OOP and no strong equity. Checking controls the pot, induces bluffs from missed draws (e.g., spade flush draw, gutshots like J9/89), and allows hero-call or fold to a bet based on sizing (small bet might represent weakness). Folding to aggression preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 84 hands left. No fold equity from betting thin value here; check maximizes EV long-term.

After the turn pairs the Ten, Elon has checked following his pre-flop 3-bet and flop continuation bet. My Ace-Jack currently holds showdown value against a significant portion of his 3-betting range that missed this board, such as Ace-King, Ace-Queen, or King-Queen. By checking back, I realize my equity for free and avoid potentially value-betting myself into a trap (if he has a Ten or a slow-played overpair). The board is extremely dry, so there are few draws to worry about. Checking here allows me to evaluate the river and potentially win at showdown without further investment.

River board 2s4cTdTh8c pairs the tens, villain Flash called our preflop 3bet (wide button range narrowed to strong Ax, broadway, Tx, pairs/overpairs) and flop c-bet on dry 2s4cTd, then check-check turn pairing Td. Ace-high no pair has blockers to AA/AK but loses to all Tx combos (now trips/two-pair), overpairs QQ+/JJ (if held), sets unlikely but possible. 8c adds minor club draw completion (board has 4c8c) and gutshots like 3-5/6-7/9-J straights unlikely improved specifically. No value to bet thin - worse hands (missed draws, pure air) fold, better hands raise/call. OOP first to act, checking maximizes EV: induces bluffs from missed draws (spades preflop, gutshots), weak Ax/AQ, allows hero-fold/call to sized bets (blockers good vs villain bluffs/value), controls pot, preserves deep stack (~9500 eff, 84 hands left) for better spots. Tournament phase favors discipline over marginal hero plays.

The river 8c is essentially a blank. Elon has checked for the second consecutive street after his pre-flop 3-bet and flop continuation bet. This sequence typically suggests either a complete give-up with missed broadway cards (like KQ, KJ, QJ) or a medium-strength hand (like 77-99) that is looking to get to a cheap showdown now that the board has paired the Ten. My Ace-Jack holds decent showdown value against the air portion of his range, but I am unlikely to fold out better hands or get called by worse ones if I bet. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and see the showdown without further risk.

I’m on the button with 8♠6♠ in a 3-handed pot with deep stacks — this is a spot to open a wide, aggressive range. Limping is passive and surrenders initiative; a standard 2.5–3x BB open gives good fold equity and keeps the pot manageable if I face a 3‑bet. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) to take the lead and play postflop in position. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll re-evaluate (fold to heavy pressure, or call/squeeze depending on size and opponent tendencies).

K2o is a trash hand offsuit with poor playability and dominated often by Minni's wide button opening range. Facing a raise, even from button, folding from SB is standard to preserve stack for premium spots. Deep stacked tournament, no need to defend this wide. Pot odds don't justify calling with such weak equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong hand in a 3-handed game against a button open. Minni has been active and opens frequently. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position against a deep-stacked opponent. While 3-betting is an option, calling is a solid defensive play here to keep the pot manageable and play post-flop.

On this Jd-2h-5d flop, I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. As the Big Blind in a heads-up pot against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check to the raiser. Donk betting here wouldn't accomplish much as I have some showdown value but not a strong enough hand to bet for value or a clear bluff. I will check and see how Minni responds.

I opened to 300 preflop and was called by the big blind; the flop J♦‑2♥‑5♦ completely misses my 8♠6♠ (no pair, no real draw). As the preflop aggressor in position I should c‑bet to leverage my range and fold out unpaired overcards while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll fire a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot = 200) to apply pressure and get value from worse hands/draws; if I get raised I’ll fold to heavy pressure since I have almost no equity, and if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only barrel with a real improvement (a pair or a favorable, non‑coordinating card).

I am facing a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 650 from Minni. With Ace-high (Ah 8d) and a backdoor diamond draw, I have enough equity and showdown value to continue, especially given the excellent pot odds (over 4:1). Minni is an aggressive player who likely c-bets a wide range on this board. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

The Ten of Hearts on the turn is a neutral card that does not improve my Ace-high (Ah 8d). I called a small bet on the flop to realize my equity and see if the opponent was continuation betting light. Out of position, the most prudent play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out now would turn my hand into a bluff unnecessarily, whereas checking allows me to see how Minni values the turn and potentially reach a cheap showdown if the betting remains low.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the J♦‑2♥‑5♦ flop (200) and was called. The T♥ on the turn does not improve 8♠6♠ — no pair, no spade draw, just six simple pair outs. Flash’s call on the flop represents a fairly strong and/or sticky range (pairs, Jx, diamond draws, some broadways), so firing a second barrel here would be a thin bluff that risks a lot of chips and can be raised off. With position and deep stacks, pot control and realizing equity is the better option: check the turn, see what he does on the river, and then decide whether to bluff/call/fold based on river texture and bet size.

On the river, the board is Jd-2h-5d-Th-3h. I have Ace-high (Ah 8d). Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, bet small on the flop and checked back on the turn. This check-back on the turn often signals a hand with some showdown value (like a small pair or a weak Jack) that she doesn't want to bloat the pot with, or a total air hand/draw that she is giving up on. Since I have some showdown value with Ace-high against her missed draws (like diamonds), checking is the best course of action. I don't have enough strength to bet for value, and turning my hand into a bluff is risky against an opponent who might call with any pair. I will check and hope to reach a cheap showdown.

I raised preflop and c‑bet the J♦‑2♥‑5♦ flop; Flash called. I checked the T♥ turn and he checked back. The river 3♥ completes a three‑heart board, but I hold 8♠6♠ with no pair, no heart, and no realistic showdown equity. Flash’s flop call and subsequent checks point to a sticky but medium range (Jx, small/mid pairs, maybe missed draws or diamond draws that didn’t fill). This is a classic thin‑bluff spot: a bet could fold some overcards or pure air, but it will get called by Jx and pairs and can be check‑raised by value — and I have essentially zero fold‑equity against hands that beat me. With deep stacks and a 1,050 pot, risking chips here for a low‑EV bluff is unattractive. I’ll control the pot and take it to showdown: check and show down rather than make a marginal bluff.

Preflop on the button with 3s4d offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability. Pot is 150 with blinds posted (likely SB limped or completed), to call 100. Even in position with deep stacks, 34o has low equity against calling ranges and minimal straight potential offsuit. Standard play is to fold trash hands preflop to avoid difficult postflop spots OOP when raised or with bad flops. No steal incentive here as blinds aren't folded. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit (A5o). After the button folds, the action is on me. Ace-high hands are quite strong in head-up situations like this. To take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately, I will put in a standard raise to 300 chips.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB, Elon folded. I’m in the BB with Kh6s, stack 15,050; pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (300 total). K6s is marginal but has decent heads-up playability (king blocker, backdoor heart potential) and with the button folded I will act last post‑flop, so I prefer to defend rather than 3‑bet light. The call gives ~2:1 immediate pot odds and lets me realize equity on many flops; I’ll proceed postflop based on texture (be aggressive if I hit a K or hearts, fold to heavy pressure on dry boards).

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a very coordinated board (7-8-5). This board texture likely favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many middling connectors and gappers. Betting here might turn my hand into a bluff or force me to face a raise on a board where I have limited equity against a strong range. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate Minni's reaction. If she checks behind, I can potentially get to a cheap showdown or see a safe turn card. If she bets, I will likely call once to see how the turn develops, given my pair and the strength of my kicker.

Check. Flop (7c 8c 5s) I have K♥6♠: no pair but meaningful equity — an open‑ended straight draw to 4 or 9 (8 outs) plus three kings that make top pair (≈11 useful outs, roughly ~35–42% to improve by the river, though some outs can still lose). The SB opened preflop then checked the flop, which looks like pot‑control with a wide, often marginal checking range. With deepish stacks and a decent drawing hand, I want to realize equity and keep the pot manageable rather than bluff into a potentially strong checking range or run into a check‑raise. Plan: check and see turn — call small turn bets to continue with my draw, fold to large, committed aggression if the board stays dry and I miss; if I pair the K or complete the straight I’ll take the lead aggressively.

On the turn 10d, the board is 7c-8c-5s-10d. I still have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. The board is increasingly coordinated, making it risky to bet. Minni checked back on the flop, suggesting she might have a marginal hand or a draw. By checking again, I am maintaining a small pot and exercising pot control with a hand that has some showdown value but is vulnerable to many cards in the opponent's range. If she bets, I will have to decide whether my pair of fives is worth calling, but for now, checking is the most prudent move.

Check. On the turn (7c 8c 5s Td) K♥6♠ has missed the straight but still has an OESD to 4/9 (8 outs) plus 3 kings — ~11 outs total (~24% to improve to pair/straight on the river). Flash opened from the SB but checked both flop and turn, which looks like a relatively wide/passive checking range; there’s no bet to call now. With only one card to come and modest equity, betting here risks getting raised or committing chips with weak equity, while checking lets me realize equity cheaply and keeps pot manageable. Plan: check to the river; if I hit the straight or pair K I’ll value-bet; if opponent bets the river I’ll call small/medium bets with the draw-made hands and fold to large overbets when I miss.

The board has run out 7-8-5-T-J, which is extremely coordinated. My pair of fives is now the lowest possible pair on this board. Since the action has been checked through the flop and turn, it's possible Minni also has a marginal hand. However, almost any hand in her big blind calling range (like 67, 78, 89, T9, J9, etc.) has me soundly beaten. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands and wouldn't serve much purpose as a bluff given the small pot and my previous checks. I will check and hope to get to a free showdown; if she bets, I'll likely have to fold as my hand has very little showdown value on this texture.

Board is 7c 8c 5s Td Js and my Kh6s has missed — no pair, no straight, no flush. I defended preflop and intentionally checked flop/turn to realize equity and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Flash raised preflop but has been passive (checked flop and turn), which indicates a wide, often medium-strength checking range (small/medium pairs, Tx/Jx, some overcards and occasional air) and makes him unlikely to fold a river bluff frequently. Betting here would be thin value at best and risks getting raised or called by better hands; checking is the low-variance, exploitative line. Plan: check to showdown; if Flash fires a small river probe I’ll fold to large pressure and only call very small bluffs given his passivity.

I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Hold'em. While being on the button allows for a wide opening range in a 3-handed game, 7-4 offsuit is generally too weak to open against competent opponents like Minni and Elon. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I’m in the small blind with T♣5♦ (T5o) and only the big blind to act. The pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete to 100 total. T5 off suits is a marginal hand and playing out of position postflop is difficult — raising to 200 will bloat the pot and often get called or 3‑bet by a wide, capable big blind, putting me in tough spots with a dominated holding. Calling the 50 (completing to 100) keeps the pot small, gives good pot odds (~4:1) to see a flop, and allows me to proceed cautiously OOP — fold to heavy aggression, float on favorable boards, or bet when I pick up equity. I’ll complete and see the flop.

Preflop as BB facing a SB limp (wide range). 5c7h is a weak offsuit gapped hand with low cards, poor playability OOP heads-up deepstacked. No strong value or bluff incentive to raise. Checking realizes equity cheaply against SB's wide limping range, avoiding dead money commitment with trash. Folding BB voluntarily is -EV. Plan to c-fold weak flops, play strong ones.

Flop is 4♦ K♣ 4♣. I'm out of position with T♥5♦ — no made hand and essentially no realistic draws. This is a paired board that heavily favors the BB’s calling range (Kx, 4x, sets, overpairs) so my raw equity is very poor (only three tens + three fives as outs and those often still lose). Priority is pot control OOP: check and preserve stack rather than bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Plan: check now and fold to any meaningful continuation bet; if checked to me I’ll reassess on the turn and only continue if I improve or get a very clear bluffing spot.

Preflop limp from SB is wide, check-call OOP standard with speculative hand. Flop 4d Kc 4c is paired board, K-high, no hits for 5c7h (7-high, backdoor club flush draw at best, no pair/straight draw). Minni checks SB first postflop, indicating possible weakness or trap but likely capping weak hands/draws. Deepstacked heads-up, checking realizes equity cheaply vs her wide range without bloating pot OOP effectively (acting 2nd here but BB positionally disadvantaged). No value bet, low fold equity for bluff. Plan per previous: c-fold weak flops. Check to see turn free, control pot, avoid spew.

Turn comes 4♦ K♣ 4♣ 9♦ and I still have T♥5♦ — no pair, no meaningful draw, and I’m out of position. My flop plan was to check and fold to a c-bet; the 9♦ doesn’t improve my hand or create a realistic bluff-catcher. Betting here is a thin bluff — the BB’s checked flop and can easily have Kx, 4x, sets or lots of hands that will call or raise, so betting just bloats the pot with poor equity. I’ll control the pot size: check and fold to any meaningful bet; if he checks back I’ll reassess on the river for a possible small bluff or surrender to showdown depending on the card.

Heads-up OOP on turn with complete air (7-high, no pair/draw). SB Minni limped preflop (wide range) and checked flop/turn, representing possible weakness but also traps/monsters. Paired board with K-high dampens bluffs; diamond draw on turn adds caution. No value, low fold equity OOP deepstacked. Checking realizes equity cheaply, controls pot OOP, induces bluffs on river if she values weak showdowns. Consistent with prior plan to c-fold weak boards. Pot 200 is tiny relative to stacks.

Board: 4♦ K♣ 4♣ 9♦ 8♠. I hold T♥5♦ — no pair, no real showdown value. Elon (BB) has been passive (checked flop and turn), which suggests a lot of his range is unpaired high-cards or medium hands that will check down. Many of those hands will fold to a small-to-medium river bluff, while hands that actually beat me (Kx, 4x, sets, 9x/8x) are likely to call. A half-pot probe applies pressure, preserves my stack, and targets the unpaired part of his range. I will bet a modest size (100) to try to take it down; if I get raised or called and face meaningful resistance I will give up — this is a pure bluff attempt with no showdown value.

River facing a small 100 bet into 300 pot from SB Minni after mutual checks flop/turn on a paired K-high board with diamond draw. My 5c7h is pure air (7-high, no pair/draw/showdown value). Preflop limp-check from SB is wide range, but checking down caps her monsters; small river bet likely thin value (weak K, paired 9/8/J/T, completed draws) or probe. OOP deepstacked (14k+ effective), calling 100 for pot odds (3:1) but 0% equity vs her value range, negative EV even if she bluffs sometimes. Consistent with plan: realize equity cheaply on weak board by checking/folding, avoid spew OOP. Fold preserves stack for better spots in tournament.

I’m on the button with Q♣3♥ and the pot is only 150; it’s 50/100 so to see a flop costs 100 and the min open is 200. Q3o is a marginal hand to open-raise — it’s easily dominated and vulnerable to the frequent preflop aggression I’ve seen from the SB. With ~150bb effective stacks and position, a flat-call is the lowest-variance, highest-ROE play: I can see a cheap flop in position, fold to any sizable 3-bet, and only invest more when I actually connect or have a plan for postflop play. Plan: call 100; fold to a large preflop reraise, c-bet or realize equity in position if the flop is playable (top pair, good draws), otherwise give it up.

Preflop in SB with J4s facing a button limp from Minni. Pot is 250, calling 50 more for total 100 into the pot gives excellent implied odds at 5:1 direct odds, and likely multiway with BB behind. J4s is a speculative suited hand with straight and flush potential, plays well in position post-flop (acting before button if BB checks). Folding is too tight from SB; raising is unnecessary with a marginal hand against likely wide limp range. Opponents from history play loose passive often, checking down many pots, favoring seeing flops cheap. Call to realize equity multiway.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Both the Button (Minni) and the Small Blind (Elon) have limped into the pot. While A5o is a decent hand in a three-handed game, it doesn't play particularly well in large pots out of position. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and keeps the pot small, which is a low-risk strategy for this hand strength.

Flop of T♥9♥7♣ is highly coordinated with straight draws (open-enders like 8x, QJ, J8, etc.) and flush draws (hearts). Multiway pot (Button limp, SB limp/complete, BB check) with passive opponents who frequently check down pots based on history. My J♠4♠ is jack-high with a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (8x hits 7-8-9-T-J), plus backdoor spade flush draw. No showdown value currently. Acting first in SB position, checking is optimal to pot control, realize equity cheaply on later streets if checked behind (likely given opponents' passivity), and avoid bloating the pot with marginal strength. Folding is -EV preflop investment; betting risks getting check-raised or calling down worse while protecting better hands from draws unrealized. Plan to check, re-evaluate turn with improved equity potential.

On a very wet board like Th-9h-7c, my Ace-high has very little equity against two opponents who limped in. This board texture heavily favors limping ranges which often include medium connectors and suited cards. Since I have no pair and no draw, there is no reason to bet into two people. I will check and hope to see a free turn, but I am prepared to fold to any significant aggression.

I called preflop to see a flop cheaply and I missed this one. With Q♣3♥ on T♥9♥7♣ I have no pair and only a backdoor heart/very remote straight outs — essentially a bluff-candidate only if heads-up. There are two opponents in the pot and both checked, so my fold equity is poor and a c-bet will get called too often by tens, nines, hearts and straight draws. My prior plan was to give this up unless I actually connected; pot control is the lowest-variance line. I will check and fold to meaningful aggression, re-evaluating only if the turn gives me a real improvement (a Q, a heart, or a cheap free card situation).

On the turn, the board is T♥9♥7♣3♦, highly draw-heavy with flush and straight possibilities. My J♠4♠ is jack-high with a missed gutshot straight draw from the flop (needing an 8) and only a weak backdoor spade flush draw. No pair, no strong draw now, minimal showdown value against passive ranges. Pot is 300 multiway with deep stacks (14k effective). Opponents have shown passivity in history, frequently checking down pots even on coordinated boards. As first to act in SB, betting risks check-raises from better hands or draws, isolates me from cheap realization of equity. Checking preserves pot control, induces checks behind (likely), and allows free river card where I can catch a J, 8, or spade for showdown. Thin value bet not warranted; fold impossible post-investment. Optimal play is check to realize thin equity cheaply in positionally disadvantaged but passive spot.

On the turn, the board is 10h-9h-7c-3d. I have Ace-high with no draws. Both opponents checked the flop, and the Small Blind (Elon) has now checked the turn. While my Ace-high could potentially be the best hand if everyone is completely empty, this board is very coordinated and likely hit someone's limping range (which often includes 7x, 8x, 9x, or 10x). Since there is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff in a small pot against two opponents, I will check to take a free look at the river. If there is any betting action behind me, I will fold.

I turned bottom pair (3s) with Q♣3♥ on T♥9♥7♣3♦. Both opponents checked flop and turn, but this is still a multiway pot and my pair has poor showdown value versus two players — many hands (T9x, 9x, 7x, J8 straights, paired overcards) beat me and small bluffs are unlikely to fold enough equity out. Pot is small (~300) versus deep stacks (~150bb), so pot control and preserving stack is the lowest-variance, highest-ROI line. I will check and see the river; fold to meaningful aggression unless the bet is tiny and offers correct pot odds or I improve on the river (trips/Q or a very favorable runout).

River 4♦ pairs my 4s for bottom pair with J kicker on a T♥9♥7♣3♦4♦ board. Multiway pot checked to river by passive opponents who frequently check down coordinated boards based on extensive history (e.g., Hands 1-3, 8-9, 18, 24, etc.). My hand has showdown value against missed draws (hearts completed nothing, diamonds only two), weak Ax/high card hands, and inferior pairs from wide limp/check ranges. Betting risks folding out worse (draws/bluffs) while facing calls from overpairs/better pairs or check-raises; thin value bet not justified against passivity. Checking induces likely checks behind or allows bluff catches, realizing equity optimally deep-stacked (~14k effective). Consistent with prior pot control strategy now strengthened by pairing up.

The board is quite coordinated with potential straights and pairs that could have limped in. However, since the hand has been checked by all players through the flop, turn, and now the river has been checked by the Small Blind, my Ace-high may actually be the best hand. There is no value in betting here, as I likely won't get called by worse, and a bluff is unnecessary in such a small pot. I will check to see the showdown.

River completes T♥ 9♥ 7♣ 3♦ 4♦. I hold Q♣3♥ — bottom pair (3s) in a three-way pot with a tiny pot (300) and very deep stacks behind. Both opponents checked flop and turn, which signals either missed hands, showdown-caught hands, or slowplays, and multiway check-throughs massively reduce my fold equity. Betting here would only get called by better hands (T9x, 9x, 7x, any pair >3) and fold out very little; as a bluff it’s unlikely to succeed against two players. The lowest-variance, highest-ROI line is to check and take showdown: preserve a large stack, avoid committing chips with a marginal hand, and fold to substantial bets. If faced with a tiny bet (well under ~1/3 pot) I might call, but I will not lead out. Action: check.

Preflop on the button with Tc6h (T6o). This is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, no suited potential for strong draws, and dominated by many better hands. Pot is 150, to call 100 gives pot odds of roughly 2.5:1 immediately, but multiway pots are likely with passive limped histories, reducing equity and playability. Deep stacks (149BB effective) favor waiting for better speculative hands suited/connected or premiums. Folding preserves stack for high-EV spots, avoiding marginal postflop situations OOP if raised or multiway.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. Elon has folded on the button, leaving only me and Minni in the big blind. While I could attempt a steal or complete the bet, playing this hand out of position against Minni is generally unprofitable. I will fold and conserve my stack.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with Ace-Four offsuit. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is a consistent opening size for me and should either take down the blinds or allow me to play a pot in position with an Ace-high hand.

I have A♥K♦ in the small blind facing a button open to 300. This is a premium hand that I want to build value with and ideally isolate the button rather than play a multiway pot out of position. With ~15k effective stacks, a standard 3‑bet sizing that exerts pressure and charges speculative hands is appropriate. I’ll 3‑bet to 1,200 total to define the pot and force decisions — if the big blind 4‑bets large I can fold/re‑evaluate, if I get heads‑up I’ll play aggressively postflop.

Facing a button raise to 300 and a small blind 3-bet to 1200 preflop from the big blind with J7s suited. This is a speculative hand with strong playability, potential for straights and the nut flush draw. Pot odds are excellent: risking 1100 to win 1600 (about 1.45:1 odds, requiring ~40% equity, but actually better implied). In 3-handed play, button open ranges are wide, SB 3-bet ranges are polarized but often include bluffs or light value given history of aggression from both. Flash (button) likely folds to the 3-bet often, putting me heads-up OOP vs Minni. Even multiway, suited connectors perform well deep-stacked with implied odds. Folding is too tight; 4-betting risks too much with a bluff here OOP deep; calling keeps pot small, realizes equity, and leverages postflop potential.

I opened from the button with A4o, but the action behind me is very strong. Minni 3-bet from the small blind and Elon cold-called from the big blind. A cold call of a 3-bet usually signifies a very strong range (premium pairs or high Broadway). A4o is likely dominated and plays poorly in a multi-way 3-bet pot. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I was the preflop aggressor (3‑bet to 1,200) with A♥K♦ and the flop comes 5♠ Q♣ 3♥. I’ve missed — I only have ace‑high and there are no realistic backdoor draws (two hearts including my A and only one heart on board means a flush is impossible). That said, I have range advantage as the 3‑bettor and this flop is very dry and low‑connected aside from the Q which will hit some of his calling range (Qx, some broadways and pocket pairs). 

Goal: take the pot down now when I can, charge unpaired hands and weaker broadways, but avoid bloating the pot out of position with nothing. I’ll run a standard continuation bet as the preflop aggressor but keep the sizing modest to preserve fold equity without committing too many chips OOP. 

Plan: bet ~1/3 pot (≈900). If he folds I collect the pot. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn — barrel only if I improve (A or K) or if the turn gives me additional equity or clear fold equity; fold to a large check‑raise since ace‑high has poor showdown value against committed aggression.

Preflop call with J7s was justified for playability and pot odds in 3-handed deepstacked spot against polarized 3-bet range, isolating OOP vs Minni's aggression. Flop 5s Qc 3h completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot or open-ender), only backdoor spade flush draw and a live J overcard. Minni's donk bet of 900 (25% pot) into 3600 is likely polarized towards Qx, sets, overpairs, or bluffs, but my equity is poor (~15-20% vs value, less vs bluffs where I can occasionally catch J). OOP postflop with no showdown value or strong draw, calling realizes minimal equity multiway streets while bloating pot; raising as a bluff risks stacks deep with fold equity low given Minni's history of calling stations and aggression (e.g., Hands 12,20,38). Best to fold and preserve stack for better spots, as continuing OOP with air is -EV long-term in tournament.

I’m on the button with J♥9♥ in a 3-handed game. J9s is a good hand to open — it has decent equity and lots of post‑flop playability in position against the blinds. With deep stacks (≈164 BB) I want to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll open-raise to the table min (200) — this gives fold equity, isolates when the BB folds, and still allows me to play many flops comfortably in position. If I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and villain tendencies.

Jd5c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability post-flop, no straight or flush potential, and dominated by better jacks in Minni's likely wide button raising range. Facing a raise to 200 from the button, calling 150 into a 350 pot offers insufficient implied odds given the hand's trash equity. Folding preserves my stack for premium spots in a tournament setting where survival and chip accumulation over 150 hands is key. Minni raises frequently from history, but this isn't a profitable defend.

I am in the Big Blind holding a small pocket pair (4h 4s). The Button (Minni) has raised to 200. With the Small Blind folding, it is 100 more for me to call into a pot of 350, giving me 3.5-to-1 pot odds. Small pairs are excellent for 'set-mining' in deep-stack situations, and even in a 3-handed game, 44 has enough equity against a Button opening range to justify a call. While I will be out of position post-flop, the price is too good to fold, and 3-betting here might inflate the pot unnecessarily with a hand that is difficult to play if the flop doesn't provide a set or a very favorable low board.

I am out of position as the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni). My pocket fours are currently an underpair to the board of 10-7-2. Leading into the aggressor on this board texture doesn't serve much purpose, as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands or get called by worse. I will check to the Button to see how she chooses to proceed. My plan is to evaluate her bet size if she continues, though an underpair on this board is relatively weak.

I have J♥9♥ on a 7♥ T♥ 2♠ flop — a very strong drawing hand: two hearts in my hand plus two on the board (9 hearts unseen) gives me a high-value flush draw, I also have a Jack over the Ten and a gutshot to an 8. I’m on the button in position and Flash (BB) checked, so a continuation bet here leverages my fold equity and builds the pot when I hit. I’ll c‑bet ~2/3 pot to price out some equity for his weaker holdings and charge worse draws, while still keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. If he check-raises big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to extreme aggression with just a draw); if he calls I’ll proceed based on the turn texture (value bet when I improve, exercise pot control on blanks).

I am facing a 300-chip bet into a 450-chip pot on a board of 10-7-2. My pocket fours are an underpair, and while they might occasionally be ahead of a pure air-bluff, they are behind all of Minni's 10s, 7s, and middle-to-high pocket pairs. Given that I am out of position and have very few ways to improve (only two remaining fours for a set), calling a 2/3 pot bet is unprofitable in the long run. The risk of being bluffed off later or losing a larger pot on the turn/river outweighs the current pot odds. I will fold to preserve my stack.

3-handed on the button with Qs4h offsuit. This is a playable hand from the button due to excellent position and wide stealing range in short-handed play. The pot is small at 150 with only 100 to call (likely blinds posted, possibly SB limped or folded). Raising builds the pot in position and can steal blinds frequently against these opponents who have shown passivity in some spots from history. Standard button raise size around 3x BB to 300. My stack is deep enough at 13k+, opponents similar. Folding too tight, calling marginal OOP risk, raise applies pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with JTo. Elon, on the button, has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, JTo is a solid enough hand to defend against a button opening range. While playing out of position is not ideal, the pot odds (250 to call into a pot of 450) make a call reasonable to see a flop. 3-betting is an option, but calling keeps the pot manageable while I have a hand with decent post-flop potential.
