Elon opened to 300 from the button and SB called, so this is a three‑way pot (pot 700). I have K♣ J♠ in the big blind. KJ offsuit has decent equity multiway but is often dominated by the button’s raising range (AK/AQ/KQ). With the small blind already in, a light 3‑bet or a tiny min‑raise is unattractive — it bloats a multiway pot and won’t reliably isolate. Calling 200 more (300 total committed including my BB) is the standard defensive play: I get good pot odds to see a flop, can realize equity with backdoor draws or a king/jack, and can control the pot size postflop. Plan: see flop and proceed based on texture — continue with top pair or strong draws, pot‑control or fold to heavy action with air.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a King) and a very weak backdoor flush draw with the Ten of Hearts. This board (A-Q-8) is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range. Being out of position against two opponents, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will see how they react to the board before deciding whether to continue with my draw.

I have K♣J♠ on Q♦ A♥ 8♥ — no pair and only ~6 clean outs (k or j). This is a three‑way pot with 900 chips already in; betting into two opponents with no pair is risky because I’m likely dominated by A‑x or Q‑x and will get called by heart draws and worse. I want to control the pot and see a turn card for free (or induce a bluff from a float), then re‑evaluate on a king/jack or if a heart lands. Check and fold to heavy action, call small bets with correct pot odds, or raise only if I pick up a real equity improvement.

Flop is Qd Ah 8h, giving me pair of Queens (middle pair) with 4 kicker and a heart for some backdoor flush potential and flush draw blocker. Checks from both opponents suggest weakness, especially OOP, consistent with their history of frequent check-calls and passivity. In position as preflop raiser, my range is perceived as strong (Ax, Qx, suited broadways, draws). Betting extracts value from weaker pairs (88, lower), Ace-high no kicker, missed draws, or floats while protecting against runner-runner draws or overcards. Half-pot size balances value and protection without overcommitting with marginal strength. Pot control via check is an option, but aggression maximizes EV in position against passive lines. Size 450 into 900.

On a board of Qd-Ah-8h, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a King) and J-high. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 450 into a 900 pot. This board texture heavily favors a button opening range, as it contains an Ace and a Queen. My gutshot offers approximately 8% equity on the next card, while the pot odds offered are 3:1 (25%). I am out of position and Minni is still to act behind me. Given the high probability that Elon or Minni has at least a pair of Queens or Aces, and the presence of a flush draw that could further complicate the board, I do not have sufficient equity or implied odds to justify a call with J-high.

Pot is 1350 and Elon has bet 450 on Q♦ A♥ 8♥. I hold K♣ J♠ — no pair, ~6 clean outs (3 kings + 3 jacks) giving ~24% to hit by river. The break‑even equity to call is about 25% (450 / (1350+450) ≈ 25%), so a pure draw is marginal. However Elon’s bet size is only ~half pot and his c‑bet range from the button includes bluffs, Q‑floats and A‑high hands I can occasionally outdraw; I also block some K combos. Raising as a bluff is thin here (I have no heart/backdoor equity and don’t block enough of his A combos), and folding would concede too much with reasonable equity and implied odds. Plan: call 450 to see the turn. If I hit a K or J I will play aggressively for value; if I miss and face significant turn pressure I will fold to avoid losing more with a bluff catcher; I’ll re‑evaluate on any Ace/Queen/heart turn for pot control or a fold to large bets.

Elon checked the turn after c‑betting the flop; I'm K♣J♠ on Q♦ A♥ 8♥ 7♣ with no pair. I only have 6 clean outs (3 Ks + 3 Js) — ~13% to improve on the river — and very limited fold equity if I lead now. Betting as a bluff is thin because his flop bet then turn check often indicates either a value/control line (A/Q) or a float — many of those hands will simply call. I'm the chip leader and should avoid bloat/fighting large pots with a marginal hand. Plan: check and control the pot, see the river for a cheap card, then (a) if I pair a K or J I can value bet, (b) if a scary card (A/Q/heart) hits and he fires, I fold most turn/river aggression, (c) if river is blank and he checks, consider a small bluff depending on sizing/read.

Turn board 7♣ is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete obvious straights or flushes, though it could connect with some straight draws like T9 or 5-6. Minni's check after calling flop on a draw-heavy A♥ Q♦ 8♥ board suggests a marginal Ax (like A10-AJ, A8), a weaker pair (JJ, TT, 99 preflop calling range), or possibly a missed draw turning into a bluff catcher. Her history shows frequent check-calls with passive tendencies, defending wide OOP. My Qs4h gives second pair (pair of Queens) with a terrible kicker, but in position as preflop raiser/C-bettor, my range is perceived strong (sets, two-pair, top pair Ax, combo draws). Betting extracts thin value from her weaker pairs, Ace-high no pair, or floats while protecting against river draws (hearts, straight fillers like 9,5,6). Checking risks giving free cards to draws or letting her showdown weaker hands cheaply. Half-pot bet (900 into 1800) balances value, protection, and fold equity without overcommitting our deep stacks (~12k effective). Allows room for river flexibility. Folding equity decent against her passive line.

I have K♣J♠ on Q♦ A♥ 8♥ 7♣ and am facing a 900 bet into a 2700 pot (to‑call 900). Calling requires ~25% equity (900 / (2700+900) ≈ 25%). I only have ~6 clean outs (3 kings + 3 jacks) → ~6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river, well below the break‑even threshold. Raising as a bluff is thin: I don’t block enough A/Q combinations, a raise commits a large chunk of chips (min‑raise to 1800) and will be called by many hands that dominate me. I’m the chip leader (15,900) and should avoid inflating pots with a marginal draw against an opponent who has shown willingness to fire for value. Fold and wait for a better spot with real equity or initiative.

I am on the button with QTs, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. It plays well post-flop and has great potential for straights and flushes. Standard strategy is to open-raise to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. A raise to 3x the big blind (300) is a standard and effective sizing here.

5♥4♠ is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with very poor playability. I’m in the small blind and would be out of position against both the button raiser and the big blind in what will likely be a multiway pot. Calling 250 to play OOP with this hand is not +EV despite the small immediate cost (250 ≈ 1.6% of my stack) because I’ll have difficulty realizing equity and will often be dominated. A light 3-bet to 500 is too speculative here — it’s small and unlikely to fold out hands that dominate me and could get me into a bloated pot unnecessarily. Preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

AhTc is a strong defending hand from the big blind against a button raise, especially after the small blind folds. Flash's range is wide from the button, including many weaker aces, broadway cards, suited connectors, and bluffs. Calling offers excellent implied odds with position postflop, good playability for floats, straights, and top pair potential. Pot odds are favorable: risking 200 to win 450 (pot will be 650), about 3.25:1. Folding is too tight given the wide range; 3-betting risks playing out of position with a marginal ace. Will call and play postflop dynamically based on board texture and Flash's aggression history.

Flop of 8h-7s-7h is extremely favorable for my AhTc: I have the nut flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity standalone), Ace-high overcard blocker, and the paired board reduces opponent's made hands while capping strong holdings like sets or two-pair. Flash's button opening range is polarized wide: bluffs, floats, weak pairs, broadway, suited connectors, but unlikely to have 77 pre (too speculative even for button). OOP as BB defender, checking first is optimal to pot control, induce bluffs/bets from his weak range, and set up a check-raise shove or large raise with excellent fold equity and implied odds. Direct betting risks check-raise from his value (unlikely strong) or getting called light; checking maximizes EV by realizing equity multi-way if he bets weak and plays aggressively on turn. History shows Flash bets flops often (e.g., Hand 51, 73), so high likelihood of continuation bet to exploit.

I opened pre-flop from the button and the Big Blind called. The flop is 8-7-7 with two hearts. This board texture is somewhat coordinated, but as the pre-flop aggressor, I want to maintain initiative. I have two overcards (Q and T) and a backdoor straight draw. A small continuation bet of 250 (approximately 38% of the pot) is appropriate here to put pressure on the Big Blind's non-pairing hands and to capitalize on my range advantage. If called, I have equity to improve on the turn.

Facing a small 250 bet into 900 on a paired 7h-8h-7s board with AhTc, holding the nut flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity), Ace-high blocker, and live overcard. Flash's button continuation range is wide: bluffs, weak Ax, broadway, suited connectors, floats, unlikely to have 77 or strong trips given preflop action. OOP check-raise exploits his wide range for fold equity while building the pot with massive implied odds if called. Small bet sizing screams weakness or thin value, inviting a raise. Calling keeps pot small but realizes equity slowly OOP; folding absurd. Raising to 900 (3.6x his bet) balances value from worse and pressure on floats/weak pairs, with stacks allowing (Flash has 8875). History shows Flash folds to aggression postflop (e.g., Hand 52, 73). If he shoves, we have odds; if calls, great spot on turn flush-heavy boards.

Elon's check-raise on a paired board of 8-7-7 is a strong move that connects well with a Big Blind's defending range, which contains many 7x combinations, 8x, and various straight or flush draws. While I have two overcards, my Queen-Ten high is currently behind most of his value range and even some of his bluffs. Elon has shown a pattern of high aggression in previous hands, meaning if I call now, I will likely face further large bets on the turn and river. Since the board texture favors his range significantly and my hand has limited equity to improve to a definitive winner, folding is the best way to preserve my stack for more advantageous situations.

On the button with 7♦5♥ facing a raise to 200 (100 to call) in a three-handed spot. Position is very valuable and the call is cheap — pot is 150, I risk 100 to win ~250 (≈2.5:1), so I only need ~28–30% equity. 7-5 offsuit is marginal but has enough flop playability in position to justify a defend; I don't want to 3-bet and bloat the pot with a hand that is often dominated. I'll call to see a flop in position and proceed based on texture and opponents' reactions.

Preflop in SB with J5o, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity multiway OOP. Button limp suggests wide range, BB likely to defend. Deep stacks but folding trash preserves stack for better spots in tournament. No steal value here given positions and potential multiway pot.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce offsuit. Minni (the Button) has limped in and the Small Blind has folded. Since there is no additional cost to see the flop, checking is the most standard and lowest-risk option here. Raising with a weak King out of position against a larger stack is unnecessary. I will check to see the flop for free.

I have King-high on a board of Ten, Seven, and Three. Since this was a limped pot and I am out of position against Minni, checking is the most standard play. I have very little equity here and don't want to bloat the pot with a weak hand. I'll check and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or if the turn brings an improvement.

Flop is T♥ 7♠ 3♠ and I hold 7♦5♥ — middle pair with a very weak kicker. We're heads-up vs Flash (BB) who called preflop and from the history tends to check-call a lot postflop rather than barrel big bluffs. The board has a Ten (beats me) and two spades (possible backdoor/flush draw combinations), so my hand is vulnerable to overcards and draws. In position I want to both extract thin value from worse pairs/draws and deny free cards to spade and straight draws, while keeping the pot manageable given my mediocre kicker. A small-to-medium bet accomplishes that and will be called by worse hands but fold out pure air. I will bet ~150 into a 250 pot (≈60%) — big enough to charge draws and get value, but small enough to fold to significant aggression and keep options for turn play.

In this limped pot, I am holding King-high on a Ten-high board. Minni has bet 150 into a pot of 250 after I checked. While I have one overcard to the Ten, my kicker is extremely weak, and I am out of position for the rest of the hand. In a limped pot, Minni's range is wide, but even against a bluff, I don't have enough equity or a strong enough draw to justify calling. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

On the button with K7s, a strong suited hand with a king blocker and flush potential. Deep stacks, great position to raise for value, steal blinds, and maintain initiative postflop. Standard 3x open vs blinds. Minni and Flash have shown tendency to call wide but also fold to aggression in history. Pot odds irrelevant since raising. Folding too weak, limping passive. Raise to 300 total.

I am in the Small Blind with Eight of Spades and Six of Spades, a decent suited gapper that plays reasonably well post-flop. Elon, on the Button, has opened for 300. With 50 already committed as the Small Blind, it costs me 250 to call into a pot that will be at least 650 (if Minni calls or folds). Given the pot odds and the playability of suited connectors, I will call to see a flop, despite being out of position for the rest of the hand.

Fold. 10♦4♥ is a very weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand that will be dominated by the button’s raising range and often by the small blind’s calling range. This is a 3‑way pot, which drastically reduces my equity and makes postflop playability terrible. Calling 200 into a 700 pot (~28.6% pot odds) is not enough justification given my likely equity and positional disadvantage; squeezing would be a thin bluff against two players. Preserve chips and wait for a much better spot to invest my stack.

I have Eight of Spades and Six of Spades on a board of Four of Spades, Nine of Clubs, and Ten of Clubs. I have a backdoor flush draw and no made pair. As the Small Blind caller, I am out of position against Elon. Checking is the standard play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor acts on this board, which likely connects better with a button opening range than with my calling range.

As preflop raiser on the button heads-up vs SB, initiative is with me after Flash checks the flop. My Kh7h has no pair, straight draw, or flush draw on this 4s9cTc board, but K-high has some showdown value against bluffs, and I have backdoor heart flush and straight potential. The board is draw-heavy (open-enders like 67, J8; club flush draw; spade gutshot), so Flash's SB calling range is wide but capped somewhat. A continuation bet represents strong hands like overpairs, 9x/Tx, sets, which he may fold weak Ax, low pairs, or missed draws to. Pot is 700, stacks deep (my 14925, his 8475), so betting ~2/3 pot (450) applies pressure, denies equity, and builds pot when ahead or takes it down often enough. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or letting him bet weak, losing position advantage. Standard c-bet spot for thin value/fold equity.

On a board of 4s-9c-Tc, my hand (8s-6s) has missed almost entirely, offering only very weak backdoor potential for a flush or straight. Elon's bet of 450 into the 700 pot (roughly 64% of the pot) is a strong continuation bet on a board that connects reasonably well with a Button opening range. Given I am out of position and lack even a basic draw or pair, calling is not mathematically or strategically sound. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. I have a stack of approximately 85 big blinds, which gives me plenty of room to play. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the action and utilize my positional advantage. This hand is well ahead of the average range of the blinds.

Three-handed, I’m in the small blind with 6♦10♦ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250). This is an out-of-position spot against a likely wide but still dominating open range. T6o has poor playability, gets crushed by most of the button’s holdings and will be difficult to play postflop with the big blind also to act. A thin call gives bad SPR and little fold equity; a 3-bet bluff here would be better with blockers or a hand that plays well postflop. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after SB folds. Pot is 450, calling 200 more (total 300 into pot), offering ~2.25:1 odds. Q2 suited is a speculative hand with queen-high, flush potential, and straight possibilities. Button (Flash) opens wide from late position (~40-50% range based on history of frequent raises). Equity realization OOP is acceptable with suited hand vs wide range; folding is too tight at 2BB call. Raising is overplaying a weak hand with poor postflop playability. Call to see flop cheaply, realize equity, and exploit wide range while pot-controlling OOP.

Flop of 6h 2c 2s gives me trips with 2d Qd (three 2s: 2d, 2c, 2s), an extremely strong hand on this paired, dry board. The board is low, uncoordinated (no flush draws or strong straight draws), and villain (Flash on button) has a wide preflop raising range after SB fold and my cheap call with Q2 suited. As first to act OOP with a disguised monster (preflop call looks weak/speculative), checking is optimal: induces a continuation bet from Flash's wide range (overpairs like QQ+, Ax, Kx, 6x, bluffs, etc., all of which we dominate except rare 66, 62, or the case 2h for quads). We beat nearly everything in his range. Betting risks scaring off weaker hands and allows check-behind; checking sets up a check-raise for max value/protection. Pot 650, deep stacks, tournament ICM favors trapping aggressive button. Plan: check-raise his likely c-bet.

On a very dry and paired flop of 6h-2c-2s, I hold Ace-high with a strong kicker. This board is unlikely to have improved Elon's big blind defending range. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot to capitalize on the board texture, folding out his weaker hands and maintaining the initiative. If called, I still have significant showdown value or the potential to improve on future streets.

Facing a 250 bet into 650 pot (now 900, calling 250 gives ~4.6:1 odds) on a dry, paired 6h-2c-2s board where I hold trip deuces (2d Qd), an absolute monster that beats nearly Flash's entire button opening range (overpairs, Ax, KQ, bluffs, weak 6x). Preflop, my Q2s call looks speculative/weak, disguising strength perfectly. Board is uncoordinated rainbow (minimal draws: runner-runner gutshots/wheel or flush rare). Flash's 38% pot c-bet is standard with wide range, often air/value thin. Folding or calling is suboptimal: fold wastes equity, flat allows cheap outs/check-behind. Check-raise extracts max value from worse (TT+, AJ+, sets <5% range), protects vs live draws, builds huge pot OOP with deep stacks (his 7925, mine 15325). Sizing: pot-sized raise to 900 total (~3.6x his bet) balances value/induction, looks polarized (nuts/bluff), gets calls from dominated hands while stacking off lighter. Tournament mid-stage (hand 78/150, 3 players), ICM favors trapping aggression. Plan: bomb it, exploit history of Flash's aggression (frequent button opens, c-bets).

Elon's check-raise on this extremely dry, paired board (6h-2c-2s) is suspicious, especially since he performed a nearly identical move in Hand #75 on an 8-7-7 board, which caused me to fold. My Ace-Jack offsuit still holds significant value here and is likely ahead of many semi-bluffs or 'test' raises. Folding would make me too easy to exploit on paired boards. I will call the raise to see how the turn develops and evaluate if he continues his aggression, while keeping the pot manageable with my Ace-high.

Trip deuces remain a monster on this 6h-2c-2s-9h board, beating Flash's likely range of overpairs (QQ+, AK/AQ), two-pair combos (e.g., A6, 96, 62), mid-pairs, and stubborn draws he called the flop check-raise with. The turn 9h adds a heart flush draw (possibly completing some hands like AhXh) and minor straight draw potential (e.g., T8/T7 gutshots), but doesn't significantly improve many calling hands; we still dominate most value. Preflop my Q2s call disguised weakness perfectly, and Flash's flop c-bet/call into raise shows strength but not the nuts (sets rare ~3-5%: 66, 2x<2%). OOP first to act on 2450 pot vs his 7275 stack; checking risks free card to draws or check-behind from cautious hands, missing value. Value bet extracts from worse (TT+, AJs+, 6x pairs), protects equity (beats ~90% range), builds pot for river stack-off. Sizing: ~3/4 pot (1800) pressures draws/overpairs while allowing calls from dominated hands; larger risks fold equity too high from QQ/TT but optimal mid-late tournament (78/150 hands, 3 players) to deny equity/charge. History shows Flash calls aggression (e.g., Hand 51, 53), so expect thin value calls.

On the turn, the board is 6h-2c-2s-9h. Elon has followed up his flop check-raise with a significant bet of 1800 into a 2450 pot. This line strongly represents trips (a 2 in hand) or at the very least a pocket pair that beats my Ace-high. While Elon has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands (notably Hand #7 and #23), calling here would likely commit a large portion of my stack to a hero call on the river. Given the dry, paired nature of the board, my Ace-Jack has not improved and is unlikely to be the best hand against this specific betting sequence. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation where I have a better-defined hand or more equity.

I’m on the button with 8♣5♣ — a suited, connected, speculative hand that plays well in position. With three players and deep stacks (I’m ~158 BB, Elon deep, Flash ~72 BB), the button should open wide. Raising takes the initiative, gives fold equity against the blinds (especially the shorter Flash) and lets me play postflop in position if called. I’ll make a standard open (min/2x size here) to 200. If I face a large 3‑bet I can fold this marginal holding; if called I’ll use position to realize equity and look for turn/river opportunities (flush/straight draws or cheap pots to exploit).

Preflop in SB with J4s facing a button raise to 200 (to call 150 into 350). J4s is a marginal suited hand with some playability potential for flush/straight draws, but out of position against a likely wide button range from Minni. Post-flop OOP is difficult with this gappy hand, and BB (Flash with 72BB) may defend wide, risking multiway pots. Deep stacks (168BB effective vs Minni), but folding speculative hands preserves stack for better spots. No strong blockers or premium value for 3-bet bluff. Standard fold to avoid tough post-flop decisions.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200. While I am getting 3.5-to-1 pot odds to call the additional 100 chips, this hand is extremely weak and plays very poorly out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and many hands left in the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a marginal holding against a capable opponent.

3-handed on the button vs BB after SB likely folded (pot 150 with to-call 100). T6o is a standard raising hand from the button in this spot due to position, dead SB money, and BB's wide defending range. Deep stacks (16k effective vs BB 15k), small blinds, goal to maximize stack by stealing frequently. Raise to 3x BB (300) for value/protection/fold equity. Folding too tight misses steals; calling bad without position.

I am in the Small Blind with Jh 8s. Elon (the Button) has raised to 300. In a 3-handed game, the Button's opening range is quite wide, but Jh 8s is a weak hand to play out of position against a player who currently has the largest stack and has been aggressive. Calling puts me in a tough spot post-flop without the lead, and 3-betting as a bluff against a large stack is risky. Given the current tournament status and my stack size, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

Elon opened to 300 from the button and the SB folded — I’m heads-up in the big blind with 9h3h and a 15,900 (≈159 BB) stack. Calling costs 200 more into a 450 pot; after my call the pot will be 650 so I need ~31% equity to justify a call. Versus a button opening range this hand has some hidden equity (suit, backdoor straight/flush) and with deep stacks I can realize equity and bluff-catch or fold later. 3-betting OOP with 9-3s is risky and inviting larger pots/multiple streets out of position, so I’ll defend the blind and see a flop — folding to heavy aggression if I miss.

Check. I have 9h3h on Kd Js 8h — no pair, only a backdoor heart draw (need both turn+river, ~4.3%) and a couple of low-card pair outs that are often second-best vs a button raiser. Out of position and facing a wide/strong c-betting range, I don’t want to inflate the pot with essentially nothing. Checking controls the pot and lets me fold to a normal continuation bet. Plan: fold to any meaningful C-bet; I’ll consider calling very small bets (≈≤140 chips) because the pot odds can justify chasing backdoors or a cheap turn, but fold to larger sizing (a 300-ish bet requires ~24% equity which I don’t have). If he checks back I take a free card and re-evaluate on the turn.

Flop board K♦ J♠ 8♥ is fairly dry but has some straight draw potential (e.g., QT, T9). As the pre-flop raiser from the button with position, I have a strong range advantage including overpairs (AA-JJ, TT?), top pairs (AK, KQ, KJ, KTs), combo draws, and sets. My T6o completely misses but is a standard bluff candidate in this spot due to position, Minni's wide BB defending range (which includes many floats, weak pairs, and draws that fold to c-bet), and the need to maintain aggression to maximize stack growth by denying equity and forcing folds. Deep stacks favor frequent c-bets (~60-70% frequency here). Checking back risks giving free cards to overcards/draws and realizes no equity on missed hands. Bet sizing ~40% pot (260) balances value (from Kx/Jx) and bluffs, charges draws, and builds pot when ahead.

Elon c-bet 260 into the pot on K♦ J♠ 8♥. I have 9♥3♥ — no pair and only a backdoor heart (need both turn+river, ~3–4% equity) plus a few low pair outs that are often second-best. The bet gives very poor pot odds: calling 260 to win the ~1170 pot after my call requires roughly 22% equity (260/1170 ≈ 22%), which my hand does not have. I explicitly planned to only call very small flop bets (~≤140) to realize equity; this sizing exceeds that threshold. Raising out of position with nothing is also a losing line. Fold, preserve my deep stack, and wait for a better spot to apply pressure or float in position.

I am on the button with a strong suited connector (7s6s). In a 3-handed game, this hand is well worth a raise to take initiative and build a pot while in position. A standard 3x open to 300 chips is appropriate here to pressure the blinds.

I have A♥9♥ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 250 to call). Short-handed this is a strong, suited Ace with good blockers and postflop playability. I prefer to 3-bet here to isolate the button and take the initiative instead of playing a capped, OOP pot. I choose a sizing of 1,200 — big enough to generate fold equity against many of Flash’s wide opens and to discourage loose cold-calls from Elon, but small relative to effective stacks (Flash 6,825) so I can comfortably continue if called. Plan: fold to a large 4-bet, defend/call smaller 4-bets selectively, and play aggressively on favorable flops (c-bet/bluff with blockers, value-bet strong boards).

Q5o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Facing a button open from Flash (who can be wide) and a 3-bet from Minni in the SB (suggesting strength, as SB 3-bets are often polarized but Q5o doesn't block well or play well postflop). Pot is 1600, calling 1100 OOP commits a large portion relative to hand strength, with dominated equity against typical 3-bet ranges (AQ+, JJ+ etc.). Folding preserves stack for better spots; no reason to speculate here in a tournament with 70 hands left and deep stacks. Min raise to 2100 is too thin without equity advantage.

Minni has 3-bet my opening raise to 1200 chips, which is a substantial 4x sizing. While 7s6s is a strong suited connector and I have the advantage of position, calling 900 more would commit nearly 15% of my stack pre-flop. With my current stack of 6825, an SPR of approximately 2.3 post-flop would make it difficult to play speculative hands profitably without hitting the board hard. Given Minni's demonstrated aggression and the high-stakes nature of the tournament, I choose to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot where I can better contest the lead or have higher raw equity.

Raise to 300. On the button in a 3-way pot with 4♦T♠ I have a marginal but playable hand — the positional advantage and backdoor outs make an open profitable. Calling 100 gives a cheap look but invites the shorter big blind and the very aggressive SB to see the flop cheaply; a 3x open (300) applies pressure, narrows the field and preserves fold equity. I avoid the minimum (200) because with deep stacks it tends to encourage loose calls/3-way pots; a 300 sizing is small relative to stacks but meaningful enough to isolate or pick up the blinds. Plan: if I face a large 3-bet from Elon I will fold (hand is dominated), but if I get single-call(s) I’ll proceed in position, c-betting when I connect or exercising pot control and folding to significant resistance.

Preflop as SB facing a button raise to 3x from Minni, who has been aggressive throughout the tournament. My hand is A5o, which is offsuit with a weak kicker and poor post-flop playability out of position. Defending wide OOP risks difficult spots multiway if BB continues. Pot odds are ~1.8:1 but equity realization OOP is low. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots with 170BB effective depth.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is the weakest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I am in the Big Blind and the pot odds are somewhat tempting to defend, I would be out of position for the entire hand against Minni, who has a significantly larger stack. Given my current stack size of 6725, I need to be more selective with the hands I play to avoid chipping away my stack on low-equity situations. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here.

3-handed on the button pre-flop with Qd2c offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against calling ranges. Blinds are posted (pot 150, to call 100), stacks are deep (me 170BB, SB 67BB, BB 161BB). While button is a stealing spot, Q2o is not in the raising range—too weak, disconnected, offsuit, dominated often. Opponents have shown aggression historically but also folding to raises. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities; limping or raising risks trouble OOP post-flop. Clear fold to maximize long-term EV.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 2s, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Elon has already folded on the button. Although it's only 50 chips more to call, the hand has almost no post-flop value, and Minni in the Big Blind has been active and aggressive. There's no reason to invest any more chips in this hand, even at a discount.

Holding Q8 suited on the button in 3-handed play is a standard opening hand. I have position on both Minni and Elon. A 3x raise is a standard opening size to put pressure on the blinds and take initiative in the hand, especially since my stack is currently the shortest at the table and I need to look for opportunities to accumulate chips without necessarily going to showdown.

2♦6♦ is a very weak, low-card suited hand that performs poorly out of position. Flash (button) opened to 300 and Elon (BB) still has a decision — calling 250 to see a flop gives poor equity and forces difficult postflop decisions against one or two players, while a small 3-bet has little fold equity and would put me in awkward spots with a garbage hand. I have a healthy stack and should conserve chips for better spots in this 3-handed game, so I fold preflop.

87 suited is a strong speculative hand with excellent playability: potential for straights, flushes, two-pair, etc. Facing a button raise after SB fold, Flash's range is wide (steal attempt likely). Pot odds are favorable: calling 200 into 450 (pot will be 650 after call, ~3.25:1). Implied odds strong with deep stacks (my 17k, opp 6k effective). Defending BB wide OOP vs button. Raising has merit for fold equity but prefer seeing flop cheaply to realize equity. Folding too tight given hand strength and position dynamics. Will call and play postflop cautiously OOP.

Flop hits me hard: top pair sevens with an eight kicker and nut spade flush draw (9 outs), plus the 7s blocks opponent's possible sets/pairs. Board is drawy with diamond flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., J9, 65). Flash's button raise preflop is wide (steals, suited connectors, broadways), so his range includes many overcards, weaker pairs, and pure air he'll cbet frequently. As OOP first to act, leading out denies equity to draws, gets value from worse hands like 9x, Tx, Ax high, and builds pot with my equity. Checking risks giving free cards or facing a bet where check-raising is fine but donk allows initiative. Pot 650, betting ~2/3 pot (425-450) balances value/protection. Effective stack 6375 allows aggression without overcommitting. With 66 hands left, exploiting opponent's position and range maximizes EV.

I have completely missed the flop with Queen-high and no draws on a board of Ten-Seven-Three. Elon has led out with a 'donk bet' of 425 into a 650 pot, representing at least a piece of the board or a draw. Given that I have no equity and limited backdoors, calling is not an option. While I could attempt a raise to represent a big pair or a set, Elon's aggressive leading range makes a fold the most disciplined and chip-preserving play here, especially as I am currently the short stack at the table.

Button, 7♥2♦ — essentially the worst starting hand. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call (min raise to 200). Calling 100 means I need ~40% equity to break even (100/(150+100)=40%), but 7‑2o vs two random hands is roughly ~12–15% equity — far below break‑even. Raising as a light steal is thin: Flash (BB) has shown willingness to defend and Elon (SB) opens/3-bets frequently, so a min‑raise will likely get called or squeezed and put me in a dominated, low‑equity spot postflop. I have a deep stack and many hands left — no reason to gamble with 7‑2o. Fold and wait for a much stronger spot.

Preflop in the small blind facing a folded button, heads-up versus the big blind with 6,275 effective stack. A♥2♣ is a profitable spot to raise as it blocks AA/AK, has decent ace-high playability, and good equity against the big blind's wide defending range (~50%+ equity). Standard raise size of 3x the big blind (300) to steal the pot or build it when called. Aggressive play maximizes stack growth over many hands against potentially passive opponents based on history.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game, especially when facing a raise from the Small Blind. Elon's raise to 300 is a standard opening size. Calling here allows me to play a pot in position with a hand that likely has good equity against a wide Small Blind stealing range. A three-bet is an option, but calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop without over-committing to a marginal hand pre-flop.

On the flop of Kc-2s-Jd (rainbow), holding Ah2c for bottom pair with ace kicker OOP vs BB defender. As preflop raiser, this is a standard c-bet spot: board has some coordination (possible QT straight draws) but is relatively dry. My hand has showdown value, blocks some overpairs (AA), and has good equity (~45-55% vs calling range of broadways, mid-pairs, draws). Betting extracts value from Ax-high, weaker pairs, draws, and provides fold equity against floats/A-high. Check would allow Flash to realize equity cheaply or bluff. Size to ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) is standard for polarization/value, maintains playable SPR (~9.5 effective), and fits aggressive image from preflop steal attempt. Maximizes EV by building pot when ahead and denying equity.

I have Ace-high on a board that features two high cards (King and Jack) which are very likely to be in the Small Blind's opening range. Elon is leading out with a continuation bet of 40% of the pot. Given that I have no pair and no significant draws on this board, calling here would be a float with very low equity. It is better to preserve my stack and look for better spots than to chase with Ace-high on a board that heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range.

Folded to me on the button in a 3-handed pot with SB dead money in. Pot 150, BB 100 to call if limping. Th4d is a playable hand from the button 3-handed due to position, fold equity, and some straight potential postflop. BB (Minni) defends wide based on history of frequent calling and checking down pots. Raising steals the 150 dead money often or builds pot in position if called. Standard open size around 2.25-2.5BB. Raising to 225 for value/fold equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 6d, which is a weak offsuit connector. Elon, the chip leader on the Button, has opened for 225. Playing this hand out of position against a big stack is not profitable, and 76o is well below the defensive range for the Small Blind in this situation. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack.

Elon opened to 225 from the button; the pot is 375 and I need to call 125, which requires ~25% equity to break even. In 3-handed play the button’s opening range is fairly wide, and J9o has decent equity and post-flop playability against a wide range. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to 3-bet as a bluff with a marginal offsuit hand (deep stacks mean less fold equity and tougher spots post‑raise). I’ll defend the big blind by calling to see a flop and then proceed cautiously — continue on favorable flops (top pair, good draws) and give up to heavy aggression on dry boards.

I’m on the big blind with Jh9d on a Qh–4h–2s flop after calling preflop. I have no pair and only a single heart (a backdoor flush that needs both turn+river), so my equity is low versus a button’s fairly wide but often-paired/overcard-heavy raising range. Out of position and with deep stacks, I want pot control and to avoid difficult turns — this is a dry board where I should give up to heavy aggression. I’ll check and plan to fold to any substantial continuation bet; I may call a very small probe (roughly ≤150 chips, ~30% of the pot) to realize equity, but otherwise check and see a free turn to reevaluate.

Preflop raise with Th4d from button was a standard positional steal attempt, called by Minni who defends wide based on history. Flop 4h Qh 2s gives me bottom pair with a stubborn kicker, plus backdoor straight and diamond potential. Board is draw-heavy (hearts flush draw, gutshots), so Minni checked likely weak/marginal. As preflop aggressor in position, c-bet for thin value against worse (overcards, weak Qx), protection against overcards/draws, and fold equity vs air. Pot 500, bet 1/3 pot (167) rounded to 200 for simplicity, but sizing up to 250-300 to deny equity effectively deepstacked. Choose 250 as balanced size denying draws while keeping pot manageable.

Fold. Elon’s 250 into a 750 pot means I must call 250 to win 1000 (break-even equity ≈25%). With Jh9d on Qh–4h–2s I have only a weak J-high and a runner-runner heart possibility (very low realized equity) and I’m out of position. Versus a button’s c-bet range (lots of Qx, overcards and heart combos) my equity is well below 25%, so calling or raising would be playing a marginal hand in a tough spot. I’ll preserve my 16k stack and fold to this bet; I would only defend to a much smaller probe (~≤150).

I am on the button with a very weak hand, 6-3 offsuit. The stack sizes are significant, and I am the shortest stack with 6025. Although it's only 100 to call from the button, this hand has very little post-flop potential and I don't want to bloat the pot or put myself in a position to be bluffed out. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

Heads-up vs the big blind with A♥J♣ — a strong, high-equity hand that plays well both IP and OOP postflop. I want to take the initiative, build a pot when I’m likely ahead of Elon’s defending range, and deny correct odds to his speculative hands. I’ll open to 300 (standard ~3x BB here) to get fold equity and keep the pot manageable against deep stacks; plan is to C-bet most flops and re-evaluate if Elon 3-bets (fold to huge 3-bets, call or 4-bet against small/isolatable 3-bets). Amount is the total I’ll commit preflop (300).

2c7s is one of the worst possible hands in Hold'em: offsuit, unconnected low cards, minimal showdown value, and poor postflop playability. Minni's raise from the SB (to 300 into 150) is standard aggression, and her range is wide but still crushes this hand. Pot odds are about 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), but equity is too low (~30% or less vs. her range), and position is poor heads-up. Folding preserves stack for better spots. History shows Minni raises frequently, but defending this wide would bleed chips long-term. Optimal play is fold.

3-handed on the button with 5♦8♠ (offsuit). With deep stacks and only blinds in, button open-raising is standard — I can exploit position and fold equity. 5-8o is marginal but playable as an open because I’ll have the last action post-flop and can take the pot down often. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — a size that pressures the 59bb big blind and sets up straightforward postflop play. Plan: continuation-bet favorable flops, fold to strong 3-bets from Elon, and fold to an all-in from Flash unless pot odds justify a call.

Pocket eights is a strong hand in a 3-handed game, especially facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive in previous hands. Button ranges are wide, so 88 has excellent equity against her calling range. 3-betting from the small blind takes initiative, builds the pot for value, and potentially isolates or folds out worse hands. Calling OOP is suboptimal due to position disadvantage post-flop. Raising to 900 (3x her raise size) is a standard sizing that pressures her while leaving room for further action. Flash in BB is shorter-stacked and less likely to get involved profitably. Deep stacks allow for post-flop play if called.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9d 4h, which is a very weak hand. There has been a raise to 300 from the Button (Minni) and a 3-bet to 900 from the Small Blind (Elon). Given my hand strength and the fact that I would be out of position against a 3-bet range, folding is the only viable option.

I opened to 300 on the button with 5♦8♠ to leverage position, but Elon 3‑bet to 900 from the SB. With Flash folded it's heads‑up vs an opponent who has been 3‑betting and playing big pots — his 3‑bet range is heavily value‑weighted (broadways, big pairs). To call I must put in 600 into the ~1300 pot, needing roughly 31–32% equity to break even. 5♦8♠ is frequently dominated and only has ~20–25% equity vs a typical SB 3‑bet range, so a call is +EV negative. A 4‑bet bluff with this offsuit hand would be too thin and leaves me in a tough, bloated pot deepstacked. Per my preflop plan (fold to strong 3‑bets from Elon), I conserve chips and fold.

3-handed on the button pre-flop with 5s8d offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability. Standard button opening range in 3-handed is wide (50-70%), but 85o is a gapped offsuit hand with low equity against calling/3-betting ranges and difficult post-flop play. Opponents have shown aggressive tendencies and frequent defense from blinds based on history (Minni calls/raises often). Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are marginal (+EV limp rare), and raising risks difficult spots OOP for blinds. With deep stacks (my 182BB largest), better to wait for stronger hands to apply pressure rather than risk chips on marginal spot. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit (T5o). The Button has folded, leaving only me and Minni (the Big Blind). T5o is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a player like Minni who has shown a high level of aggression and willingness to defend her blinds. Given my stack size and the remaining hands in the tournament, folding here is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary chip loss with marginal holdings.

Holding 2h 5c on the button with a stack of 5875 (approx. 59 BB). This is a weak, non-connected, offsuit hand. In a three-handed game, I should play a wide range from the button, but 5-2 offsuit is near the very bottom of any reasonable range. While I could attempt to steal or bluff, my stack is small enough relative to the blinds that I should prioritize better starting hands and wait for a more profitable spot rather than bloating the pot with trash. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Heads-up from the small blind with Qh Td — a playable, initiative-worthy hand against the big blind. Stacks are deep (15.8k vs 18.1k) and Elon shows a wide, often aggressive defending range; raising takes the initiative and leverages the SB's positional advantage postflop. Min-raise to 200 (2x BB) is a standard, efficient size: it gives fold equity, builds the pot when I'm ahead of BB's calling range, and keeps pot control reasonable if he 3-bets. Calling 50 would be passive and allow Elon to see the flop cheaply; folding is too tight. I raise to 200 to isolate, win the pot preflop often, and play the flop in position when called.

Preflop in BB facing a raise to 200 from SB Minni after BTN Flash folds. My holding 3s4d is extremely weak: offsuit, low, poorly connected, minimal equity against any reasonable SB opening range (which includes all pairs, broadway, suited cards, etc.). Pot odds are 3:1 (call 100 to win 300), but postflop playability is poor OOP with such a hand. Stacks are deep (181BB), so no need to gamble. Minni has shown aggression in past hands but folding trash preserves stack for premium spots with 60 hands left. Standard fold.

I’m on the button with 5♦3♦ — a marginal but suited, positionally-playable hand. With the pot 150 and two blinds to steal, I want fold equity and to play any multiway pot from a position of strength, so I’ll open to a standard 3x-ish size (300). A 300 raise gives good chance both blinds fold or I go heads-up with positional advantage; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand. Postflop I’ll play cautiously — c-bet when favorable, otherwise use position to realize equity or take the pot on later streets.

3h Js is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind facing a button raise from Minni, who is active. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (calling 250 into 450), requiring roughly 35% equity, but this hand has poor post-flop potential and low equity against her likely range. Flash in BB is short-stacked but irrelevant here. Best to fold and wait for better spots with 18k stack and 60 hands left in a 3-handed tournament.

In a 3-handed game, Q9o is a standard defending hand from the Big Blind against a button open. Minni's raise to 300 is a 3x open, and I am getting favorable pot odds (2.25:1) to call the additional 200. Given that I have a mid-sized stack compared to the others, I'll call to see a flop and evaluate, rather than folding or 3-betting a marginal hand out of position.

On a very dry, paired board of 4-4-7, I am checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) as is standard for the big blind defender. My Queen-high has some showdown value against her air range but is not strong enough to lead out. I will evaluate based on her bet size and future street development.

Flop: 4♦4♥7♠. I’m on the button with 5♦3♦ — I’ve missed the flop: no pair, only backdoor possibilities. I have 4 outs (any 6) to make a straight and 3 outs (any 5) to make a pair that would beat the board; combined improvement chance by the river is under ~30% and the runner-runner diamond flush is very unlikely. Flash (BB) called my preflop raise from a wide range and is relatively short-stacked (5,575), so a small continuation bet will take down a lot of his marginal holdings and gains useful info. I don’t want to commit a big portion of the effective stack with nothing, so I’ll make a modest c-bet to buy the pot or see one more card cheaply. If he check-raises big I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only with real improvement or strong equity. Bet size chosen ~250 into a 650 pot — enough to generate fold equity but easy to fold to a large raise.

On this extremely dry, paired board (4-4-7), Minni is expected to C-bet with a very high frequency regardless of her actual hand. My Queen-high (Qh 9s) has significant showdown value in a 3-handed game against her wider button-opening range, which includes many lower high-card hands and air. Given the favorable pot odds of 3.6:1 (calling 250 to win a total pot of 900), folding immediately would be too exploitable. I will call this flop bet to see how the turn develops, intending to fold to further aggression unless my hand improves or the turn card is very favorable.

On the turn card 6c, which is relatively blank and doesn't significantly change the board texture, I will continue to check to the pre-flop aggressor. My Queen-high (Qh 9s) has some showdown value against her air range, but it is not strong enough to lead out with a bet. I will check and evaluate Minni's action to see if she continues her aggression.

Turn: 6♣ gives me 5♦3♦ and the board 4♦4♥7♠6♣ — I’ve just made the 3-4-5-6-7 straight. Flash (BB, 5,325) called my small flop c-bet and then checked the turn; his range is likely dominated by single-pair hands (7x), medium pairs and floats. Very few hands beat me now (pocket 7s → full house, pocket 4s → quads), so I should be extracting value and charging the range that will call. Betting denies a free river that could pair and cost me, and will get called by worse hands I beat. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (800 into 1,150) — big enough to extract value from one-pair hands and charge draws, but not so huge as to needlessly overcommit. If Flash check‑raises very large or jams, that’s a strong signal of full house/quads and I’ll heavily discount calling; if he simply calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river and extract more value when appropriate.

Minni has increased her betting frequency and sizing on the turn, betting 800 into a pot of 1150 (approx. 70% pot) after a small flop c-bet. My hand, Queen-high (Q9s), has no pair and no draw on a 4-4-7-6 board. While it is possible Minni is double-barreling with air, even many of her air hands (like Ax or Kx) are currently ahead of my Queen-high. Given the sizing escalation and the fact that I have zero closing equity or a reliable way to win at showdown without hitting one of my six outs (which may not even be good if she has a 4 or a higher pair), I cannot justify a call. I will fold and preserve my remaining 53.25 big blinds.

AJo is a premium hand from the button in a 3-handed game. Position is excellent for postflop play. Standard strategy is to raise for value and initiative against the blinds. Opponents have shown aggression in history, but with position and a strong ace, raising 3x the BB to 300 is optimal to build the pot while maintaining fold equity. Limping is too passive, folding too tight this late in the tournament with deep stacks. Min raise is 200, but sizing larger for better pot odds control.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 3c, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Elon has raised from the Button. While Button raises are often wide, 93 offsuit is far below the threshold for a profitable defense, even at these pot odds. I am out of position for the rest of the hand and my hand has almost no post-flop playability. Folding is the only sensible option here.

Call 300. T8d is a good hand to defend in the big blind against a button open in a 3-handed game: I only need to put in 200 more into a 450 pot (about 30% equity), and against a wide button opening range T8s has sufficient equity and excellent implied odds deep-stacked. A minimum raise to 500 is too small to be a clean bluff (won't fold out many hands) and risks facing a 4-bet or uncomfortable postflop spots. I’ll call to see a flop, realize my equity, and only commit further with strong pair/draws or a clear read on Elon’s tendencies.

I have T8d on 8c Kc 2c — a middle pair (eights) with a decent kicker on a monotone (three clubs) board. Pot is 650 and Elon checked to me. I want to both get value from worse hands (small pairs, Kx that checked back, A/x floats) and charge any one‑club combos that currently have four clubs and can turn a flush. Checking gives him a free card and hands that want to bluff or barrel later will do so cheaply; betting gains fold equity and protects my pair. I’ll make a half‑pot lead (~325) — large enough to charge draws and get value, but small enough to fold to a polarized check‑raise (which will usually represent a made club flush or very strong boat). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the turn (continue on non‑club turns for value/protection, and slow down if a club appears or he shows heavy resistance).

Preflop AJo is strong from the button, raised standardly. Flop 8cKc2c is a highly coordinated board with a flush draw possible on every street, king high. I have ace-high with no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw, pure air. Minni donk-bets ~1/2 pot after check-calling pre from BB, which often reps polarized range: strong hands like sets, two-pair, Kc, or flush draws, bluffs less likely from BB donk. Position helps, but calling 325 into 975 pot requires ~25% equity, which AdJh doesn't have against her likely range (blocks some but equity low, around 20-30% vs nutted/draw range). Raising as bluff risky on draw-heavy board; she can easily have flush draw/set and shove deep stacks. Folding high card air on scary board maximizes EV long-term, preserves stack for better spots late tournament. History shows Minni aggressive, often bets/value owns pots.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. My stack is roughly 53 big blinds, which is sufficient for standard play despite the larger stacks of my opponents. I will raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop or play in position.

I hold Q♠8♦ — a weak offsuit queen with limited postflop playability. Flash raised to 300 from the button; I’ve already posted the 50 SB so it would cost me 250 more to call (300 total). Calling puts me out of position to the button and likely allows the big blind (Elon, deep stacked and willing to defend/3-bet) to enter the pot or isolate. Q8o is frequently dominated by a button opener and is a poor candidate for a 3-bet bluff (Flash has ~50BB, so a 3-bet could commit him or draw a shove or isolate into a tough spot). With deep stacks and 3 players, I want to avoid marginal OOP calls that give opponents favorable ranges and initiative. Folding preserves my ~169BB stack and waits for a better, more profitable spot — I’ll fold and forfeit the small blind rather than invest 250 into a weak, OOP hand.

65o is a trash hand with poor playability and low equity, especially out of position in the big blind against a button raise. Flash is likely raising wide from the button, but this hand doesn't defend well post-flop. Better to preserve chips for stronger spots with 58 hands left in the tournament.

Button with 5♣3♣, stacks deep (~169bb) and pot is only 150 with 100 to call. This is a cheap price to see a flop in position and 5-3s plays well postflop (flush/straight potential) — multiway pots reduce its value, but position compensates. A min-raise to 200 would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and is likely to get called by the blinds (Elon is loose/aggressive in prior hands; Flash defends). I’ll take the cheaper line: call 100 to see the flop and use position to probe or fold if I miss and face aggression.

Preflop in SB facing button limp from Minni (wide range likely 3-handed) and BB Flash short-stacked (53BB). Pot 250, calling additional 50 for ~5:1 odds plus implied odds. Kh6d is a decent completing hand: king-high with decent playability postflop, position on BB, blocks strong kings. Raising risks getting dominated or multiway; folding too tight given odds and dynamics. Call to realize equity and control pot.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 4s. The Button (Minni) completed the blind and the Small Blind (Elon) checked. Since there has been no raise and I have a weak hand, there is no reason to inflate the pot or attempt a bluff against two opponents with significantly larger stacks. Checking allows me to see a free flop.

Preflop limp-call was fine with Kh6d for its playability and position. Flop 3d-3h-2h is a paired board with backdoor straight/flush potential but I have zero equity: no pair, no flush draw (6d is diamonds, board has one diamond), no straight draw, just king-high. Multiway pot (3 players) with limpers who have wide ranges including sets (33,22), two-pair (A4? unlikely), straight draws (A4,45), flush draws (any two hearts), and weaker hands. First to act as SB, betting king-high is a pure bluff into two opponents, one short-stacked (Flash 53BB) who can shove wide. Low fold equity on this texture where villain ranges hit often. Checking preserves stack, induces bluffs/checks from worse, and allows cheap fold to bets from better/drawd. Position on BB but button acts last. Plan: check, evaluate turn.

I have 7-high on a paired board in a limped pot. There is no reason to bet into two opponents here, as I have no equity and the pot is small. Checking allows me to see the turn for free and preserve my stack.

Flop: 3♦-3♥-2♥ and I hold 3♣5♣ — I’ve flopped trips with a small kicker. This is a very strong hand in a 3‑way pot but is vulnerable to very rare combos (the one remaining 3 for quads or pocket 2s / 3+2 combos that make a full house). Both opponents checked, so their ranges are wide (missed overcards, small pairs, some backdoor draws). I want to extract value and deny free cards that could give someone a full house/quad on later streets. A pot-sized bet is appropriate here: it charges worse hands and draws, gets called by one-pair/medium pair hands and sticky players, and still lets me fold/reevaluate if I face a big raise (which would indicate a full house/quads). Bet 300 into 300 to build the pot and protect my trips.

Flop board 3d3h2h pairs the board low and brings backdoor flushes/straights, but Kh6d has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw, just overcards. Minni limped preflop (wide range including 22-66, A3s+, suited connectors, Ax) and leads out for 300 into 250 pot after two checks, representing value frequently (sets, trips, two-pair combos like A4, draws like flush/straight). Calling 300 into ~550 gets <2:1 with no equity/showdown value and risk of multiway pot with short-stacked Flash behind who shoves wide. Raising pure bluff with poor fold equity vs sticky button limper. Standard check-fold as planned; preserve deep stack (176BB) for premium spots with 56 hands left.

I have 7-high with no draws on a paired board. Minni has bet the full size of the pot into two players, and while this could be a bluff on a dry board, I have zero equity to continue. With only 5,325 chips remaining, I need to be more selective with the hands I play and not waste chips chasing pots where I am significantly behind. Folding is the most prudent move here to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

3-handed on the button first to act preflop with KJo offsuit, which is a strong opening hand in this format. Button open range is wide, and KJo has good equity, playability, and domination potential against calling ranges. Opponents: Flash short-stacked at ~53BB, Minni deep. Raising builds the pot, applies pressure to blinds, and takes advantage of position. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300 given aggression in history and deep stacks. Folding or limping is suboptimal.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300. With a stack of over 50 big blinds and getting decent odds (250 to call into a pot that will be at least 700), JTo is a strong enough hand to defend against a likely wide button opening range. While playing out of position is not ideal, the hand has good connectivity and high-card value in a three-handed game.

Three-handed with Q♥4♥ in the big blind facing a 300 open from the button and a small‑blind call. Pot is 700 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total) — break‑even equity ~22%. Q4s has reasonable multiway playability (backdoor flush/straight, Q‑outs) and versus a wide button opening range plus a calling SB it likely has at least the required equity. I’m deep (≈170BB) so I don’t want to 3‑bet this hand out of position — a min‑raise to 500 is too thin and will often put me in dominated spots. Call to defend, see a flop, then proceed cautiously: fold to heavy action on dry boards, continue with top pair/strong draws or exploitative aggression if we pick up fold equity or favorable action.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a relatively dry board (Jd-8c-2s). In a three-way pot and being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) to see how the action develops. Leading out (donk betting) often narrows the opponents' ranges to only hands that beat me or have significant equity. By checking, I can control the pot size and potentially induce a continuation bet from Elon with a wide range of hands, including bluffs or weaker pairs that I currently beat.

Three‑handed, out of position to the BTN opener with the SB also in. Q♥4♥ missed this dry flop (J♦ 8♣ 2♠) — I only have an overcard and backdoor hearts, not a made hand or a strong draw. Betting OOP into two players with such weak equity is low EV and will frequently put me in marginal dominated spots. I want pot control and to preserve stack depth for better spots. I will check and see action. Plan: call small continuation bets to realize equity or float and attempt to take the pot later; fold to large, committed bets. If checked through to the turn I’ll re‑evaluate and consider a delayed c‑bet on a favorable card (a heart or a queen) or continue checking for pot control.

Top pair top kicker (TPTK) with Kc Js on a dry rainbow Jd8c2s flop. Preflop I raised from button and got two callers, so their ranges include a mix of pairs, suited connectors, broadways, but this board hits my range well and misses many. Both opponents checked, indicating weakness or traps, but betting extracts value from worse pairs (like Jx, 88, etc.), floats, and weak draws while protecting against turn draws (e.g., 9T, 57 suited). Position allows control. Pot 900, bet ~2/3 pot (600) balances value, denial, and fold equity without overbetting vs. Flash's ~50BB stack. Checking back risks free cards and misses value.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack on a relatively dry board of Jd-8c-2s. After I checked, the pre-flop aggressor, Elon, bet 600 into a 900 pot. This is a significant bet (2/3 pot), but with top pair in a three-handed game, folding is not an option. Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me (AJ, KJ, or sets) and would force out the bluffs or weaker hands I am currently leading. By calling, I keep the pot manageable, allow Elon to continue bluffing on later streets, and keep the Big Blind (Minni) in the pot with a potentially wider range. I will call the 600 and re-evaluate on the turn.

Three‑way, out of position on J♦‑8♣‑2♠ with Q♥4♥. I checked as planned; the BTN fires 600 and the SB calls, leaving a 2,100 pot and 600 to me (break‑even ≈22%). I have only 6 realistic outs (three queens, three fours) and — importantly — no hearts on the board so a heart flush by river is impossible. Multiway and OOP reduce both my equity and any realistic fold equity; the SB’s call makes a successful bluff much less likely. Raising would be wildly speculative OOP and calling commits 600 to chase weak, often dominated pair outs. Fold and preserve my deep stack for a better, more profitable spot.

The turn brings a Queen, which is an overcard to my pair of Jacks, meaning I now hold second pair. However, the Queen also provides me with a gutshot straight draw (a 9 would complete an 8-9-T-J-Q straight). Being out of position, I will continue with a check. This allows me to see how the pre-flop aggressor, Elon, reacts to the overcard. If he checks back, it's a good result for my marginal hand; if he bets, I'll have to decide if the price is right to continue given my pair and straight-draw equity.

TPTK (KJ on Jd8c2sQd board) remains very strong after turn Qd, which introduces diamond flush draw and some straight draws (e.g., KT OESD, T9 gutshot). I block KJ and have good kicker vs worse Jx. Flash's preflop SB defend + flop call range includes weaker Jx (JT, J9), 88/22 sets (unlikely given check-call), mid pairs, and missed draws. His turn check indicates caution post-Qd but not monster strength. Preflop raiser image continues as value-heavy. Bet for thin value vs worse pairs/draws, protect vs completing draws (river diamond or runner-runner). Pot 2100, bet 1400 (~2/3 pot) balances value/denial, pressures his ~4425 stack (effective SPR low), gets called by worse without overbetting. Check-back risks free river card to draws or bricks where I lose value. Position allows control if he shoves/folds.

The turn Queen is a significant scare card that hits much of the Button's opening range (AQ, KQ, QJ). My pair of Jacks is now second pair, and while I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9), the 4 outs are not enough to justify calling a 2/3 pot bet of 1400, especially since my remaining stack of 4425 would be heavily committed. Elon has shown consistent aggression throughout the tournament, and while he could be bluffing the overcard, the risk of being crushed by a better hand is too high given the board texture and pot odds. I will fold to preserve my remaining 44 big blinds for a better spot.

I am currently the short stack with approximately 44 BBs in a three-handed game. While 8-7 offsuit is often an opening candidate from the button in a shorthanded format to steal the blinds, both Minni and Elon have shown significant aggression and willingness to defend their blinds or 3-bet. Given my stack size relative to the others (who both cover me significantly), I want to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position or being forced to fold to a 3-bet with a marginal hand. Folding here allows me to preserve my stack for better spots while maintaining a solid image.

Heads-up after the button folded I'm in the small blind with J♠2♦. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 more to see a flop. Elon (BB) is a deep, often-aggressive player who will call or 3-bet light, so making a raise with this weak, easily-dominated hand is risky — I’d face pressure or a 3-bet out of position. Better to take the cheap pot odds and see a flop: call 50 more (total committed 100). Postflop I'll proceed cautiously: continue only with a jack, good backdoor draw, or if I can realize fold equity; fold to significant aggression on dry boards.

Preflop in BB facing SB limp (Flash folded). Holding 2c5h, one of the worst hands in poker with poor connectivity, low cards, offsuit. Deep stacked tournament, pot 200. Checking realizes equity for free in position postflop against SB's wide limping range. Raising is spewy and unprofitable with this trash hand lacking domination potential or fold equity justification here. Plan to fold most flops unless major improvement.

I hold J♠2♦ on a dry Qh-7s-5d flop: no pair, no meaningful draws (a flush is impossible with only one spade in hand and one on board, and there’s no real backdoor straight). Elon checked, and against a deep, often-aggressive opponent I have little fold equity and would be called or exploited if I c-bet. I’ll conserve chips and keep decision-making simple: check behind to see a turn for free. Plan: fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve to a jack or pick up a genuine draw; if checked to on the turn re-evaluate with any additional equity.

On the flop of 7s5dQh, I hold 2c5h for bottom pair with a terrible deuce kicker. This is a marginal holding OOP against SB's wide limping range after they check. The board has two overcards (Q,7) that beat us, straight draws possible (6-8), and flush draws. Pot is 200, stacks deep at 18700. Betting risks folding out better equity realization or getting raised off by better hands/draws. Checking pot controls, realizes equity for free vs worse (which SB check reps often), and allows seeing turn cheaply. Bluffing here unprofitable given weak showdown value and position. Consistent with preflop plan to check and play postflop cautiously with trash that marginally improves.

I improved to a single jack on the turn (J♠2♦ => pair of Jacks with a 2 kicker), but this is a marginal one-pair hand — easily dominated by any Qx and vulnerable to better jacks/two-pair/sets. Elon has shown himself as a deep, often-aggressive player, and he checked both the flop and the turn, which can mean either weakness or a pot-control line with a strong hand. The pot is tiny (200) relative to our stacks, so betting here gains little value and only risks a check-raise or getting called by better hands. I prefer pot control and to keep the pot small with a marginal holding: check back and re-evaluate on the river. Plan for river: call small-to-medium bets as a bluff-catcher, fold to large aggression unless I improve to two pair/trips, and if checked to on the river consider a small value bet only if I improve.

On the turn, board is 7s5d Qh Jd, giving me bottom pair fives with a terrible deuce kicker (2c5h). Pot is only 200, stacks deep (~18k effective). OOP vs SB Minni who limped wide preflop and has checked flop and turn, representing weakness but could have overpairs, better pairs (Q,J,7), straights (T8), or draws. My hand has minimal showdown value and blocks little; betting thin value risks folding out worse hands while getting called/raised by better. Bluffing lacks fold equity against a checking range that often floats or has medium strength. Checking realizes equity for free, controls pot, and keeps me in position for river decisions. Consistent with passive postflop plan vs trash that marginally hit bottom pair. Will evaluate river check/call based on action.

I have J♠ + board Jd Jh Qh 7s 5d — that’s trips (three jacks). Against Elon's passive line this hand is very strong versus his checking/calling range; the only real worries are rare full houses/quads (pocket QQ/77/55 or combinations containing the remaining J plus a Q/7/5). The pot is small (200) relative to our stacks, so I should extract value from hands that will call (single-pair Qx/KQ, weaker pairs, some two-pair variants) while keeping risk limited. I’ll bet a small, standard value size (100, ~1/2 pot) to charge worse hands and deny a free showdown. If he check-raises a large amount I’ll respect that as likely a full house and fold; I’ll call small raises depending on sizing, but for now bet 100 to build value.

Facing a tiny 100 bet into 300 pot on river (pot odds ~4:1, needing ~20% equity) OOP with bottom pair 5s and deuce kicker from passive preflop limp-check line by SB Minni. Board 7s5dQhJdJh pairs top-end Js, completing possible two-pair (QJ, JJ) or trips, but Minni's double check suggests not monster (overpairs like QQ+ might bet earlier, sets/draws less likely after checks). Limp range wide includes Ax, Kx, suited connectors, pairs below Q; many missed draws (flush no, str8 limited like T8/T9 unlikely after checks), bluffs, or thin Qx/Jx value. My 52 blocks few strong hands, beats pure air/bluffs/worse pairs (76, unpaired), and pot control was plan but call cheap vs small bet. Deep stacks (18k eff), realize equity/showdown value profitably; folding too tight OOP to minbet after weakness shown, raising unprofitable with trash kicker vs calling range. Call extracts value long-term.

Pocket tens on the button in a 3-handed game is a strong, standard open-raising hand. Blinds are 50/100 (pot 150); calling 100 or limping is passive and gives the blind players free or cheap equity — I want to take the initiative and build a pot with a hand that plays well post‑flop. A 3x-type open to 300 is the normal size in this game (fits the table history) — it gives good fold equity, isolates the shorter big blind (Flash ~43bb) at a price that still leaves plenty of postflop maneuvering room against the deeper small blind (Elon ~185bb). If I get a cold 3-bet, TT is usually a call and can continue postflop; vs very large, polarized 3-bets I can fold or 4-bet depending on reads. Raise to 300 for value and to seize position and initiative.

73o is an extremely weak hand with no playability, suitedness, or connectivity. Facing a button raise to 300 (facing 250 to call into a pot of 450 from SB), the standard play is to fold. Minni has been aggressive but this hand has no showdown value, poor post-flop potential OOP, and defending wide here would be exploitable. Save chips for better spots with deep stacks.

Defending the Big Blind with A7o against a Button open in a 3-handed game. With a stack of 43BB, calling is a standard and effective play to see a flop while maintaining pot control, as A7o is ahead of a typical button opening range but doesn't want to face a 4-bet shove.

I am defending my big blind with Ace-high on a low, connected flop. This board (4-2-5) generally favors the defender's range more than the button's opening range, but checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most balanced play. I have an overcard and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3), giving me enough equity to continue if a bet is small, but for now, I will check and see how the button responds.

I have TT — a clean overpair — on an extremely dry flop (4c 2s 5h). I opened preflop and the BB called with a wide range, so many of his hands missed this board. I want value from worse pairs and to charge any backdoor draws while keeping the betting lead, so I’ll c-bet about half the pot. A 300-chip bet is standard here: large enough to get value/protection but small enough to keep a calling range of worse pairs and weak Ax hands.

Minni's half-pot bet of 300 into 650 is a standard continuation bet on this low, disconnected board. I have Ace-high, one overcard, and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3). The pot odds are approximately 24% (300 to call for a total pot of 1250), and my equity with the gutshot and the Ace overcard is sufficient to continue. Additionally, this board hits a big blind defending range reasonably well, making a call the most balanced play to see the turn without bloating the pot.

The turn pairs the five, which is a card that interacts well with both ranges but specifically hits the Big Blind's defending range. With Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw, I have decent equity and some showdown value. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most balanced play here, allowing me to see how she responds to the board pairing.

Turn pairs the board (5s) and significantly increases the number of hands in Flash’s flop‑call range that now beat me (5x trips/quads, pocket 22/44 turning full houses, etc.). I opened and c‑bet the dry flop expecting to get called by lots of weaker pairs and Ax hands; that call on the flop means his continuing range contains the small/medium pocket pairs that are now very dangerous. With an effective stack of ~38bb and a pot of 1,250, I don’t want to build a bigger pot thinly with an overpair that’s vulnerable to these combos. I’ll check to control the pot, induce bluffs or small value bets from worse one‑pair hands, and re‑evaluate on the river — calling small bets but folding to polarizing shoves/large raises that represent full houses/quads.

On this board of 4-2-5-5-9, I have Ace-high. Minni's check on the turn after a flop continuation bet suggests she likely has a hand with some showdown value but not enough to value-bet the turn (like a mid-pair or a better Ace), or she was bluffing and has given up. By checking, I allow myself to reach showdown for free and potentially win against her missed draws or weaker Ace-high hands. If she bets, I will have to decide if she is trying to buy the pot, but checking is the most balanced play to realize my showdown value in this position.

Check. I have an overpair (TT) but the turn paired the board (5s) which greatly increases the combos that now beat me (full houses/quads from 22/44/55) and the river 9 can give villain a turned/caught set (99). Flash's line — cold‑call preflop, call my dry flop c‑bet, then check turn and check river — is passive and looks like medium pairs or weak Ax hands that I beat, but also makes strong full‑house combos less likely (they would often bet/raise the turn). Betting here would risk folding out some worse hands and run into a value jam from the rare full house/quads; checking to showdown preserves my equity and avoids bloating the pot with a hand that is vulnerable to a few very strong combos. With the pot size (1250) and the opponent's effective stack, the safest +EV play is to check and show down rather than thin‑value bet or force a decision against stronger holdings.

A4o on the button in a limped pot (pot 150, call 100) is a strong spot to raise. Position is excellent, ace blocker reduces opponents' premium ace ranges, hand has decent playability postflop for floats or semi-bluffs. SB Flash is short-stacked at 3775 (~38BB) and may over-defend or shove wide, while BB Minni is deep but we can isolate the short stack. Raising to 4x the BB (400 total) builds the pot, takes initiative, and applies pressure to likely weak limping ranges. Folding is too tight; calling is passive without isolation potential. History shows aggressive preflop play works well.

In a 3-handed game, King-Queen offsuit is a premium starting hand. Elon has raised to 400 from the Button, which is slightly larger than his standard 300 opening. My stack is currently the shortest at approximately 38 big blinds. While a 3-bet is a viable aggressive option to take down the pot immediately, calling allows me to see a flop and keep the pot size manageable while out of position. Given the stack depths and the stage of the tournament, I will call to see how the board develops, intending to play aggressively on King or Queen high boards.
