Button opened to 400 and SB called; I’m in the big blind with Jh4c. Calling 300 into a ~900 pot gives me ~25% break‑even equity. J4o offsuit is weak and largely dominated by a typical BTN opening range, and with the SB also in the pot my postflop equity vs two ranges is likely well under break‑even. I’d be out of position and have little fold or bluff equity postflop; a light 3‑bet to isolate is risky and unnecessary with this holding. Preserve the deep stack, fold and wait for a better spot (position or a much stronger hand).

On a flop of Kh-Qd-Ks, I have flopped a full house (Kings full of Queens) with my Kd-Qc. This is a monster hand, virtually the nuts unless Elon holds the final King. Given the board is extremely dry and I am out of position, leading out would likely force a fold from Elon's 'air' (non-pairing hands). By checking, I am playing into the standard 'check to the raiser' dynamic, which encourages Elon to make a continuation bet with his entire opening range. My goal is to maximize value from a stack of roughly 34 big blinds; trapping here is the most effective way to ensure the pot grows before I commit the rest of my chips.

Preflop raise with A4o on button was standard isolation into limpers, building pot OOP but gaining initiative. Flop Kh Qd Ks is excellent for my preflop raising range: hits AK, KQ, KJs, pairs like JJ/TT fold to c-bet often. Ace blocker reduces villain's AK combos. Board paired kings with queen, somewhat dry (no flush draw, limited straight draws like JT), favors my range over Flash's SB calling range (often wide, speculative like suited connectors, Ax suited, pockets). Flash checks SB first, showing weakness; short stack (3425 ~3.8x pot) likely to fold medium strength or fold/call all-in wide but we have fold equity. C-bet 55% pot (500) balances value (top pair-ish) and bluffs (missed A-high), denies equity to draws like JT/AT, takes it down frequently. Check risks giving free card to villain's floaters. Shove too thin, small bet risks call but maximizes fold equity while keeping pot manageable. History shows Flash calls but folds to aggression postflop often (e.g., Hand 20, 32). Builds stack long-term.

I have flopped Kings full of Queens, which is the effective nuts given that only one King remains in the deck. Elon has made a continuation bet of 500 into a pot of 900. While I could raise to build the pot now, doing so would likely scare off all of his bluffs and many of his marginal made hands. By calling, I maintain a deceptive line that looks like a Queen or a pocket pair, encouraging Elon to continue firing on the turn with his air or semi-bluffs. My objective is to maximize value from my relatively short stack by keeping his entire range in the pot.

I have flopped Kings full of Queens, which is a near-nut hand. The Ten of Hearts on the turn potentially completes a straight (AJ) or creates flush draws, which are actually good for me as they may encourage Elon to continue betting or commit more chips. I have played the hand deceptively so far by check-calling the flop. By checking the turn, I maintain this 'weak' appearance, giving Elon the opportunity to semi-bluff with his draws or value-bet a hand like a Queen, Ten, or a newly formed straight. My stack is small enough that I can easily get the rest of my chips in on the river regardless of whether he bets or checks here, but checking is the best way to keep his entire range in the pot.

Turn 10h completes some heart flush draws and adds straight draw possibilities (e.g., QJ, J9), increasing Flash's equity if he has a draw after check-calling flop. However, board remains king-heavy (paired Kh Ks Qd), favoring my perceived range as preflop raiser and flop c-bettor (AKxx, KQ, sets, two-pair combos). A4o blocks strong Ax and provides ace-high showdown if needed, but primary plan is aggression for fold equity against short-stacked SB (2925 ~1.5x pot) who has checked twice, indicating capped range (likely Kx weak kicker, Qx, mid-pairs, missed draws). History shows Flash folds to repeated pressure (e.g., Hands 20, 32, 49). Betting ~2/3 pot (1300) balances value from trapped KQ/KT and bluffs out draws/medium strength, denying equity while keeping stack commitment low (7% of my 17k). Checking risks free river card to villain's live draws or induces bluff-catch, suboptimal OOP shorty. Shove too thin (he snaps better); smaller bet invites calls. Builds stack via thin value/fold equity in late stage vs short stack.

I have the absolute nuts. With the board showing Kh-Qd-Ks-Th and holding Kd-Qc, I have a full house, Kings full of Queens. Since I account for three of the four Kings (one in hand, two on board), it is impossible for my opponent to have Quads or a better full house. Elon's bet of 1300 into a 1900 pot suggests he either has a very strong hand (like a King, a straight with AJ, or a set of Tens) or is semi-bluffing with a heart draw. My remaining stack is 2925, and after calling this 1300 bet, I will have 1625 left in a 4500 pot (an SPR of roughly 0.36). By just calling, I maintain a deceptive line that looks like a draw or a weak Queen, which encourages Elon to continue bluffing or to value-bet the river. Given how shallow the stacks are relative to the pot, all the money is going in on the river regardless, but calling the turn is the best way to maximize the chance that Elon commits the rest of his chips.

I have the absolute nuts with Kings full of Queens (Kd-Kh-Ks-Qc-Qd). On a board of Kh-Qd-Ks-Th-7h, it is impossible for any opponent to hold quads or a superior full house, as I hold the third King and second Queen. Elon has been the aggressor on every street, having raised pre-flop and bet both the flop and the turn. My line of check-calling throughout the hand has been designed to trap and keep his entire range, including bluffs, in the pot. With only 1,625 chips remaining and a pot of 4,500, checking the river is the most effective way to induce a final bet or a shove from Elon's bluffs, straights, or flushes. If I lead out, I risk forcing a fold from his air, whereas a check invites him to attempt one last bluff or value-bet a worse made hand.

River 7h is a blank for most hands but completes heart flushes (three on board now). Flash has check-called preflop iso raise, flop c-bet on paired kings board, and large turn bet OOP as short stack (now 1625 into 4500 pot ~28% pot odds for call). His range is capped: weak Kx (KJo-K9o/s), Qx (AQo/s, KQo/s but KQ strong), mid-pairs (JJ/TT folded pre/often flop), missed draws (JT for str/drw turned flush? unlikely cold call pre+flop+turn). Strong hands like KK/sets/KQ/AK boat value-bet/raise earlier; flushes (two hearts suited like Ahxh) likely check-raise turn/river given odds. Ad blocks his AK/AQ top value; 4s irrelevant. Line reps Kx/KQ/boats thin value, but history (Hands 20,32,49,58,91) shows Flash folds OOP to repeated aggression vs my range. Shove 1625 (~36% pot) maximizes fold equity vs capped range (weak top/mid showdown), risks only 10% stack for 4500+ win if folds (huge +EV late tournament). Check loses at showdown (ace-high crushed); small bet called wide. Consistent with aggressive preflop/flop/turn story, exploits short-stack ICM/passivity. Builds stack vs weak shorty with 52 hands left.

I have the absolute nuts. On a board of Kh-Qd-Ks-Th-7h, my hand Kd-Qc gives me Kings full of Queens. Since I hold the third King and there are two on the board, it is impossible for my opponent to have four of a kind. My full house also beats any other possible full house (like Queens full, Tens full, or Sevens full), any straight, and any flush. Throughout the hand, I have played deceptively by check-calling to induce Elon to continue betting with his entire range. Elon has now bet exactly my remaining stack of 1,625 chips into a pot of 4,500. This is the ideal outcome of my trapping strategy. I will call to commit my remaining chips and win the pot.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fours in a 3-handed game. Pocket pairs are strong opening hands in this format, and I have a healthy stack of 77.5 big blinds. I will put in a standard 3x raise to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage over the blinds.

I’m on the small blind with pocket 5s facing a button open to 300. I’ve already posted 50, so calling costs 250 more (300 total committed). With deep stacks and an aggressive button who opens wide, this is a standard set-mining / pot-control spot — calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves implied odds to hit a set, and avoids building a bloated pot OOP with a small pair. A minimum 3-bet is too weak and a larger 3-bet risks bloating the pot without a made hand. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to extreme postflop action if I miss and face heavy resistance, play aggressively if I flop a set or strong two-pair.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 by Flash and a small blind call by Minni for 250 more, making the pot 700 and costing me 200 to call. My hand is K4 offsuit, which is a marginal holding with poor playability multiway out of position. K4o has limited equity against likely calling ranges from both opponents, and postflop dynamics would be tough OOP against two players. With 146 chips BB (14625 stack), folding trash preserves my second-place stack (behind Minni's 17225, ahead of Flash's 7450) for better spots late in the tournament (hand 99/150). Pot odds are ~3.5:1 but insufficient for this hand multiway. Standard fold.

Called preflop as a set-mining/cash-control spot and I missed the set. Flop is 6s-3c-6h, so my pocket 5s is an underpair to the paired sixes — behind any 6 and most pocket pairs 7+. Flash was the preflop raiser (button) with a wide range, but I'm OOP and with deep stacks I don't want to bloat the pot with just an underpair. I check to control the pot, realize my equity cheaply, and induce bluffs. Plan: check-fold to a meaningful continuation bet (anything near or above ~1/2 pot), consider calling a very small probe-bet to see a cheap turn only if villain shows lots of light aggression; if Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn for a delayed bluff or showdown line.

The flop of 6s-3c-6h is relatively favorable for my pocket fours, as it is unlikely to have improved Minni's range unless she is holding a six or a higher pocket pair. With the board paired and very dry, a continuation bet is standard here to protect my hand against overcards and define where I am in the hand. I will bet 300 into the 700 pot, which is a sizing that puts pressure on high-card hands while keeping the pot manageable.

I have 5♦5♥ on 6♠-3♣-6♥ — an underpair to the paired sixes. Flash (button) opened wide preflop and has made a small probe-bet (300 into a 1000 pot ≈30%). With deep stacks and a loose button range this is the sort of bet I can call to keep the pot controllable and see a cheap turn. Calling 300 preserves options: I can fold to significant aggression, call small turn bets to realize equity, and I still have backdoor value if a 5 or a favorable runout appears. I will not lead or raise OOP with an underpair; if Flash raises me or barrels large on the turn (≈≥1/2 pot) I’ll fold. Therefore I call the 300 to see one more card and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have 5♦5♥ on 6♠-3♣-6♥-K♣ — an underpair to the paired sixes and now a dangerous overcard (K) on the turn. I’m out of position vs the button (Flash) who opened wide preflop and made a small flop c-bet that I elected to call. With no backdoor draws and very little fold equity, I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP or bluff into a range that improved heavily on the turn. Checking controls the pot and keeps my options open: if Flash checks back I get to see a cheap river; if he fires a small turn bet (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ ≤ ~430) I can call to realize equity and potentially see a river; if he bets medium/large (≈ ≥ 1/2 pot ≈ ≥ ~650) or shows strong aggression I’ll fold — my hand is highly likely behind. I will not lead or raise OOP with just an underpair. Therefore: check and re-evaluate based on Flash’s turn sizing.

The turn King is an overcard that likely connects with Minni's calling range from the small blind (Kx hands). Having bet the flop with pocket fours on a paired board and been called, my hand is now primarily a bluff-catcher or a hand with thin showdown value. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid being check-raised off my equity, and allow myself to see the river for free. If she has a 6 or a King, I'm not getting better hands to fold by betting, and I'm unlikely to get value from worse hands. Checking is the most stable play here.

Board: 6s 3c 6h Kc 9d. I hold 5d5h — I never improved and am an underpair to the paired sixes. Flash (button) opened wide preflop, c‑bet a small 300 on the paired flop and then checked the turn. That line is ambiguous: it can be a weak c‑bet (air/overcards) or a pot‑control line with a strong made hand (6x, Kx, or a pocket pair). I have essentially no fold equity OOP on the river — a bluff would need to fold a large percentage of his range to be +EV (for example a half‑pot bet ~650 requires >33% folds), and his line suggests he’ll call down many Kx and pocket pairs. I also have decent stack preservation value and little to gain by bloating the pot with a likely second‑best hand. Best play is to check to showdown; if Flash leads into me I can fold to large aggression or call a very small thin value/probe bet, but I will not bluff the river here.

On the river, the board is 6s-3c-6h-Kc-9d. I hold pocket fours, which is currently the third pair on the board. Minni has checked to me for the third time this hand (having check-called the flop and checked the turn). While the King and Nine are overcards to my pair, Minni's passive line suggests she likely has a weak made hand or missed high cards. My pocket fours have reasonable showdown value against her range of missed draws or lower pocket pairs like 22. Betting is unlikely to get a better hand to fold and won't be called by many worse hands, so checking back to see the showdown and preserve my stack is the most logical choice.

Q♦2♥ is a very weak, frequently-dominated offsuit queen with a terrible kicker. I’m first to act preflop in a 3‑handed pot with both the SB (Elon) and BB (Flash) to act behind me; both players have shown willingness to defend and 3‑bet, so attempting a steal with this holding gives me low fold equity and creates awkward postflop decisions out of position. Limping is also undesirable because it invites multiway action with a hand that plays poorly. With a healthy stack I’ll preserve chips and fold — wait for a better spot to apply pressure or play a hand with real postflop playability.

Preflop in the small blind, heads-up against Flash in the big blind after Minni folds on the button. My hand is 3d5c, which is offsuit, disconnected, and low-ranked trash with no speculative value. Stack sizes are deep (145BB effective with Flash at 70BB), but completing the small blind with this hand offers poor implied odds and is dominated by Flash's likely calling range. Folding preserves chips for better spots, avoiding difficult postflop situations where this hand plays poorly multiway or heads-up. Raising to steal is suboptimal with such a weak hand, as Flash can defend wide and we lose when called. Standard fold to maximize long-term EV.

AQo is a premium hand on the button in a 3-handed pot. With the pot at 150 from blinds and to call 100, it appears the SB (Flash) has folded, leaving us vs the BB (Minni) who has a deep stack of 18k+. Raising is standard to isolate, build the pot with position, and apply pressure. Min-raise to 200 is too small; a standard 3x raise to 300 gives good pot odds while maintaining fold equity and allowing post-flop flexibility. My stack of 14.5k supports this aggression to maximize EV over the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 9c. Elon, on the button, has raised to 300. While 98-offsuit is a decent speculative hand, playing it out of position against the chip leader (Minni in the BB) and a very active button (Elon) is risky, especially as the shortest stack at the table. I need to preserve my 71 big blinds for better spots where I have a range advantage or position.

I’m in the big blind facing a standard button open to 300. I hold Qc5h — a marginal, often‑dominated hand, but against a likely wide button opening and with very deep stacks the price to see a flop is reasonable. I need to call 200 more (300 total committed) which gives me ~30% pot odds to continue; I’ll defend and see a flop but won’t 3‑bet bluff with this hand. Postflop plan: continue only with a made queen (or better), two pair+, or strong draws; fold to significant pressure on dry boards where I miss.

Check. I missed this flop entirely with Q5o — no pair, no meaningful draws (only a single heart backdoor), and I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser. The board is paired (6-6-8) which reduces my fold equity and makes a flop bluff low EV: Elon’s raising range contains lots of hands that connect here (6x, 8x, and many overcards/pairs that will call or continue). With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with air — pot control and giving up to pressure is the prudent line. Plan: check and fold to any significant bet; only consider calling a very small probe (e.g. a ~100 c-bet) to see a cheap turn if I have a read he’s firing tiny often, otherwise give up.

Paired flop with 6s-6h-8d rainbow board hits few strong hands from BB calling range. My AQo has ace-high showdown value, blocks AA/KQ, and position for post-flop control. Minni's check suggests weakness or trap, but vs her history of aggression turning passive post-flop sometimes, c-bet for fold equity against overcards/draws, thin value vs 66/86/76, and pot control. Pot 650, betting ~2/3 pot (450) builds it nicely without overcommitting, maintains range advantage. Deep stacks allow flexibility.

Fold. I checked the flop with Q5o and missed completely — no pair and no realistic draws on 6s-6h-8d. Elon’s 450 into the 650 pot (~69% pot) leaves me needing ~29% equity to justify a call, which Q5o doesn’t have versus a preflop raiser’s range that connects well here (6x/8x/overpairs/sets). The paired board also reduces my fold equity, and I’m out of position; bloat the pot with air is low EV. Unless I have a very specific read that he’s barreling huge with complete air, I’ll preserve chips and fold.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with J9o. This is a standard opening hand from this position to apply pressure to the blinds. My stack size is around 71 BBs, which is healthy enough to open-raise and play post-flop in position. I will raise to 300, which has been a consistent opening size for me.

I'm in the small blind with 8c9s facing a button open to 300 (pot 450) and I must call 250. 8-9 offsuit is a marginal, draw-heavy hand that becomes hard to play out of position — and if the big blind defends this will likely be a 3-way pot where my equity drops significantly. A min 3-bet won't reliably isolate and a larger 3-bet is too speculative with this hand. With a deep stack and many hands left, I’ll conserve chips and fold this marginal OOP spot.

Preflop in BB facing a standard button raise to 300 from Flash (6850 stack). My holding Js8d is offsuit with marginal high card strength and poor connectivity/suitedness. Button ranges are wide but J8o has subpar equity (~35-40% vs loose range) and limited playability OOP. Pot odds 450:200 (~2.25:1) require ~31% equity, but postflop dynamics favor folding marginal hands to avoid difficult OOP spots. Flash aggressive historically, but no need to defend wide here. Preserve stack (14,825) for premium spots with 3 players left and 48 hands remaining.

J4o on the button is marginal but in a 3-handed game it’s a standard open-raise candidate to pick up the blinds. Pot is 150 and calling 100 simply limp‑calls into a likely multiway pot OOP. I’ll make the minimum open to 200 to generate fold equity and keep the pot controllable — if SB Elon 3‑bets big I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack; if I get one caller I’ll proceed cautiously post‑flop (small c‑bets on favorable textures, check back dry boards). Raising avoids being forced into awkward multiway spots with a dominated hand and maximizes long‑term EV from position.

Hand #103, pre-flop in SB with As4c (A4 offsuit). Facing a button min-raise to 200 by Minni, who has been aggressive historically. Pot is 350, to call 150 OOP. A4o has poor post-flop playability out of position, dominated by better aces in Minni's wide button range, and no strong blockers or suited connectivity. Deep stacks (147BB effective vs Flash's 72BB), no need to defend marginal hands in SB. Flash in BB may 3-bet or call, but folding preserves stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 5s. Minni has raised to 200 from the Button, and Elon has folded. Although I only need to call 100 more to see a flop (getting 3.5:1 pot odds), 75 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against a deep-stacked and active player like Minni. To preserve my stack and avoid difficult post-flop situations with a marginal hand, I will fold.

3-handed on the button with QsJd, a strong offsuit broadway hand with good playability, position, and equity against the big blind's wide defending range. The pot is unopened (SB folded, pot 150 with blinds), so raising is standard for value, initiative, and fold equity. Minni in BB has been aggressive but defends frequently. Raise to 3x BB (300) is a balanced size given deep stacks (my 14775, Minni 17925). Limp or fold is suboptimal; calling plays out of position poorly.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five suited. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While the hand is suited, it is quite weak to play out of position against an aggressive player like Elon, especially with Minni still to act in the Big Blind. Calling 250 more into a 450 pot doesn't offer the implied odds necessary to justify seeing a flop out of position with this holding. I will preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I'm in the big blind with 3h4c facing a button open to 300 (SB folded). The pot is 450 and I need to add 200 (my total committed will be 300). Pot odds: 200/(450+200) = ~30.8% equity required. Versus a typical button opening range (Elon opens a lot in this game), this hand has roughly that ballpark equity heads-up, so defending the blind is justified. I'm out of position and the hand is very weak, so I will call and see a flop rather than raise — plan to fold to heavy aggression on dry boards and continue only with any pair, two pair/trips, or strong draws. Calling keeps the pot controlled and avoids getting 3-bet with junk.

I defended the big blind preflop with 3h4c to see a flop given the pot odds, but the flop Kd Tc 2s leaves me with nothing — no pair, no flush draw and only a remote two‑card straight possibility. Elon checked to me, so betting as an out‑of‑position bluff is high variance and likely to be called by better hands (Kx, Tx, pocket pairs, A‑x) or check‑raised. With a 17.7k stack and a 650 pot, the lowest EV play is to bloat the pot with air. I’ll check and realize equity for free; fold to any meaningful bet on the flop (only consider very light calls to tiny probe bets, but generally give up). If Elon checks back I can re‑evaluate on the turn for a potential bluff on very favorable runouts.

3-handed button vs BB after c-bet pre and her call. Flop Kd Tc 2s is a dry king-high rainbow board with minimal draws (backdoor flush possibilities only). My QsJd is pure air but blocks strong broadway hands like KQ, KJ, QT that she might defend with, making it a good bluff candidate. She checked, which is weak, and history shows Minni defends wide but often checks weak hands post-flop. Deep stacks (14k+ effective), position allows me to c-bet for thin value from worse overcards/Ax or bluff to take it down ~2/3 time. Betting 1/3 pot (225) maintains balance, gets value from weaker hands, and has fold equity. Checking risks giving free cards and losing initiative on a board where I have range advantage.

I defended preflop to see a flop, but K♦ T♣ 2♠ gives me nothing — no pair, no real draw. Elon’s bet 225 into a 875 pot is ~20.5% pot odds, but my hand (3♥4♣) only has ~6 immediate outs to pair (≈24% to hit by the river) and almost no meaningful redraws; even when I hit a pair I’m often behind Kx/Tx or pocket pairs. I’m out of position, have a healthy stack (17.7k), and raising/bluffing OOP with complete air is high-variance and low EV. Fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o), which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack of over 70 big blinds, though I am the shortest at the table. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early.

Fold. 4♥10♣ is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and I’m in the small blind (out of position) facing a button open to 300 with the big blind yet to act. Calling 250 to see a flop would require ~36% equity to break even (250 into a 700 pot) and often leaves me in multi-way or facing further aggression — not a spot I want to invest in with this hand. A raise is unreasonable with such garbage. Preserve my 17k+ stack and wait for a better, positionally favorable hand.

Pre-flop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 after SB folds. Pot is 450, to call 200 for ~31% pot odds. My hand is 98o, which is offsuit and marginal at best. It has some straight potential but poor playability out of position post-flop. Flash on button has a wide raising range, but 98o is not strong enough to defend profitably OOP against this range. Standard play is to fold weak offsuit broadways/connectors like this from BB vs button open. Preserve stack for better spots with 150 hands remaining.

6♠3♣ is one of the weakest offsuit hands. Calling 100 into a 150 pot is tempting by pot odds, but multiway and versus these opponents the hand is frequently dominated and will put me in tough post‑flop spots. A min‑raise to try to steal is thin — Elon has shown aggression and can easily 3‑bet or call, which I'd rather avoid with this holding. With a healthy stack and plenty of hands left, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold preflop.

Preflop in the small blind with Minni folding from the button, leaving heads-up vs Flash in the big blind. 4c9d is a marginal offsuit hand, but the pot odds are excellent: risking 50 more to win the 150 already in the pot (effective odds ~3:1). 49o has ~35-40% equity against a typical BB defending range and some post-flop playability (can flop pairs, gutshots, or backdoors). Stacks are deep enough (effective ~7k), and folding dead money from the blinds unopened is suboptimal. I'll complete and play post-flop cautiously OOP, check-folding weak boards and looking for bluffs or value if improved. Raising with this hand is too thin; folding wastes EV.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-2 offsuit, which is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Elon, the Small Blind, has limped in. Since there is no additional cost to see the flop and my hand has zero value as a raise, I will check to see if I can catch an improbable flop for free.

Flop of 5d-7c-8s is highly coordinated with straight draws (4-6, 6-9, 9-T), two-tone potential, and pairs possible. Heads-up OOP with 4c9d: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot to 6 is too thin at ~4% equity), overcard 9d with weak backdoors (clubs/diamonds). Equity against BB check-back range (~40-50% showdown hands/draws) is mediocre (~35%), but vulnerable to any bet. Preflop limp was correct for pot odds/dead money, but postflop OOP weak hand calls for check-fold discipline to avoid bloating pot OOP or spewing chips on bluffs with poor playability. BB likely checks back weak/marginal and bets stronger/draws, allowing free turn card or easy fold. No value/bet incentive, bluff sizing risky vs shorter stack (7.2k). Check preserves stack, realizes equity passively if checked behind.

I have middle pair (sevens) with a deuce kicker on a coordinated 5-7-8 board. In a limped pot, middle pair is a reasonable hand, but the board texture is quite connected. Since Elon checked to me, I will check behind to keep the pot small and see the turn. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'll likely only be called by better hands or draws, and checking allows me to realize my equity cheaply.

Turn board pairs the 8h, creating trips possible and completing some straight draws (6-9 now live). My 4c9d remains a complete miss: no pair, no straight (9 high only plays if paired or runner-runner), backdoor club draw irrelevant. Equity remains low (~20-30% vs BB range including pairs/draws/trips now). OOP first to act, pot 200 tiny relative to effective stacks (7.2k). Betting value impossible; bluffing poor: no strong blockers (blockerless to 8x trips, weak to 6x straights), board texture favors hero calls (pairs, floats improved), history shows checky play post-flop. Checking realizes equity passively (free river if checked back), invites villain bets (easy fold to value/bluffs), preserves stack for better spots. No fold needed as to-call 0. Consistent with preflop/flop plan: pot control OOP with marginal hand.

I currently hold a pair of sevens on a board that has paired eights, giving me two pair (8s and 7s) with a deuce kicker. This is a decent hand in a limped pot. Elon has checked for a second time, suggesting he likely doesn't have a strong eight or a high pocket pair, though he could be trapping. Since the board is somewhat connected (5-7-8) and another eight has appeared, I have showdown value but little incentive to bet and potentially face a check-raise or build a pot with a vulnerable hand. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and evaluate based on the final card.

River 4s pairs my 4c, giving me two pair (eights and fours) using board's 8s-8h-4s + my 4c, with 9d/7c/5d kickers. This is a strong made hand on a paired board with no flush or straight possible (board 4s-5d-7c-8s-8h; straights blocked by 7). Everyone has at least pair of 8s, but villain's check-check-check-check (preflop check, flop/turn/river checks) suggests weak range: missed draws (J9/T9 gutshots), low pairs (below 7), ace-high, or marginal like 65 (now pair 5<4). Trips 8x possible but unlikely given passive check-back on turn pairing board IP (value betting spot). Quads/set unlikely. My equity >70% vs this range. OOP first to act, pot 200 tiny (0.03SPR), must bet for value against worse pairs/A-high that call light. Sizing 125 (~60% pot) extracts max value without overbetting vs passive villain (7.2k stack), induces calls from Jx/9x/Tx/6x/Ax while fold equity vs air. Checking risks free showdown or villain check-raise bluff (low EV). Consistent play: cautious preflop/flop/turn OOP paid off with river brick/improver; now capitalize to grow stack in tournament.

In this limped pot between the blinds, the board has run out 5-7-8-8-4. I hold a 7, giving me two pair (8s and 7s with a 5 kicker). Both Elon and I checked the flop and the turn, which suggests that neither of us has a particularly strong hand like an 8 or a high pocket pair. On the river, Elon leads out with a bet of 125 into a 200-chip pot. While a 6 would make a straight, it is unlikely Elon would check a straight draw on both the flop and turn. My two pair is a relatively strong hand in a blind-vs-blind limped scenario. Given the pot odds (calling 125 to win 325) and the passive history of the hand, I have enough showdown value to justify a call against a potential bluff or a lower pair (like a 5 or 4).

On the button with KQo offsuit, a premium broadway hand with excellent position against the blinds. The pot is 150 with only 100 to call, but raising is standard to steal the dead money or build the pot in position. Opponents have deep stacks but have folded frequently to preflop raises in the hand history (e.g., many folds from Flash and Minni). Min-raise is 200, but sizing to 3x the BB (300) is balanced and applies pressure without overcommitting. Equity realization is high postflop in position.

I am holding 8-2 offsuit in the small blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Elon has raised from the button, and calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with almost no equity. It is a standard fold to preserve my stack.

Headed-up vs Button open to 300 — Elon’s opening range will be wide, but I’m out of position. 9♠7♥ is a marginal offsuit hand: not a great candidate for a 3-bet bluff (no suit/blockers and a small min-raise to 500 would be easy to call or shove over by an aggressive opponent). Pot is 450 and it only costs me 200 more to see the flop (total 300 committed), so defending is cheap and keeps the pot controllable with deep stacks. Plan: call to realize equity and play cautiously postflop — continue only with a pair, strong draw, or good board texture; fold to heavy pressure when completely missed.

I check. I have top pair (7s) with a middling 9-kicker on a very wet 6-7-8 flop and I’m out of position. Betting to protect is an option, but it bloats the pot and makes me vulnerable to check-raises from sets/two-pair — plus Elon opened wide from the button and will c-bet a lot of marginal hands and bluffs. Checking controls the pot and lets him barrel bluffs so I can call manageable bets. Plan: check-call small-to-medium continuation bets (roughly <= ~1/2 pot) to realize equity and fold to large raises/overbets that polarize to stronger hands; if he checks back I get to showdown cheaply and re-evaluate on the turn.

Flop is 7d-8c-6s, a connected rainbow board that hits BB calling ranges with pairs like 77/88/66, two-pair, sets, and straight draws (5x/9x). My KQo has no pair or draw but position and range advantage as BTN raiser. Minni's check suggests weakness or trapping, but history shows frequent postflop checking and folding to aggression (e.g., many folds to c-bets). C-bet for value against weaker pairs and thin value, plus high fold equity against air/missed draws. Size ~40-50% pot (650 pot) to ~300 denies equity to draws (like 59, 45) and puts pressure on floats. Deep stacks allow multi-street play if called. Folding or checking too weak, missing value from position.

Call. I have top pair (7) with a 9-kicker on a very coordinated 6-7-8 flop and I’m out of position. Elon opened wide from the button and c-bet a very small amount (~300, roughly 25–35% of the pot) that will include a lot of bluffs and marginal hands. Calling keeps the pot controllable and lets me realize equity — I have ~10 clean outs to improve (4 fives + 4 tens to complete a straight, 2 sevens to make trips) plus decent showdown value. I will fold to a large, polarizing raise or overbet, but call the cheap bet to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the card and his sizing.

Turn card is 5♦ — with my 9♠7♥ and board 7♦ 8♣ 6♠ 5♦ I have a made straight (5-6-7-8-9). Elon opened wide preflop and c-bet small on the flop, then checked the turn, so his range still contains a lot of pairs, single-pair+draw hands and bluffs; the only realistic hands that currently beat me are narrow T9 combos that make a 10-high straight. Checking back risks giving free cards and missing value from weaker hands that will call. I want to extract value and charge any remaining draws while keeping the pot controlled against the few superior combos, so I will value-bet about half the pot. If Elon check-raises very large I will re-evaluate (that sizing would strongly indicate the rare T9 or a very strong trap).

Facing a turn raise (bet) of 625 into 1875 pot from Minni after her flop check-call. My KQo has no pair, draw, or even backdoor equity on this highly connected board (7d8c6s5d), which screams straights (e.g., 45,49,59), two-pair combos (67,78,56), trips (sets improved), and strong draws that hit. KQ is pure bluff-catcher at best, but her check-call flop then leading turn sizing up suggests significant strength, not a bluff given history of folding to aggression postflop. Pot odds require ~25% equity for a call (~625 to win 2500), but vs. her polarized range, we have <10% (overpair crushed, high cards no good). Raising denies no equity and risks disaster. Deep stacks, but folding preserves stack for better spots; no need to hero here in position but out of line.

In a 3-handed game, the button opening range should be quite wide. King-Four offsuit is generally within the top 40-50% of hands, which makes it a standard opening raise on the button to apply pressure to the blinds. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to try and take the pot down pre-flop or play in position.

Facing a button raise to 250 from the small blind with Ks3d (I’ve already posted 50, need 200 to call). K3o is a poor holding here — heavily dominated by the button’s opening range (many Kx/Ax/broadways) and I’ll be out of position against both the button and the big blind if I call. The pot odds for a call (200 into 400) look OK superficially, but multiway and OOP play will kill my realized equity, and a min 3-bet to 400 would be a thin bluff that invites calls or a shove from deeper stacks. With deep effective stacks and plenty of hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is higher EV than playing this dominated, OOP hand. Fold.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 250 into a 400 pot, requiring a call of 150. My hand Qs2h is extremely weak offsuit with poor playability, dominated by any queen or better in Flash's wide button raising range. Out of position postflop, this hand has no equity realization potential against a strong continuing range. Historical play shows Flash raises aggressively from late position, often with speculative hands, but defending with trash here risks spew. Pot odds are decent but not justifying the massive EV loss OOP. Optimal play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots late in tournament.

Button with A♠4♦ — a hand worth opening for position and fold equity. With 50/100 blinds and deep stacks I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300) to isolate the blinds and take the initiative. The size is large enough to pressure Flash’s 100 BB but small enough to avoid bloating the pot vs Elon's frequent 3-bets; if Elon 3-bets a small/standard amount I can call and play postflop in position, but I’ll be prepared to fold to a very large 4-bet/shove. Raise to 300 to steal the blinds and play the hand in position.

Q♦3♠ is a trash hand offsuit with a large gap, terrible playability especially out of position in the SB. Minni's button raise suggests a wide range, but defending with this hand OOP is -EV. Pot odds are poor (calling 250 into 450+ is ~2.25:1 effective, but realization and position make it unprofitable). Fold to preserve stack for premium spots in this deep-stacked tournament phase.

I am in the Big Blind with Q2o. Minni raised to 300 from the Button. While Q2o is a weak hand, the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 450. However, out of position against a strong stack like Minni's, Q2o plays very poorly post-flop and doesn't hit many boards well. Given the tournament context and my current stack of ~75 big blinds, I prefer to fold and wait for better spots rather than defending with such a marginal hand.

3-handed on the button first to act preflop with 7s9d offsuit. Excellent position with deep stacks (140BB effective vs BB). In short-handed play, button opening range is very wide (~50%+ of hands), and 79o has good playability: can flop straights, two overs, decent high card value. Opponents from history appear somewhat passive with frequent checks and small bets, so value from position. Standard open-raise size 3x BB to 300 to build pot and take initiative. Limping weak, folding too tight.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. Elon has raised from the Button. While Button opening ranges are usually wide, Jack-Six offsuit is too weak to defend out of position, especially with a large stack like Minni behind me in the Big Blind who might squeeze or call. As the short stack, I should look for better spots to commit chips than a marginal hand like this.

I have the best possible preflop hand: pocket Aces (As Ah). Elon opened to 300 from the button and Flash folded, so this is a great spot to 3‑bet for value and isolate him heads‑up. Stacks are deep (18325 vs 13750), so I don’t want to shove — I want a standard, value‑oriented 3‑bet that prices out speculative callers (suited connectors, small pairs) while keeping strong hands and broadways in. A raise to 1200 (about 4x the open) achieves that balance. I’ll re‑evaluate if Elon 4‑bets, but plan to continue for value on most flops.

Facing a 3-bet to 1200 from Minni in the BB after my button open-raise to 300 with 7s9d offsuit. Position is excellent (in position postflop heads-up), stacks are deep (~137BB), and pot odds are decent (calling 900 into 1550 pot for ~1.7:1 direct, better implied). However, 79o is a marginal speculative hand with poor equity vs a typical BB 3-bet range (QQ+, AKo/s, maybe JJ/TT, some suited broadways/Ax, polarized with bluffs like A5s/KJs). It flops playable hands like gutshots, open-enders, or flush draws occasionally but struggles vs strong pairs/sets/overpairs due to reverse implied odds and domination risks (e.g., vs 98s/JTs). History shows Minni aggressive preflop (frequent raises, some 3-bets/calls deep), but not clearly bluff-heavy; folding weak opens to 3-bets is standard to avoid tough postflop spots OOP for them but still exploit position selectively with better hands (e.g., suited connectors like 89s, live cards). Shoving/4-betting not viable without blockers; calling risks multi-street investment with trash. Discipline: fold to preserve stack for superior edges in 3-handed deepstack play.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. While in a three-handed game the button range is wide, T7o is at the bottom of it. Given my current stack size of approximately 75 big blinds and the fact that I am the shortest stack at the table, I should be slightly more selective. Minni (the big stack) has been showing aggression recently, including a 3-bet in the previous hand. I'll choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Heads-up, Q♦J♦ is a strong, playable hand that benefits from taking the initiative. From the small blind I’ll open-raise to isolate the big blind, gain fold equity, and get value from worse broadways and suited connectors. I choose 300 (3x BB) — a standard sizing that narrows Elon’s defending range while leaving plenty of stack behind to play postflop. If Elon 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing/reads, but the default EV-maximizing play here is to raise.

Facing a raise to 300 from Minni in the SB after BTN fold, this looks like a steal attempt with a wide range. My 5d4s is a speculative suited connector with excellent playability, straight and flush potential. Deep stacks (13k effective vs her 18k) provide great implied odds if I hit. Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 into 400 (3:1). OOP but standard defend wide in BB vs SB open. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks premium with blockers but calling maximizes equity realization vs her likely capped range.

I was the preflop raiser and this flop (Kc 9c As) is two big overcards to my QJ plus a two-club texture. My hand has only one-card outs to the Broadway straight (four Tens) and no diamond backdoor, so I’m not trying to see a free turn — I want to leverage my initiative and try to fold out worse broadways and Kx/KQ-type hands while charging club draws. A standard half-pot c-bet keeps the pressure on Elon’s wide defending range, preserves fold equity, and lets me keep the pot manageable if he calls. Plan: bet ~50% pot now; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (bet big if I hit a Ten, play cautiously if a club/overcard arrives); if he raises big I’ll respect that strength and fold rather than bloating the pot with a drawing QJ.

54s completely misses the AcKc9c flop: no pair, no flush draw (spade and diamond don't connect to clubs or board spade), no straight draw (gutshot unlikely, needs specific 2-3-6 but board high cards block). Pre-flop speculative call was fine deep-stacked for implied odds, but flop is a total whiff against SB continuation range which includes Ax, Kx, sets, draws. Pot odds 300 into 900 (3.3:1) but zero equity realization OOP; calling commits to tough turns/rivers with air while her range hits often. Raising as bluff possible but her stack 18k allows her to continue wide; better to fold and preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 40 hands left. Standard discipline.

Button with 3♣5♣ — a marginal but suited, positionally-playable hand. I want to take initiative and apply pressure to the blinds rather than limp into a multiway pot. A standard 3x open (to 300 total) gives good fold equity vs the SB/BB and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet from Elon (SB); if only one caller (likely the BB), play postflop in position — c‑bet small on dry boards, proceed cautiously on coordinated/draw-heavy boards and realize our backdoor club/straight equity. Raising here leverages position and table tendencies without overcommitting with a weak hand.

A♥6♠ is an offsuit ace-high hand with poor post-flop playability out of position against a button raise from Minni, who has shown aggression in prior hands. Calling 250 into a 450 pot OOP risks difficult decisions on unfavorable boards, and raising with this hand lacks equity and fold equity given stack depths and opponent tendencies. Folding preserves our stack for better spots in this tournament phase with 38 hands remaining, prioritizing long-term EV.

I am in the big blind with 8-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni raised from the button to 300. Given the trashy nature of my hand and the fact that I'd be out of position for the rest of the hand against the chip leader, there is no reason to defend. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q9o is a playable hand on the button with excellent position. The pot is 150, and calling 100 offers great pot odds (pot will be 250, needing ~29% equity, which Q9o has against typical calling ranges). From history, opponents like Minni and Flash defend wide pre-flop but check down marginal hands post-flop often, allowing me to realize equity well in position. Raising risks getting 3-bet by aggressive Minni (BB), but calling keeps the pot small and lets me play post-flop optimally. No need to fold such a good spot. Will call and proceed cautiously post-flop if needed.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o. Elon has limped from the Button for 100. The pot is currently 250 and it only costs 50 more to call and see a flop. While Q7o is a mediocre hand, the pot odds are 5:1 (50 to win 250), which makes it a mathematically justifiable call in a three-handed game to see if I hit a piece of the board, especially since Elon's limp often indicates a wider, weaker range than a raise.

Holding 4♣3♥ — a weak, unsuited small connector. Both opponents have limped, creating a multiway pot out of position; raising to 200 would build a pot I don’t want with junk and will likely get called or met with aggression from Elon. I’ve already posted the big blind, so I’ll check and see a cheap flop. Plan: only continue with a meaningful improvement (pair or strong draw); fold to significant flop aggression to protect my deep stack.

I have flopped top two pair (Queens and Sevens) on a board of Qc-7h-8h. This is a very strong hand, but the board is quite 'wet' with both straight draws (69, J10) and a flush draw (hearts) available. Since the pot is relatively small (300) and I want to build value while protecting against these draws, I will lead out with a bet of approximately two-thirds of the pot.

Board is Qc 7h 8h; I hold 4♣3♥ — no pair, only a one-heart backdoor and runner-runner straight outs. Flash’s flop raise to 200 into a limp pot is a sign of real strength (top pair, a heart draw or a value/semibluff), and Elon still acts after me which can put me in a tough OOP decision. Pot is 500; calling 200 gives ~28.6% pot odds, but my realistic equity (runner-runner flush ≈4%, occasional pair-outs) is far below that. I don’t want to build a pot with junk and risk facing further aggression, so I fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot.

Facing a flop bet of 200 into approximately 250 pre-flop pot (now 500 with bet), with top pair queens (Qd pairing Qc) and a 9 kicker on a Q-8-7 rainbow-ish board with two hearts. Board is drawy (heart flush draws, straight draws like 9T, J9, 56). Flash limped from SB pre-flop (loose range likely including draws, weaker Qx, lower pairs), now donk-betting into me post-flop. Excellent pot odds: calling 200 to win 500 (pot becomes 900, ~22% equity needed), and my hand has strong equity vs their range (beats draws, Ax high, lower pairs, weaker Qx like QJ/QT possible but 9 kicker not terrible). Position on button allows controlling pot and realizing equity well OOP opponent may check turn. Deep stacks (133BB effective vs Flash's ~70BB), so value thin value-bets/calls post-flop viable. Raising risks vs possible better Qx/sets/draws, folding top pair too tight. Call, see turn, bet if check or if improves.

I have improved to a full house (Sevens full of Queens) on the turn. This is an extremely strong hand, nearly the nuts in this situation given the pre-flop action. Since I led out on the flop and Elon called, he likely has a Queen, a pair of eights, or a straight/flush draw. I want to continue extracting value while the board still looks somewhat 'wet' to his draws. A bet of 450 into the 700 pot is a standard value-building size that keeps his range wide and prices in draws or weaker made hands.

Facing a second barrel of 450 into 1150 pot (~40% pot) on a paired turn board (Qc 7h 8h 7s), holding top pair queens with 9 kicker (Qd 9h). Flash limped SB pre-flop (wide range: suited connectors, Ax suited, pocket pairs, weaker Qx), donk-bet flop (loose value/draws), now continues after 7 pairs the board. This pairs some straight draws (e.g., 56 becomes two pair), but kills others and doesn't complete hearts. My hand blocks Q9 (possible str8), has showdown value vs bluffs/missed draws (JTs, flush draws, Ax), and beats weaker Qx/pairs. Pot odds excellent: 450 to win 1600 (~28% equity needed), and range equity ~40%+ (beats air, draws, lower pairs). Position allows free river control; raising risks vs trips (77), two pair (87/Q8/Q7), better Qx; folding top pair OOP aggressor too tight given history of check-downs. Deep effective stacks (66BB), call realizes equity optimally, value bet rivers if checks.

I have a full house, Sevens full of Queens, which is a near-nut hand on this board. The river 5d is a blank that doesn't complete the obvious flush or straight draws. Elon has called bets on both the flop and turn, which strongly suggests he has a hand with showdown value, most likely a Queen (Top Pair) or a pair of Eights. Since he is unlikely to bet if I check, I must bet for value. A bet of 1000 into the 1600 pot (roughly 63% pot) is a strong value size that a Queen should find difficult to fold, while still appearing like a bet that could be trying to protect against missed draws.

River decision: Holding top pair Q with 9 kicker (Qd9h) on Qc7h8h7s5d board. Pot ~1600 pre-river bet, Flash (SB limper, wide range: suited connectors, weak Ax, pairs, Qx) donk-bets flop 200 (polarized: draws like hearts/JT OESD, weak Qx/pairs), I call OOP aggressor. Turn 7s pairs board (improves 7x trips, 78/Q7/Q8/87 two pair), Flash bets 450 (~30% pot), I call given pot odds/SDV. River 5d blank (pairs nothing new, misses most remaining draws). Flash bets 1000 (~39% pot into 2600 total), pot odds 1000 to win ~3600 (~28% equity needed). Vs value (trips 7x/87, two pair Q7/Q8/75/78, better QJ/QK/QT): poor equity; vs bluffs (missed Ahh/Khh/JTh/9Ts/T9s/A high): crushes. SB OOP triple-barreler likely polarized, history shows aggressive betting but frequent check-downs/marginal plays. Q9 blocks Q9s (str?), beats weaker Qx (Q8-QT), all bluffs/Ax/pairs. Equity ~35-45% vs range, pot odds covered. Position for free SD if villain bets air; stacks deep (my 12650>Flash 5625 post-bet). Call maximizes EV, realizes equity; fold too tight pre river completion; raise thin vs nuts, commits too much with SDV hand. Call 1000.

I am on the button with 6h4h, a speculative suited connector. The pot is currently 150, and it costs 100 to call since the small blind and big blind are already posted. Given my stack size of 9225 and the potential for a high-value hand if I hit the flop (straights or flushes), I will raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate here to test the blinds' strength.

I’m in the small blind with 7♥8♣ (offsuit) facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call. 7-8 offsuit is a marginal, low-equity hand OOP; against a button opening range and with the big blind yet to act I’m likely to be multi‑way or out of position heads-up, both of which reduce my realized equity. Calling 250 into 450 gives only ~1.8:1 pot odds (~36% equity needed) — 7-8o doesn’t have that vs a standard open, especially multi-way. A light 3-bet would be thin and unnecessarily bloats the pot out of position. With healthy stack sizes I’d rather preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Pocket Jacks is a premium hand, top of my range for 3-betting. Flash's button raise is likely wide, including many speculative hands. With Minni folding SB, this isolates to heads-up vs Flash. Deep stacks (mine 11.5k, Flash 8.9k), so 3-bet sizing to 900 builds the pot while denying equity to worse hands. Folding or calling is too passive; JJ performs well against button ranges postflop but better when we charge draws and medium strength.

I opened from the button with 6h4h, a speculative hand that plays well in position but is vulnerable to aggression. Elon has responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the big blind. Calling 600 more (roughly 7% of my stack) with such a low suited connector is marginal, even with positional advantage. Given that 4h6h is at the very bottom of my opening range and faces significant reverse implied odds against a likely range of high pairs or strong overcards, I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable spot.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed game — great position and an opportunity to steal the blinds. J♣4♠ is weak, but in 3-way play the button should open a wide range to pick up dead money; a small, standard min-raise keeps the initiative and gives fold equity versus Flash and Elon. If Elon responds with a big 3‑bet I’ll fold (hand plays poorly out of position vs a reraise); if called I’ll use position to continue on favourable flops and fold to heavy resistance on dry boards. The risk (200 into a 150 pot) is small relative to my 19k stack and the potential reward justifies a standard button steal.

Preflop in SB facing a min-raise to 200 (2BB) from Button by Minni, who frequently raises small from late position. My 4h5d is a suited connector with excellent playability, flush and straight potential. Pot odds are favorable: calling additional 150 into 350 (23% equity needed), and 45s has ~38% equity vs a typical loose BTN opening range (~40-50%). Deep stacks (120BB effective) provide strong implied odds for when we hit. BB (Flash) acts behind but folding too tight in SB vs BTN steals is exploitable over many hands. Plan to call and play postflop cautiously OOP, folding to aggression if miss but stacking off if hit big.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 6d. Minni raised to 200 from the button, and Elon called from the Small Blind. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 600. These are excellent pot odds (6:1), and even with a weak hand like 96o, defending the big blind here is mathematically sound as I only need about 14% equity to break even, and I close the pre-flop action.

Preflop call with 45s was speculative for multiway potential and playability, but the flop Ks Tc Qc completely misses my hand – no pair, no straight draw (45 doesn't connect with KQT), no flush draw. Board is highly coordinated with broadway straight draws (J9), two clubs for flush draw, and top pair potential. OOP as SB, betting into two opponents (Minni as preflop raiser likely has some KQJTxx strength or draw, Flash called wide) is disastrous for a pure bluff with no equity. Pot 600, checking allows control and folding to aggression, which is GTO standard for missed speculative hands multiway OOP. No fold equity justifies a bluff here given ranges and deep stacks.

I have completely missed this flop with 9-high on a board of King-Queen-Ten. This board is extremely coordinated and very likely to have connected with at least one, if not both, of my opponents' ranges. There is no reason to invest any more chips into this pot given I have no pair and no meaningful draw. I will check and expect to fold to any bet from Minni.

Check. This is a 3‑way pot and both opponents checked the flop — my preflop raise left me in position, but J♣4♠ is a drawing hand, not a made hand. I have an open‑ended straight draw (9 or A completes) — ~8 outs — and only a very remote backdoor club possibility, so my equity is decent but not dominant. In multiway spots my continuation‑bet frequency should be lower because my fold equity is limited and I’ll often be called by K/Q/T, club draws, or pairs. The pot is small relative to our stacks, so I prefer to pot‑control and realize my equity for free. Plan: check and evaluate the turn — if a 9/A/club hits I can turn aggression or raise; if I miss and face heavy action I’ll fold to avoid committing chips with marginal equity, but I’ll call small/medium bets to try to hit.

Turn board brings the 5s, pairing my 5d for bottom pair with a terrible 4h kicker. Board is extremely draw-heavy (club flush draw, spade flush draw now with Ks5s, straight draws like J9, and pairs/overpairs). Multiway pot OOP as first to act after all checks on flop, which widened ranges significantly—villains likely have wide checking ranges including missed draws, weak pairs, or floats. Betting thin value/protection risks getting called by all better hands (Kx, Qx, Tx, sets, straights) and raised off equity by strong draws or monsters. Pot control is key with a marginal hand; checking allows a free river card or cheap showdown if checked through, while folding to significant aggression. Deep stacks but no strong incentive to bloat pot here. GTO-ish play for speculative hand that semi-hit.

The turn card is the 5 of spades. The board (Ks Tc Qc 5s) remains very dangerous, and I still have no pair and no draw. Since Elon checked again and the pot is still small, there is no reason to attempt a bluff into two opponents on such a coordinated board. I will check and plan to fold to any subsequent betting action.

Check. With J♣4♠ on K♠ T♣ Q♣ 5♠ I only have A or 9 to make a straight (8 outs → 8/46 ≈ 17.4%) and the backdoor club flush is dead after the spade on the turn. This is a three‑way pot and both opponents checked the flop and turn, so my fold equity is very low — a bet will often be called by any K/Q/T, club draw, or pair, converting my marginal draw into a money‑losing call. The pot is small (600) relative to our deep stacks, so pot‑control and seeing the river for free is the high‑EV play. Plan: check to the river; if I hit (A/9) I will value bet or raise depending on sizing, and if an opponent bets the river I will call small/medium bets to realize equity but fold to large river aggression without improvement.

River Ace of Spades completes the spade flush draw (board now has Ks, 5s, As) and pairs the top effectively while enabling broadway straights with JT. My pair of 5s (5d with board 5s) with a worthless 4h kicker is crushingly weak multiway OOP after three checks on both flop and turn, which polarized ranges to strong made hands (top pairs, sets, straights) or total air/draws that missed. Pot of 600 with deep stacks (mine 11800, opponents deeper/shallower); value betting gets called/raised by better, pure bluff lacks fold equity first-in multiway against wide checking ranges. Checking allows free showdown if checked through (plausible with missed draws/weak pairs) or cheap pot control/fold to bets. GTO exploits passive history; no upside to bloating pot with marginal holding on scary runout.

The board is Ks-Tc-Qc-5s-As. I have 9-high with no pair, no straight, and no flush. While the board is very coordinated and I could potentially represent a straight (any Jack) or a flush by betting, the presence of two opponents makes a bluff risky. Everyone has checked through the flop and turn, and Elon has checked the river. Since Minni, the pre-flop raiser, is still to act behind me, and my 9-high has zero showdown value, I will check. If Minni checks as well, the hand ends. If she bets, I will fold as I have nothing.

The river A completes the Broadway: A-K-Q-J-T — I have the nut straight. Both opponents checked the whole hand, which either shows weakness or a slowplay; in any case many of their hands are likely single-pair/weak two-pair or missed draws. This is a clear value-betting spot in a 3‑way pot. I’ll size modestly to keep worse hands and one-pair A/K/Q/T hands in the pot — half the pot (~300 into 600) extracts value without folding out too many callers. If I face a raise I can comfortably call (or shove) because almost nothing beats this hand except a straight/royal flush, which is vanishingly unlikely here.

River board Ks Tc Qc 5s As is terrifying: completed club and spade flushes, broadway straight possibilities (e.g., J9, AT), top pair or better value heavy. Holding bottom pair 5s with 4 kicker OOP multiway after all checks on flop/turn, which capped our range low. Minni (preflop raiser on BTN) checks twice then donk bets 300 (half-pot) into 600—polarized range favoring thin value (Kx, Qx, Tx, Ace-high), completed draws (spade/club flushes), or rare bluffs with missed strong draws. Pair of 5s loses to all value, draws that hit, and chops some worse A-high at best. Pot odds ~4:1 require ~20% equity, but villain's bettor range crushes us (~10-15% equity tops vs polarized range). Raising gets no fold equity with weak blocker and stacks too deep for semi-bluff; calling builds pot OOP facing potential Flash squeeze and induces worse calls from BB air. History shows Minni values rivers thin post-passive streets. Standard fold for pot control/speculative hand that didn't improve enough.

The board is extremely dangerous with a possible straight (any Jack) and a possible spade flush. I have 9-high with no showdown value at all. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, checked the flop and turn but has now bet half the pot on the river. While her check-check-bet line could be a bluff or a thin value bet, I have nothing to beat even a pure bluff. Attempting a raise here to represent the flush or straight is too risky given Minni's large stack and the fact that I am in the middle of the pack with 35 hands remaining. I will fold and preserve my chips.

3-handed on the button with QcJc suited connectors – a premium hand in this spot. Excellent playability multi-way or heads-up, strong blockers, and position. The pot has 150 (blinds posted), to call 100. Assuming the SB has limped or folded and BB checked or standard blinds, this is a classic spot to raise for value/steal. Raising ~3x the BB isolates, builds the pot with a strong hand, and denies equity to weaker hands. Standard size around 300 given the dynamics and history of raises at 200-300. Opponents have shown folding tendencies to button aggression in similar spots. With deep effective stacks but tournament pressure at hand 116/150, apply pressure to maximize stack.

Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand in a three-handed game. Facing a button raise from Elon, a 3-bet is a standard play to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately. Calling would put me in a difficult spot out of position post-flop. I'll raise to 1,000 to apply pressure and define my hand.

I’m in the big blind with pocket fives (5c5h) facing a small‑blind 3‑bet to 1,000 after the button opened to 300. Pot is 1,400 and I must add 900 (i.e. commit a total of 1,000) to call. With deep stacks (~19k) and good implied odds, 5s have enough equity vs a mixed SB 3‑bet range and are worth seeing a flop for set‑mining and postflop play. A min‑4bet to 1,700 is too small to realistically fold out better hands and a larger 4‑bet overcommits a medium pair; folding concedes too much equity. Plan: call to see the flop, extract value if I hit or proceed cautiously on dry overcard boards (fold to heavy aggression).

Preflop on the button with QcJc, a strong suited broadway connector with excellent playability, position, and blockers to strong hands. I opened to 300 standard. Facing a 3-bet from Flash (SB, aggressive history, 77bb effective) to 1000 and a flat-call from Minni (BB, calling station tendencies from history, very deep 184bb). Pot is 2300, calling 700 (3.3:1 direct, multiway value). Folding is too tight as this hand dominates much of their calling/3betting ranges and plays well postflop IP multiway or heads-up. Raising risks bloating pot OOP for them or jamming overstacked vs Flash; better to realize equity cheaply OOP for Flash/Minni, control pot size, and leverage position on all streets. Deep stacks (115bb) favor speculative hands like this calling and seeing flops. History shows opponents check/fold or bet small postflop often, allowing us to outplay. Call keeps pot manageable, induces mistakes.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on an Ace-high board with straight and flush draws. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, a continuation bet is standard to extract value from weaker Aces (A8s-A4s), pocket pairs (44-KK), and various draws (club flush draws or gutshots). A bet of 1,200 (40% of the pot) is large enough to charge draws while allowing me to control the size of the pot in case I am facing a stronger Ace or a set.

Flop: 3♣ A♣ 2♠. I’m on 5♣5♥ — I flatted the SB 3‑bet preflop planning to set‑mine or fold to heavy aggression on an A‑high dry board. Flash (the 3‑bettor) is taking the lead on the flop (raising/betting 1,200 into a 4,200 pot). Calling 1,200 here requires roughly 18% equity (1200 / (4200+1200) ≈ 18.2%) to break even. My realistic ways to win by showdown are small: two remaining fives to make a set by river ≈ 8.4%, backdoor club flush needs both turn+river clubs ≈ 4.2% (these are essentially disjoint here) — combined ≈ 12.6% before accounting for villain ranges and multiple opponents. Against a preflop 3‑better who barrels the flop, the range is heavily weighted to Ax, sets or strong club combos that dominate my hand. Raising as a bluff is too thin and folding preserves a large, healthy stack for better, more profitable set‑mining spots. Conclusion: fold this flop to the aggression.

Heads-up on the flop vs Flash after Minni folded. Board: Ac 3c 2s – extremely drawy with two clubs and low cards. My Qc Jc gives me the nut flush draw (9 outs to the nuts), overcards for gutshot straight draw potential (backdoor really), and excellent playability in position. Preflop: Flash 3-bet my button open (aggressive SB history), Minni flatted, I flatted keeping pot multiway-friendly. Flash's flop bet/raise sizing to 1200 into 4200 pot (~28% pot) reps strength (sets like AA/A3/A2 unlikely but possible; top pair AK/AQ/AJ; bluffs/dry value). However, my equity is massive: ~35-45% vs range (9 flush outs + overs + fold equity). Pot odds: calling 1200 into 4200 (3.5:1 direct) + huge implied odds vs Flash's 6525 stack (my 10800). Raising risks facing 4bet shove from AA/top pair or folding equity only vs air while bloating pot OOP for future; better to call, realize equity cheaply IP, control pot, and take it away on turn if check or outdraw. History shows Flash bets aggressively but checks/folds streets; position allows floating/stealing turn. Tournament stage (116/150) favors realizing massive equity with deep effective stacks over spew-raising. Call maximizes EV long-term stack growth.

On the turn 6h, which is largely a brick, I am in a difficult position. I have top pair, but my kicker (Nine) is weak. Elon called both a pre-flop 3-bet and a continuation bet on an Ace-high board, which suggests he likely has an Ace, a set (33, 22), or a strong draw (like a club flush draw). If I bet again, I am primarily getting called by hands that beat me (AJ, AQ, AK, or two pair/sets) and potentially some draws. By checking, I can control the size of the pot, see how Elon reacts, and potentially induce a bluff from his missed draws or a smaller bet from a weaker Ace. My stack is roughly 1.2x the pot, so betting again commits me to the hand without knowing if I'm ahead.

Heads-up vs Flash on turn after Minni folded preflop call. Board: 3c Ac 2s 6h – highly drawy with Ac 3c giving flush draws, low cards for straights. Holding Qc Jc: nut flush draw (9 outs ~18-20% equity to river), suited broadway blockers, overcards. Preflop: Button open 300, Flash aggressive SB 3bet 1000, Minni flat, I flat multiway IP realizing equity. Flop: Flash donk bets 1200 into 2300 (~50% pot, reps strength but polarized given board), I called leveraging massive equity/IP/implied odds vs Flash's 77bb stack then. Pot now 5400, Flash checks turn 6h (brick). Flash's check after flop bet likely weakness: missed draws (non-nut flush draws like KcQd, suited connectors <Q), air bluffs that gave up, or trapping monster (sets AA/A3/A2 unlikely ~2-3% range, AJ/AQ top pair possible but check rare). History: Flash aggressive preflop/flop (raises, bets), but often checks turns (Hands 51,54,56, etc.), folds to aggression. My equity strong vs value (~35% vs AA/Ax), ahead vs bluffs/draws. Position: Bet semi-bluff/value to charge worse draws/Ax weak, fold out trash, build pot for river value/shove if hit, deny equity. Pot odds favor betting: SPR low ~1.2 effective (6525/5400). Size 2200 (~40% pot) denies equity cheaply, gets calls from worse, reps strength post-oop check. Checking risks free river card giving Flash outs/draws; betting maximizes EV, consistent with realizing equity IP. Tournament 116/150, apply pressure to grow stack vs passive check.

Elon's turn bet of 2200 into a pot of 5400 is approximately 40% of the pot, offering me pot odds of roughly 4.5:1. Having flopped top pair, I am ahead of his potential flush draws, straight draws, and lower Aces like A8-A4. While I am vulnerable to higher kickers or slow-played sets, my turn check may have induced a bet from his semi-bluffs or weaker holdings trying to take the pot down. Given the 3-handed context and the favorable price, I must call to see the river and re-evaluate.

On the river Jack of Diamonds, the board is relatively static. My Top Pair (Aces with a Nine kicker) is a strong bluff-catcher but likely behind Elon's value range if he continues to bet large, considering he called a 3-bet pre-flop and a flop continuation bet. However, many missed club draws (like KcQc, KcJc) and straight draws (like 45) are in his range. Checking is the most prudent play here; it allows Elon to check back with weaker Aces that I beat or to fire a final bluff with his missed draws. If he bets, I will have to decide based on the sizing, but given my stack size relative to the pot, I am likely committed to calling any reasonable bet to see the showdown in this three-handed high-stakes dynamic.

River board: 3c Ac 2s 6h Jd. Holding Jc Qc: pairs the Jd for top pair Jacks (second pair behind Aces) with Queen kicker, strong showdown value and blocks AJ. Heads-up vs Flash (aggressive SB preflop/flop, check-called turn after flop donk on draw-heavy board). Flash's range polarized: Ax (AK/AQ/AJ/Ax suited/club draws), sets (AA/A3/A2 rare), missed nut flush draws or bluffs that check-called turn fearing strength. My turn bet 2200 into ~5400 after Flash checked turn represented strength, extracting from worse Ax/draws. Flash's river check-back after calling suggests caution/weakness (weak Ax, missed draws, air), unlikely monster trap given history of folding to aggression (e.g., Hands 51,65,95). Equity: Beats ~60-70% of range (Ax weak, draws, bluffs), loses to AA/AJ/two pair rare. Pot 9800, effective stacks shallow (my 7400, Flash 4325, SPR ~0.9). Value bet thin to extract max value from worse Ax/draws that check-call; checking risks free showdown where he can bluff-catch or win unimproved. Size 3500 (~35% pot) polarizes our line (semi-bluff rep earlier), gets calls/folds correctly, < all-in to induce calls from medium strength while shoving protection unnecessary vs range. Tournament late (116/150), grow stack by pressuring passive lines. High EV spot confirmed by history (Flash calls light but folds rivers often post-check).

I have Top Pair (Aces) with a Nine kicker. On the river Jack of Diamonds, the board remains relatively safe from completed flushes, though some straight draws (4-5) and specific two-pair combinations (AJ, A6, A3, A2) or sets (33, 22) beat me. However, the pot odds are exceptional; I need to call 3,500 to win a total pot of 16,800, giving me roughly 4.8:1 odds. In a three-handed game, Elon's range includes many missed club draws and straight draws that he might be turning into a bluff after I checked the turn and river. Given the strength of my hand relative to his likely range and the price I am getting, folding is not an option.

On the button in a 3-handed game, I have a significant positional advantage. King-four offsuit is a standard opening hand in this position to put pressure on the blinds and pick up the pot pre-flop. While Elon is short-stacked at 3,800 chips, raising to 300 allows me to maintain aggression and leverage my position. If Elon shoves, I can comfortably fold given the price, but against most of their defending ranges, I am happy to play the pot in position or take it down now.

I’m in the small blind with 3♦ J♣ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450). This is a very weak, unsuited rag that will be dominated by a large portion of the button’s opening range and has poor post‑flop playability. Calling 250 to see a flop puts me out of position and invites difficult decisions (and possible isolation/shoves) from the short‑stacked big blind behind. A 3‑bet bluff here is thin — I don’t have the blockers or hand quality to realize equity or get reliable fold equity. With a healthy 18.4k stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Flash raised from the button, which is a wide range given position and history of frequent late-position aggression (e.g., many unopened button raises leading to folds or light action). My 9d5d is suited connectors with straight/flush potential and good playability postflop. Pot odds are excellent: 200 to call into 450 (pot becomes 650), over 3:1, plus strong implied odds against a deep-stacked opponent if I hit. With 38BB effective stack, defending wide in BB vs BTN is standard to realize equity and avoid being too tight. Folding weak but playable hands exploits our position disadvantage; calling balances range. No raise as hand lacks strength for 3-bet value/bluff here.

Preflop call was standard with suited connectors vs button open for playability and implied odds. Flop 6h7c2h gives me complete air: no pair, no flush draw (diamonds), weak gutshot straight draw at best (8 for 5-6-7-8-9, but unlikely), and backdoor potential only. OOP in BB vs aggressive BTN raiser who c-bets wide (history shows frequent button aggression and continuation bets). Pot 650, my stack ~36BB (3600), no fold equity strong enough to justify leading into wide c-bet range. Checking realizes equity if I improve, controls pot, and invites a bluff I can sometimes hero-call or check-raise bluff-catch. Betting risks getting check-raised off equity or called by better; fold to aggression post-check preserves stack for better spots. Long-term EV: check-fold/call selectively maximizes stack.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have positional advantage. The flop (6h-7c-2h) is relatively low, and while it can hit a Big Blind defending range, I should maintain my initiative with a continuation bet. A small bet of 250 into the 650 pot is a standard play to fold out Elon's air (like high-card hands that missed) while keeping the pot manageable. If Elon raises or shoves, his short stack indicates he is likely committed with a pair or strong draw, and I can comfortably fold my King-high.

Facing a 250 bet into 900 pot on 6h7c2h flop after checking OOP with 9d5d (gutshot straight draw to 8, ~8% equity, backdoor diamond flush). Flash's button c-bet range is wide (history of frequent button aggression, light steals, continuation bets). Pot odds ~3.6:1 but reverse implied odds poor OOP vs deep-stacked (17k) aggressive opponent; calling bloats pot, leaves me drawing thin turns/rivers OOP with minimal showdown value. No fold equity for raise with air. Folding weak hands here exploits button wideness preflop/postflop, preserves ~36BB stack (3600) for better spots in late tournament stages (hand 117/150). Realizing hand equity was via check pre-bet; now fold equity realization unnecessary.

I’m on the button with K♦7♣ facing a single raise where I must call 100 to see the flop. K7o is marginal but playable in position against a one-bettor, especially with deep effective stacks and a big blind who can be leveraged postflop. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize positional advantage, and keeps Elon (shorter 35bb stack) from turning this into a bigger isolation pot or shove where I’d be forced to commit with a mediocre hand. I’ll call and play the flop in position; I’ll fold to significant 3-bets or all-ins preflop.

QcJc is a premium suited broadway hand with excellent playability multi-way or heads-up. Button limp from Minni is often weak or speculative based on hand history patterns. As SB, completing cheaply is an option, but raising to isolate the limper, deny equity to BB, and take initiative preflop is optimal. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) builds the pot when ahead and applies pressure with deep effective stacks on opponents. My stack of 3550 allows flexibility postflop. Folding is too tight; flat risks multiway pot OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six of diamonds. Minni limped from the button, and Elon raised to 300 from the small blind. Elon is currently the short stack with only 3,300 chips. The pot currently contains 500 chips, and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 3.5 to 1, which is very favorable for a suited hand with some connectivity. While J6o would be a fold, J6s is strong enough to defend in this spot, especially since I have a significant stack advantage over Elon. I will call to see a flop and evaluate the action from there.

I’m on the button with K♦7♣ facing Elon’s open to 300 and Flash’s call. I already have 100 in, so calling the extra 200 (300 total) keeps the pot manageable and lets me use position. K7o is marginal to 3-bet with here and likely dominated by much of Elon’s raising range; raising risks isolating a ~33bb opponent who may shove. I’ll call, see the flop, and then c-bet or check/defend depending on texture — fold to significant 3-bets/all-ins preflop.

Preflop raise with QcJc was standard to isolate the button limp and defend against BB. Flop of 3h Ks 7s completely misses my range: no pair, no flush draw (clubs), no straight draw, pure queen-high bluff catcher at best.
