Multiway pot (three players) with 900 in the pot and my 3300 stack. As first to act from SB, continuation betting into two opponents on a king-high, spade-drawy board risks getting raised by value or floated by draws, and denies my equity realization. Checking preserves stack, induces bluffs from worse if checked to me later streets, allows pot control, and evaluates opponents' ranges (Minni limp-call button wide, Flash BB defend). Plan: check-fold to aggression, check-call small bets if any with backdoor potential or fold most scenarios to maximize stack long-term in tournament.

I have Jack-high with no direct connection to the flop of Kh-7s-3h. Elon, the pre-flop raiser, has checked, which could indicate a missed hand or a trap given his short stack. Minni is still to act behind me. In a multi-way pot with a very weak hand and no real equity, checking is the most prudent play. I'm not looking to bluff here against a short stack who might be pot-committed or a big stack who can easily call. I will check and see if I can get a free turn card.

I have K♦7♣ on 3♥ K♠ 7♠ — that’s two pair (Kings & Sevens), a very strong hand versus a preflop raising range. Both opponents checked, which looks like weakness or pot-control; the board has two spades so there are live flush draws I want to charge and protect against. I should bet for value and to deny free spade cards. I choose a ~2/3-pot bet (600) to extract from worse Kx/7x and charge draws while keeping the pot manageable against Elon’s short stack and Flash’s deep stack. If I face a shove or large raise, I’ll give it serious consideration — only sets often dominate me, so calling reasonable all-ins is usually correct.

Preflop raise with QcJc was standard isolation play, but flop of 3h-Ks-7s whiffs completely: queen-high with no pair, no made draw, minimal backdoor equity (clubs and broadway). Multi-way pot OOP after two checks, Minni's button donk-bet into 900-chip pot for 600 (2/3 pot) is polarized toward value (Kx, 77/33 sets, 7x) or strong spades draws, unlikely pure bluffs given limp-call sizing and history. Calling OOP commits 18% pot with dominated equity (~20-30% vs range), invites Flash squeeze or further pressure. Raising as bluff over-represents monsters while risking stack (33bb effective) against callers. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament; consistent with preflop plan to check-fold aggression on miss. Long-term EV+ to avoid speculative defense.

I have Jack-high on a board of Ks-7s-3h with no real draws or pairs. I called pre-flop with J6s and checked the flop. Elon (SB) checked, and then Minni (Button) bet 600 into a 900 pot. This is a large bet in a three-way pot. Given that I have no piece of this board and no flush or straight draws, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

3-handed on the button preflop with Qh9s, a strong open-raising hand from this position. Excellent stealing spot with fold equity against the blinds, who have shown passive tendencies in history. My 33BB stack allows aggression to build without overcommitting. Raising 3x BB to 300 applies pressure and takes initiative postflop if called. Calling is too passive; folding wastes position.

Elon has raised to 300 from the button, and he is the short stack with only 3000 chips (30 BB). Holding Jack-Trey offsuit in the small blind is a clear folding situation. This hand is too weak to defend profitably out of position against a button raise, especially with the potential for the short stack to shove or the big blind (Minni) to squeeze.

Call. Holding T♥J♦ in the big blind vs a standard 3bb button open is a defendable hand with good playability and equity against a wide opening range. Elon’s stack (~3,000 ≈30 BB) means a 3-bet will likely induce a shove or commit a lot of chips out of position; I don’t want to bloat the pot or face an all‑in decision with JTo. By calling the extra 200 (total committed 300 including my BB) I keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and can make informed postflop decisions. Plan: see the flop, continue with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on unfavorable boards.

Check. I have T♥J♦ on a dry K♠7♣2♥ flop with no meaningful draw (only remote runner‑runner outs). I was defending a wide range vs a 3bb button open — pot control OOP is the priority. Betting here is a thin bluff that will be called too often or get raised; by checking I can fold to real aggression or call small bets to realize equity. Plan: check and fold to sizable continuation bets (rough guideline: fold to bets ≥ ~1/2 pot), call very small stabs (≤ ~1/3 pot) to see a turn, and only continue aggressively if I pair or pick up clear equity on the turn.

Flop is Ks7c2h, a dry board with no flush draw complete and limited straight draws. My Qh9s has queen-high with minimal showdown value but position and initiative from preflop raise. Minni's check after calling from BB suggests weakness, consistent with her passive history (frequent checks and small calls/folds). C-betting ~60% pot (400 into 650) applies pressure, denies equity to her weak hands, and takes down the pot often. If called, I have fold equity on turn and can control pot size. Checking back risks giving free cards and losing value from folds. With 30BB stack, building pots selectively while stealing is key to growing stack in late tournament.

Board is K♠7♣2♥, I hold T♥J♦ — no pair and no realistic draw. Elon opened 3bb from the button (wide) and made a small c‑bet (400 into 1050 ≈ 38% pot). I defended preflop to realize equity, and here pot control OOP is the priority. Calling 400 here keeps the pot manageable, gives me decent pot odds to see a turn against a fairly wide c‑betting range, and lets me fold to genuine strength on a later street. Raising is thin with zero pair and would only get me in tough spots (he can shove his ~26BB stack); folding is too nitty versus a wide button range. Plan: call to see a turn, fold to large turn aggression if I miss, and only get aggressive if I pair or pick up clear equity. Call 400.

Board: K♠ 7♣ 2♥ A♣. I hold T♥ J♦ — no pair, no meaningful draw. I defended preflop vs a wide button open and called the modest flop c‑bet to keep the pot under control. The turn Ace heavily improves the button’s range (Ax, Kx, sets) and gives me almost no fold equity OOP. Betting or raising here is a thin play that will often get called or shoved by better hands; folding now would be overly nitty given my preflop defense. Best line is pot control: check and see how Elon behaves on the river. Plan: if Elon bets the river I will fold to sizable barrels (especially ≥ ~1/2 pot) and only call very small bluffs (≤ ~1/3 pot) or if I pair/improve. If he checks back, reevaluate and consider a delayed bluff on a safe river card or check‑back to showdown.

Turn brings the Ac, pairing the board's potential and completing club flush draws while improving strong Ax hands into my preflop button raising range (AK, AQ, A7s, etc.). My Qh9s is queen-high with backdoor spade flush draw at best but minimal showdown value against calling ranges; it blocks few strong hands but beats pure air/bluffs. Minni's check-call flop on Ks7c2h (dry, uncoordinated) then check suggests capped range: mid pairs (88-TT,77,22), weak Kx/draws, or Ax without top pair yet. Historical passivity (frequent checks, small calls/folds) supports weakness, but Ace hits her possible cold calls like AJo-ATo, A5s suited connectors. Pot 1450 with my 2600 stack (17BB effective vs her deep 18325); betting ~700 (half-pot) has fold equity vs trash but risks calls from improved Ax/KK-pairs/set, bloating pot out of position river. Checking realizes equity cheaply, induces her bluffs/misses river, controls pot size in late tournament (hand 119/150, 3-handed button position premium). Prefers check back to showdown or trap river bet, preserving stack for better spots while denying her free cards on weak boards.

Board K♠ 7♣ 2♥ A♣ 6♦ — I hold T♥ J♦ and missed every street. Elon opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop but then checked the turn and checked the river. That line is most consistent with pot‑control hands (Kx), medium pairs, or some air — in any case a large portion of his range will call a river bluff or simply have me beat. With almost no showdown value and very limited fold equity on this dry board, a bluff is low EV and unnecessary given my big stack. I preserve chips and go to showdown: check.

River 6d is a complete blank that doesn't complete any strong draws (no flush completed beyond clubs which hit turn, no straight obvious). Board: Ks7c2h Ac6d favors pairs, two-pair, sets, and Ax. Minni's line: BB call pre (loose), check-call flop on dry Ks7c2h (suggests mid-pair like 88-TT/77/22, weak Kx, or stubborn Ax/draw), check turn after Ac (didn't want to bet into possible Ax or capped her own range), check river (weakness, unlikely strong value or bluff). My Qh9s is pure queen-high bluff-catcher with no pair/draw; beats total air/missed draws but loses to any made hand she check-called flop with. Historical passivity (frequent checks, small calls then folds to aggression) supports capped range unlikely to hero-call or bluff-raise river. Pot 1450, my stack 2600 (~17BB effective); betting risks bloating pot OOP for her (but she's checked), low fold equity vs her showdown value, poor EV as value-bet range empty. Checking realizes equity cheaply in position, induces any bluffs she might have (unlikely), preserves stack for late tournament survival/steals (hand 119/150, 3-handed button premium). Consistent with turn check for pot control. Optimal to check behind for showdown.

I am on the button with King-Seven offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand from the button. Raising to 300 allows me to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. Elon (Big Blind) has a relatively short stack, so I need to be aware of potential shoves, but I should still lead with a standard raise.

I have pocket 4s (4d4c) in the small blind facing a button open to 300. Blinds are 50/100 so my call is 250 (to match the raise). With deep stacks (my 18,275; Flash 17,325) this is an ideal set-mining spot — Flash’s button open will be fairly wide and I get excellent implied odds to hit a set. 3-betting out of position only to a min‑raise (to 500) would bloat the pot and create tougher post‑flop decisions OOP; a large 3‑bet isn’t warranted either. I’ll call the 250, keep the pot manageable, see a flop, and play aggressively if I hit a set. If I face a large 3‑bet (or an all‑in) I’ll reevaluate and fold to unreasonable sizing but will call shoves when pot odds and ICM allow.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 from Flash and a small blind call from Minni. My hand is Qc5s, offsuit, unconnected, and weak with poor postflop playability. Q5o is not strong enough to defend against two players, even out of position with initiative lost. Flash's button range is wide but still favors hands that dominate Q5o, and Minni's call suggests decent holdings. Pot odds are about 3:1 (700:200), but equity is low (~20-30% vs calling range), and multiway OOP is unprofitable long-term. Folding preserves stack for better spots in late tournament stage.

Check. I called preflop with 4♦4♣ to set-mine; the flop A♦ Q♠ 8♦ completely misses me — I’m an underpair to two overcards with only a runner‑runner diamond possibility. Betting OOP here is thin (I have little fold equity and will get called by better hands), so I’ll control the pot and preserve my stack. Plan: check and evaluate — call small/medium continuation bets to realize set‑mining implied odds given deep stacks, fold to large, polarized bets, and if I hit a set on the turn I will play aggressively.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board came Ace-high with a Queen and an Eight. This is a very good board for my perceived range as the button raiser, as I am more likely to have strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) than Minni, who called from the Small Blind. Although I have missed the flop entirely with my King-Seven, a continuation bet here is a standard play to represent the Ace and force folds from middle pairs or missed broadway hands. Minni's check indicates she hasn't flopped a monster or is playing a 'wait and see' approach with a marginal hand. A bet of about 40% to 50% of the pot should be enough to win the hand immediately without risking too much if she happens to have a piece of it.

I called preflop to set‑mine with 4♦4♣ and the flop (8♦ Q♠ A♦) missed me — I’m an underpair to two overcards with only a very weak backdoor diamond draw. Flash’s flop action is a small c‑bet (300 into the ~1,000 pot), which is commonly part of a wide continuing range from the button. Calling 300 keeps the pot controlled, realizes my set‑mining plan given very deep stacks (good implied odds if I hit my set), and denies Flash easy fold equity to a raise. Pure raw equity to hit a set by the river (~8–9%) doesn’t justify a large bluff/raise here, so I’ll call the small bet and re‑evaluate on the turn: if I hit a 4 I’ll get aggressive for value; if an A/Q or a heavy diamond action arrives I’ll be cautious and fold to polarized, large turn/river raises.

Check. The turn (Tc) completely misses my pocket 4s — I remain an underpair to the A and Q and now have only two outs to make trips (~2 cards left: 2/46 ≈ 4.3% on the river). I called the small flop raise to realize set‑mining odds, but OOP on the turn I should control the pot rather than barreling as a bluff: any realistic turn bet (100+) would force a call that my equity does not justify. Quick math: calling a bet x into the 1,300 pot requires equity ≥ x/(1,300+2x); with ~4.3% equity I can only profitably call a tiny bet (≈≤60 chips), which is not a practical sizing here. I'll check to keep the pot small and fold to any significant turn bet; if Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river — I’ll value‑bet or raise only if I hit a 4, otherwise fold to strong action.

The turn Ten of Clubs (Tc) is a dangerous card that completes several potential straight draws (KJ, J9) and improves many of Minni's likely flop-calling hands (like QT, T8, or AT) to two pair. My King-high has very little equity, and since Minni called my flop continuation bet, it's likely she has a piece of the board. Continuing to bluff on this texture is risky because she is unlikely to fold an Ace or even a strong Queen. Checking back allows me to control the pot and see a free river card.

I called preflop to set‑mine with 4♦4♣ and never hit — board A♦ Q♠ 8♦ / T♣ / 3♥. Flash led a small c‑bet on the flop and I called; both of us checked the turn. I now have only a low underpair with no meaningful redraws and very little showdown improvement on the river. Flash’s line (small flop bet then pot‑control checks) is consistent with a wide continuing range (weak A/Qx, floats, missed draws) and gives me almost no fold equity OOP. Betting here would be a pure bluff that gets called too often and risks chips when my hand is rarely best. Pot control and preservation of stack is optimal — check and take it down if he shows weakness, fold to any meaningful river aggression.

The board is Ad-Qs-8d-Tc-3h. I have King-high (Ks-7d). I raised pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, which Minni called. I checked the turn to control the pot size on a card that potentially improved her range or completed straights. On the river, Minni has checked for the third time. While her checks might indicate weakness, her flop call suggests she has at least some piece of the board (like a Queen or an Eight) or a draw that didn't get there. My King-high has very little showdown value, but a bluff here is risky as she might be in 'catch-mode' with a marginal hand after I showed weakness by checking the turn. Given the stack sizes and the current tournament stage, checking back and conceding the pot if she has a pair is a safer play than attempting a river bluff into a capped but potentially sticky range.

I’m on the button with A♥7♣ facing a single preflop raise. I have position on both opponents and a stack (19,025) much deeper than the small blind (Elon, 3,900) but the big blind (Flash) is deep and likely to call wide. A7o is a reasonable hand to defend in position, but it’s marginal as a 3-bet bluff—BB will call light and SB can shove or isolate. I want to keep the pot manageable and use position postflop to realize equity and pick spots. Plan: call to see a flop; if I hit an Ace or a favorable board I’ll take the lead, otherwise I’ll exercise pot control and fold to heavy aggression. Calling preserves fold equity and keeps weaker holdings in range without bloating the pot out of position.

Preflop in the small blind with 9s5h offsuit, a complete trash hand with no playability, suited potential, or blockers. Minni has limped on the button, which is often weak, but calling 50 into 250 gives great pot odds, yet postflop OOP with this hand is a nightmare—low equity against any calling range, terrible implied odds, and dominated often. Raising min to 200 has no fold equity with this hand and commits us awkwardly with short-ish stack (39BB). Folding preserves stack for premium spots; tournament stage favors tight play from blinds to avoid spew. History shows passive play often, but no reason to engage here.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 4s. The Button (Minni) limped in for 100 and the Small Blind folded. Since I have already posted the big blind, I have the option to check and see the flop for free. Given that my hand is very weak (3-4 offsuit), there is no reason to raise and build a pot out of position. I will take the free flop.

I have middle pair (fours) on a relatively dry board. In a limped pot, there's no need to build a big pot out of position with a marginal hand. I will check to see what Minni does and likely call a small bet to evaluate the turn.

Check. I missed the flop with A♥7♣ — I have nothing but Ace‑high on a very dry 8♥‑4♦‑2♦ board (two diamonds but no flush possibility for me). Flash (BB) checked; as the BB they defend wide, so a C‑bet here likely won’t fold many hands that beat me (pairs, diamonds, or reasonable showdown hands) and will get called. I prefer pot control and using my position to see a turn for free: if I improve (pair an Ace or get a favorable blank) I can take the lead; if Flash fires a large bet or overbets I’ll fold to heavy aggression per my preflop plan. Amount = 0 (check).

The turn is the King of Diamonds, which completes a potential flush if Minni has two diamonds, and also provides a high card that might hit a limped range. I still have middle pair (fours) with a 3 kicker. The pot is very small (250). I checked the flop and Minni checked back, suggesting neither of us has a strong hand yet. I will check again to keep the pot small and see the river for free, as my hand is not strong enough to value bet and I'd rather not turn it into a bluff here.

Board is 8♥-4♦-2♦-K♦ and I have A♥7♣ — I missed the turn (only Ace‑high) and hold no diamond. Flash (BB) checked the turn, and their range likely contains lots of Kx and diamonds (and some weak showdown hands). A turn bet here will fold very little and bloats a pot where I’m often behind or up against draws; with deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot small and use my positional advantage. Plan: pot control — check back the turn, see the river, bet small for value only if I pair my Ace, otherwise give up to heavy aggression (fold to large raises).

On the river, the board has paired deuces (8h 4d 2d Kd 2h). My hole cards are 3h 4s, which gives me two pair (fours and deuces) with a King kicker from the board. Given that Minni has checked the flop and the turn in a limped pot, she likely has a weak hand. However, my hand is also marginal (an 8 or any King or any deuce beats me). Betting doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and checking allows me to get to a cheap showdown. If she bets, I will evaluate based on the size, but for now, checking is the most solid play.

Board: 8♥-4♦-2♦-K♦-2♥. I hold A♥7♣. I missed — no pair, no diamond, and the board is paired with deuces. Flash (BB) checked turn and river, showing either weakness or a slow-played strong hand (Kx, any pocket pair, trips with a 2, or a two-diamond flush). Betting here as a bluff has poor fold equity versus a BB who defends/calls wide in position; value-betting makes no sense because I’m rarely ahead. The pot is small (250) relative to my stack, so pot control and going to showdown is the lowest-variance, most +EV line. Action: check. If Flash leads the river I’ll fold to meaningful pressure (I’m usually behind) and only consider a very small bet-call if sizing and reads indicate a high frequency of folding better hands, which I don’t have here.

3-handed on the button with A♠T♣, a strong hand in this spot. Blinds posted (pot 150, to call 100), no prior action. Standard play is to raise for value and position. Opponents are deep-stacked (171BB+), my stack 39BB. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) to build pot with premium hand, steal blinds if they fold, or play profitably postflop. Minni and Flash have been aggressive historically, but ATo dominates their calling ranges often.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 7s, a solid speculative hand in a 3-handed game. Elon has a stack of 3,600 (36 BBs) and opened for 300 from the Button. I am getting roughly 2.8:1 pot odds to see a flop. While being out of position is a disadvantage, the playability of suited connectors/gappers makes this a standard call in this spot. 3-betting is an option, but 9s 7s isn't ideal to face a 4-bet shove from Elon's stack size. I will call and see a flop.

I’m in the BB with A♣7♥ facing Elon (button) open to 300 and Flash (SB) call — pot is 700 and it costs me 200 more (300 total) to see a flop. A7o is a playable hand but marginal in a 3‑way pot: it has a useful Ace blocker and can make top pair, but its postflop playability is limited and I’ll often be out of position vs the opener. Calling gives excellent pot odds (200 to win ~900 → ~22% break‑even equity) and keeps the pot manageable while letting me see a flop and evaluate texture/reads. Raising to 500 here would bloat a multiway pot, won’t reliably isolate the short‑stacked opener (SB already in), and could commit me to tough spots with a mediocre hand. Plan: call, see the flop; proceed with a straightforward line (value/defend with top pair, pot‑control or fold when missed and significant aggression appears).

I have 9s 7s on a Th Ah 2c flop. I do not have a piece of this board, nor any direct draws (no flush draw, no straight draw). The Ace and Ten are very much in the ranges of both the original raiser (Elon) and the Big Blind (Minni). In a 3-way pot, my 9-high has very little equity. My best strategy is to check and likely fold to any significant aggression from either opponent, as I am out of position with no showdown value or clear improvement path.

I have top pair (A♣7♥) on Th Ah 2c and it’s my turn after Flash checked. This is a multiway pot with Elon (button) as the opener and a reasonable-sized pot (900). My hand is strong enough to get value from worse A-x and from many Ten or pocket-pair holdings, but my kicker is weak so I don’t want the pot to get out of control. A small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) achieves three goals: (1) extracts value from worse A’s and Tens, (2) charges heart/other draws (there are two hearts on the board), and (3) keeps the pot manageable against Elon’s shorter 3.6k stack and Flash’s deep stack. I’ll bet 300 to accomplish that and then re-evaluate on the turn — call/flat-call if I get single calls, proceed more cautiously if I face a sizable raise (fold to very large/committing raises unless I get correct pot odds), and consider check‑back pot control on a blank turn.

Preflop I raised with A♠T♣, got two callers, effective stacks deep except mine at ~36BB. Flop Th Ah 2c gives me top two pair (aces and tens) with a backdoor spade flush draw. Monster hand, crushes villain ranges. Flash (SB) checked weak. Minni (BB) bets 300 into 900ish pot (~1/3 pot), likely ace/high, pair, or heart draw. Pot 1200, calling 300 is +EV laydown, but raising extracts value from worse (weaker aces, Tx, draws) and protects vs. flush/straight draws. Button position, act last post-raise. Raise sizing: 4x bet to ~1200 total (~pot bet), leaves room for further action, induces calls from loose history (opponents call big bets often, e.g., hands 53,98). Shove too aggressive early street, smaller raise builds pot without scaring off. Maximize EV long-term tournament.

I have 9-high with no pair and no draws on an Ace-high board. After checking, the Big Blind (Minni) led out for 300, and the Button (Elon) raised to 1,200. Elon has a short stack and this raise signifies extreme strength or a high-equity draw that he is willing to commit with. Given that I have zero equity in this pot and am out of position against two active opponents, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and preserve my chip lead.

I have top pair (A♣7♥) on Th Ah 2c after a small c‑bet. Elon’s large raise to 1,200 is polarizing — it can be a value raise (better A‑kicker, two pair, or a set) or a shove/semibluff from a short stack (he only has ~2,400). Folding top pair here is too timid; reraising with a weak kicker is over‑committal and will get called by hands that dominate me. Calling is the prudent line: I keep weaker A’s and Tens in the pot, control the size of the pot, and get roughly correct pot odds to continue (call 900 into a ~2,400 pot ≈ 27% break‑even equity). Plan: call the 900 now, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to a shove or large, clearly value action if I don’t improve, but proceed for value if the turn is favorable or he shows weakness.

Check. I have top pair A♣7♥ with a very weak kicker, but the turn 8h gave me a one-card heart flush draw (9 hearts left → ~9/46 ≈ 19.6% to hit the river). Elon’s large flop raise to 1,200 was polarizing — he can have value (AK, two pair, sets) or a big semi-bluff (two hearts) and, with only ~2.4k behind, is in position to shove. Betting myself here either (a) bloats the pot and invites a shove I don’t want to call with a marginal kicker, or (b) folds out worse Aces/Tens I want value from. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse A’s/Tens and some bluffs in, and lets Elon show more. Plan: check; if Elon bets a small/medium amount I’ll call with my pair+9‑out draw, but I will fold to a large shove (calling ~2.4k into a ~3.3k pot requires ≈42% equity, which I’m unlikely to have vs his value-heavy shove range). Re-evaluate on the river (value-bet or raise if I hit, pot-control or fold if I miss and face strong action).

Heads-up on the turn with top two pair (Aces and Tens) on a board Th Ah 2c 8h. Preflop I raised A♠T♣, got two callers; flop action: Minni donk-bet 300 into ~900 pot (likely thin value like weaker Ace, Tx, or heart draw), I raised to 1200 for value/protection (crushes ranges), Flash folded, Minni called 900 showing strength but possibly drawing/paired. Pot now 3300, Minni checks turn completing obvious heart flush draw (three hearts) and adding gutshots (e.g., 97, J9). My hand is still very strong: blocks AA, nuts vs most Ax/Tx/draws, equity crushes her calling range. Her check after calling flop raise suggests weakness (missed draw, medium strength afraid of further action, or trapping trapper). With 2400 stack (~24BB effective, tournament mid-late), pot 3300, checking risks free river card (she could hit) or missing value; betting extracts from worse Ax (KQ/AJ), Tx, missed draws turning into bluffs, sets that check. Sizing: ~2/3 pot (2200) balances value/protection, leaves little behind (~200), gets called by looser history (Minni calls big spots frequently, e.g., hands 12,20,38), shoves too polar/overplays if she has flush/set. Maximizes EV by denying equity and building stack.

Call 2200. I have top pair (A♣7♥) with a very weak kicker on T♥ A♥ 2♣ 8♥. Elon’s line (big flop raise then another large turn raise leaving only 200 behind) is polarised — he can have strong value (AK/AQ/AJ, Tx two‑pair/sets, 22/TT, or T2) or a heart semi‑bluff. Folding here is too tight given the pot odds: calling 2200 into a 5500 pot gives ~28.6% break‑even equity. My concrete improvement outs are ~12 (3 sevens + 9 hearts) → ~26% to improve, and I also have some showdown equity vs bluffs/weaker A/T combos, so a call is roughly break‑even to +EV. Raising (to 2400) only extracts an extra 200 from Elon and bloats the pot with a marginal kicker — it’s unnecessary and could commit me to worse. Plan: call to see the river. If I pair the 7 or make the heart flush I’ll go for value; if I miss and face large/shove action on the river I’ll fold to strong aggression (I’m a marginal hand without improvement).

Bet 200. I have top pair (A♣7♥) but a very weak kicker and the turn heart completed a 4‑heart board; the river 4♠ missed my flush. Elon has been very aggressive but is now short (only 200 behind). A small value bet of 200 accomplishes the goal: it extracts the last of his stack from worse A‑x, Tx or thin calls/bluffs while risking a tiny fraction of my 15k+ stack. Checking gives him a free showdown or lets a better hand keep his stack intact; a larger bet is unnecessary and will fold out worse hands. So I make a small, +EV thin‑value bet to collect his remaining chips or get called by worse.

River board Th Ah 2c 8h 4s. I hold AsTc for top two pair (aces and tens), blocking AA and As flush. Board has three hearts, so opponent needs two hearts for flush (possible given flop donk and turn check-call). Other beats: sets (unlikely), two pair better like A8/A4. Her line: flop donk small into multiway (Ax/draw/Tx), called my raise; turn check-called 2200 into ~3450 (flush draw or medium strength); river min-bet 200 into 7900. Tiny bet induces or weak value/bluff. My stack 200 equals to-call; calling all-in risks 200 to win 8100 (pot odds 40.5:1, need ~2.4% equity). Top two crushes her calling range (Ax like AK/AQ/AJ/A9, Tx like KT/JT/TT unlikely, missed draws bluffing); beats 50%+ of possible hands. Even if flush 80% of her range, +EV. History: Minni calls large bets with marginal hands (e.g., H12,20,38,53). No fold possible; raise impossible (stack=200). Call maximizes EV, builds stack in tournament.

I am on the button with Qc9s, which is a decent but not premium hand in a 3-handed game. The pot is currently small, and I am the chip leader. Standard strategy suggests opening from the button to put pressure on the blinds. A raise to 300 (3x big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the hand or win the blinds immediately.

I have T♦4♠ in the small blind facing a standard button open to 300 (I’ve already posted 50, to call is 250). This is a weak, offsuit hand with poor flop playability and a high likelihood of being dominated by the button’s opening range. Calling commits chips out of position against the button (and possibly the big blind) for marginal equity; 3-betting light here is doable in some spots but the minimum raise to 500 is too small to credibly fold out callers and would put me in a tough OOP spot if called. With deep stacks and two players who will frequently continue, the EV of folding and waiting for a better spot is higher. I’ll conserve my stack and fold.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after SB folds. My hand 6h4c is offsuit, disconnected, and very weak with poor post-flop playability. Button ranges are wide (~40-50% hands), but 64o has low equity (~30%) and is hard to play OOP. Pot odds are ~2.25:1 (calling 200 to win 450), but even with decent implied odds deep-stacked, the hand doesn't connect well enough to justify defending. Flash has been aggressive historically. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in the tournament (hand 123/150) with 80BB effective.

K♦2♣ is a marginal hand but on the button I want to leverage position and fold equity. With stacks ~150bb (my stack 14,975) a small open is low risk and often wins the blinds outright. Elon (SB) is capable of 3-betting/playing big pots and Flash (BB) will defend widely, so I’ll keep the raise small to 200 to maximize fold equity while minimizing risk. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet (I don’t want to play a big pot with a weak K‑kicker), but if called I’ll use position to c‑bet on dry flops and play cautiously on A/K‑high boards where I’m likely dominated. Raise to 200 to attempt the steal.

T4s is a weak suited hand with a large gap, unsuitable for calling a button raise out of position in the small blind. Minni's raise from the button is wide-ranging, and my hand has poor postflop playability OOP against her range. Pot odds are about 3:1 (150 to call into ~500 pot), but equity is too low (~25-30% vs typical button open), and I risk difficult spots postflop with a short stack relative to blinds progression. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this late tournament stage (hand 124/150).

I am in the Big Blind with 5-3 offsuit. Minni raised to 200 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. Although I only need to call 100 more chips to see a flop, 5-3 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position. Given my current stack lead and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (hand 124 of 150), it's more prudent to fold this trash hand and conserve chips for better opportunities.

3-handed on the button with QhTc, a speculative hand with decent high-card strength and playability. Position is excellent, stacks are deep. Standard to raise for value and to thin the field/take initiative. Minni in BB may defend wide, Flash in SB may fold often. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is a balanced size to build pot while maintaining fold equity and position postflop. Folding too tight, limping poor strategy.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-6 offsuit. Elon, on the Button, has opened for 300. While button ranges can be wide, 8-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a raise. With only 25 hands remaining and maintaining a healthy stack lead, there is no strategic reason to call a raise with a marginal hand and risk chips in a disadvantageous position. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

Elon opened to 300 on the button heads‑up. I have A♠7♦ in the big blind. To call I need 200 more into a 450 pot (I’ll be committing 300 total including my posted BB) which gives me ~31% pot‑odds. A7o has reasonable equity vs a wide BTN opening range, so defending is +EV here. 3‑betting is an option to seize initiative, but A7o is a marginal 3‑bet (often dominated by better A‑x) and a raise invites a 4‑bet or larger pots out of position; I prefer to keep the pot controllable and see a flop. Plan: call, see the flop, and play aggressively only if I pair the Ace or get good equity/draws; otherwise fold to heavy aggression.

Flop: 5♦6♦2♦. I hold A♠7♦ — one diamond in hand gives me a 4‑diamond board (9 outs to a flush ≈35% to hit by river) plus an Ace overcard and a few additional pair outs. Versus a wide button opening range my hand has strong semi‑bluff equity (~40%+ overall). Elon is a frequent opener and c‑better, so leading here both builds fold equity and charges weaker hands. I don’t want to inflate the pot too much against his 7.6k stack, so I’ll make a controlled semi‑bluff: bet ~300 into 650 (≈45% pot). If he folds I take it down; if he calls I realize my equity with the draw and re‑evaluate on the turn (bet/raise when I improve, fold to very large raises that represent made diamonds or monster hands). If he check‑raises huge I’ll give credit to a made diamond/set and likely fold to avoid a poor SPR spot.

Flop is 5d6d2d, a highly coordinated board with a flush draw and straight draw possibilities (e.g., 34, 47). My hand QhTc has no pair, no flush draw (no diamond), no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), just queen-high. Equity is minimal against Minni's donk-bet range from BB defense vs my button raise, which likely includes diamond draws, pairs with backdoors, or strong made hands. Pot is 950, facing 300 (pot odds ~3.17:1, requiring ~24% equity), but QTo realizes far less vs plausible range. Position doesn't help enough on this wet board where villain can have nutted draws or better. Folding preserves stack for better spots in late tournament stage (hand 125/150, 3-handed). Bluff raise too thin with reverse implied odds and villain's stack (14k+). Clear fold.

I am on the Button with Ace-Nine suited (Ah9h), which is a premier starting hand in a three-handed game. I want to raise to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds while playing in position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with previous play and appropriate for this hand strength.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I have 5♦4♦ in the small blind with deep stacks (I ≈154bb, Flash ≈165bb, Elon ≈75bb). 5-4 suited is a speculative, deep-stack hand that can make straights and flushes and gets good implied odds against a wide button opening range. Folding is too tight for such a small investment; 3-betting as a bluff is unattractive — 54s is easily dominated and a small 3-bet to 500 won’t fold many button opens and will often get called by the BB, leaving me OOP with a marginal hand. Calling 250 more (total 300 committed) is a cheap way to realize equity and keep the pot controllable. Plan: see the flop, fold to heavy aggression unless I hit a meaningful pair/draw (or pick up a favourable heads-up situation if Elon folds), and only invest more when I have clear equity or a good plan to pressure.

K5o is a marginal hand at best from the big blind facing a button raise from Flash, who often raises wide, and a call from Minni in the small blind. Postflop playability is poor with reverse implied odds if I hit top pair weak kicker. Pot odds are about 3.5:1 (calling 200 into 700, making 900), requiring ~18% equity, but against likely ranges dominated by Ax, broadway, pairs, and suited hands, equity is lower. Stacks are deep (75BB effective), so no need to defend weakly out of position. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

Flop: 3♦ 2♥ 8♣. I hold 5♦4♦ — I have a clean open-ended straight draw (need A or 6 = 8 outs) plus a small backdoor diamond flush possibility. By the river my equity to hit the straight is ~31–32% (plus ~4% for two-card diamond runner-runner). Pot is 700 and stacks are deep, so this is a classic draw worth seeing a turn cheaply. Betting OOP as a semi-bluff risks a raise and bloating the pot without nut equity; checking keeps the pot manageable and lets the button c-bet a range I can call profitably on smaller bet sizes. Calculation: with P=700 and equity ≈0.315, the maximum single-street bet I should call break-even is ≈321 chips (bigger bets require >~32% equity to justify a call). Plan: check and call modest continuation bets (or check-call to the turn); fold only to large overbets/raises that price me incorrectly or show strong made hands. If Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn to continue realizing equity.

I am on the Button with Ace-Nine of hearts (Ah9h). I raised pre-flop and was called by the Small Blind (Minni). The flop is 3d 2h 8c, which is a very dry, low-card board. It is unlikely to have connected strongly with the SB's calling range, which often consists of broadway cards or medium pairs. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have two overcards plus a backdoor flush draw, a continuation bet is standard here to take down the pot or define my opponent's hand. I will bet 350, which is half the pot, to exert pressure.

Flop is 3♦‑2♥‑8♣ and I hold 5♦4♦. I have an 8‑out open‑ended straight draw (A or 6) and a tiny runner‑runner diamond flush chance. Flash bet 350 into the pot (pot was ~700, now 1050), so calling 350 requires ~25% equity to break even. My two‑card equity to the river to hit the straight is ≈31–32% (plus a small backdoor flush chance), so a call is +EV. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloated the pot with a marginal semi‑bluff; check‑raising or a larger raise will often be called or re‑raised and leave me OOP with only draw equity. Plan: call the 350 to realize equity, see the turn, and only continue aggressively if I improve (6/A or diamond runner) or can get favorable odds — fold to large raises or heavy action when I miss.

The turn Ad just completed my wheel: my 5♦4♦ plus the 3♦ and 2♥ on board gives A‑2‑3‑4‑5 — a made straight — and I also have a 4‑card diamond holding (one diamond away from a flush). Flash opened and c‑bet the flop, so his range is wide and contains many hands I currently beat (Ax, 8x, single pairs, and many bluffs). Pot is 1400 and stacks are deep, so I should extract value rather than check behind and give up value to worse hands. I’ll make a value bet about half‑pot (~700) to get called by worse pairs/Ax/8x and to charge diamond draws. Plan if action continues: call reasonable turn raises (my straight is strong vs his range on this card) and re‑evaluate vs very large, polarized raises that would represent rare hands that could beat me; on the river adjust bet/call sizing based on the card (bet for value on safe runouts, be cautious if a diamond completes the flush).

The turn brings an Ace, giving me top pair. Minni leads out for 700 into a 1400 pot after calling my flop continuation bet. This lead often represents an Ace with a weaker kicker or a hand like a small pocket pair turning into a bluff. While I have top pair, my kicker (9) isn't the strongest, and raising might only get called by better hands like better Aces, sets, or two-pair. Calling is the most prudent play as it allows me to keep the pot manageable, keep her bluffs in, and evaluate her action on the river in position.

I have 5♦4♦ on 3♦‑2♥‑8♣‑A♦‑5♣ — I made the wheel (A‑2‑3‑4‑5). This is a very strong hand: almost every reasonable continuing holding Flash can have (Ax, single pairs, 8x, some pocket pairs, and many of his bluffs) is behind my straight. The only realistic hands that beat me are very narrow 4+6 combos that make a 6‑high straight, which are rare given my line and the board. Flash opened pre, c‑bet the flop and called my turn bet — a line consistent with many hands that will pay to see a river. I should extract value rather than check and give up money to worse holdings. I'll make a straightforward value bet about half the pot to get called by Ax, pairs and worse. If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (few combinations beat me, so I’ll be prepared to call reasonable raises, but be wary of highly polarized overbets).

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a board that contains a straight possibility (4-5-6), though unlikely given the pre-flop and flop action. Minni's lead on the turn when the Ace landed and her subsequent barrel on the river show significant strength, suggesting she either has an Ace, two pair, or a set. However, in a three-handed game, folding top pair with a decent kicker when receiving 3:1 pot odds is generally too tight. I lose to better Aces (AT-AK), sets (88, 33, 22), and two pairs (A8, A5, A3, A2), but I beat weaker Aces and potential bluffs or turned pairs. Raising would only isolate me against hands that have me beat, so calling is the most profitable decision to see the showdown while keeping the pot manageable.

I have A♦Q♥ on the button in a three-handed pot — a strong, playable hand I want to take the initiative with. Raising isolates the blinds, builds the pot when I'm ahead of their calling ranges, and gives me fold equity. I choose a standard 3x BB raise to 300 (total) — big enough to discourage limp/call multiway pots but not so large as to commit a big portion of my deep stack. Postflop I'll continuation-bet many favorable textures and re-evaluate if Elon (SB) 3-bets (call smaller 3-bets to see a flop, fold to very large 4-bets depending on sizing/reads).

Preflop as SB facing button raise to 300 (3x BB). Hand is 8s5s, suited connector with a gap, speculative but low equity OOP. Button open range is wide, but calling 250 OOP with this hand leads to difficult postflop spots with reverse position. No strong blockers for 3bet bluff, and stacks are deep enough (75bb) to wait for better spots. Folding preserves stack for stronger hands in late tournament stage (hand 127/150). Standard defend range SB vs BTN doesn't include 85s comfortably OOP.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Trey offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. With the Small Blind folding, I am getting roughly 2.25 to 1 on a call (200 to win a pot of 450). A3o is a standard hand to defend in this spot as it has decent high-card value and some straight potential, though I will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling is the most stable option to see a flop without over-committing to a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind and the board is 6c-4d-5h. Holding As-3c, I have an open-ended straight draw (any 2 or 7 gives me a straight). This board texture is generally better for my defending range than the Button's raising range. However, playing out of position, the standard approach is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction. If Minni bets, I have more than enough equity to continue. For now, I will check.

I raised preflop with A♦Q♥ and the BB checked on a low, disconnected flop (6♣-4♦-5♥). I have two overcards and initiative but no pair or meaningful draw — this is a textbook spot to continuation-bet to take the pot now and fold out weaker made hands/overcards. I choose a ~1/2–55% pot c-bet (350 into 650): big enough to apply pressure and deny equity to many of Flash’s calling combos, but small enough to avoid overcommitting if I face resistance. Plan: if I get called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn — value-bet if I pair an Ace/Queen, pot-control on blank or dangerous turns, and fold to a large check-raise unless the sizing/read suggests a bluff.

On a board of 6c-4d-5h, I have an open-ended straight draw with my Ace-Trey. This board texture is very favorable for my Big Blind defending range, as I have many more combinations of two-pair, sets, and straights than a Button opening range typically does. Minni's bet of 350 into 650 is a standard continuation bet. I have roughly 32% equity to hit my straight by the river, and potentially some additional equity if an Ace is good. Calling is the most efficient way to realize this equity without overplaying my hand out of position, especially since I can check-raise or lead on a variety of turn cards (2, 7, or even overcards).

On the turn, the board has updated to 6c-4d-5h-Qs. I am still holding an open-ended straight draw with my Ace-Trey. The Queen of Spades is a card that theoretically favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (Minni), potentially hitting hands like AQ, KQ, or QJ. Since I am out of position, the most prudent play is to check. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the overcard. If she checks behind, I get a free card to hit my straight. If she bets, I can evaluate the price and decide whether to call based on the pot odds and my implied odds of hitting the straight on the river.

The turn pairs my hand — I now have top pair with the best kicker (Qx with an A kicker). Flash called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn, which looks like a medium/weak one-pair holding (6x/5x/4x or a small pocket pair), a float, or a missed draw. Checking behind surrenders value and gives free cards to any runner-runner draws; betting extracts value from worse pairs and protects against the few turn/river equity cards. The pot is 1,350 so a ~2/3-pot sizing is appropriate to charge calling hands while not overcommitting if I face a big check-raise. I will bet 900. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river for thin value or pot-control; if he check-raises big I’ll give strong weight to folding (sets/two-pair), and only continue to small/medium raises depending on sizing/reads.

On the turn, the board is 6c-4d-5h-Qs. I am holding As-3c for an open-ended straight draw (any 2 or 7). The pot is 2250 and Minni has bet 900, which is a 2/3 pot bet. To justify a call based solely on pot odds, I would need roughly 28.6% equity. With 8 outs to a straight, my equity is approximately 17.4% (8/46). This means I need significant implied odds to make the call profitable. Given the board texture (4-5-6-Q), a river 2 or 7 would be quite visible to an observant opponent. Additionally, Minni has shown a willingness to fold when the board becomes dangerous (as seen in Hand #20 and Hand #23), suggesting that my implied odds might be capped. Since the direct pot odds are not sufficient and the likelihood of extracting over 2000 chips on the river is uncertain, I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Preflop on the button with JTo offsuit. This is a strong speculative hand in position against the blinds, with excellent playability for multiway pots or postflop. There's 150 in the pot and only 100 to call, likely from a limp by Flash in the SB, providing good odds. Raising isolates the limper, takes initiative, and builds the pot with position. Standard sizing here is to 3x the BB, to 300 total. My stack is 75BB deep enough for flexibility postflop.

I have been dealt pocket Aces (Ah Ac), which is the best possible starting hand. Elon has raised from the button to 300. Given my strong hand and position in the small blind, I want to build the pot immediately. I will put in a 3-bet to 1000 to extract value and potentially get one of my opponents to commit more chips pre-flop.

7♥5♠ is a weak, uncoordinated offsuit hand with poor post‑flop playability. Elon opened from the button to 300 and Flash 3‑bet to 1000 from the SB — a range that will be strong/polarized. Calling 900 into a 1400 pot requires about 900/(1400+900) ≈ 39% equity to break even, which 7‑5o won’t have versus these ranges, especially out of position in a 3‑way pot. A 4‑bet here would be an unnecessary over‑commitment with almost no fold equity. Preserve my stack and fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash in the SB after my button open to 300. JTo offsuit is a playable hand in position against a likely wide SB 3-bet range, as SB often 3-bets light to deny equity or steal. Pot odds are excellent: calling 700 into 1400 (pot becomes 2100), with SPR ~3 postflop on my 7200 stack. I have position heads-up, good playability for straights, and blockers. Folding is too tight given history of Flash's aggression (frequent raises, some light 3-bets shown). Raising is unnecessary with this hand; better to realize equity postflop. Calling keeps pot manageable and exploits position.

I have pocket Aces (Ah Ac) on a board of Qs Qc 2s. While the paired Queens are slightly concerning if Elon has a Queen in his range (like AQ or KQ), I am still ahead of most of his calling range from the pre-flop 3-bet, including JJ, TT, 99, and flush draws. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 1/3 the pot to extract value from these hands and protect against the spade draw.

Facing a flop bet of 750 into 2850 on QsQc2s with JdTh offsuit. My hand completely whiffs: no pair, no draws (backdoor at best, but irrelevant), pure air. Flash's SB 3bet preflop vs button open is polarized (QQ+/AK value or Ax suited/spade bluffs), and this board smashes the value portion while giving flush draw equity to bluffs. Donk betting into me suggests strength or semi-bluff protection, but range heavily favors hands that connect. Pot odds are ~3.8:1 (needing ~21% equity), but I have <10% vs realistic range (dominated or drawing dead often). Position doesn't compensate for zero equity/no fold equity on raise. History shows Flash aggressive, but this spot calls for discipline: folding marginal preflop calls postflop when missed. Preserves stack for better spots with 65BB depth.

I am on the button with a decent speculative hand, 97 suited. In a three-handed game, this is a standard hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play it in position. Elon is relatively short-stacked, so I need to be aware of a potential shove, but 97s is strong enough to justify an opening raise of 3x the big blind.

Call 300. Q♠8♣ is marginal but has enough showdown and playability versus a wide button opening to justify a flat from the small blind with 18875 behind. The opener (Flash) raised to 3bb and stacks are deep, so folding is too tight given the cheap price to see a flop and the potential to hit a top pair or good draws. I don’t want to 3-bet as a bluff here because Q8o is frequently dominated by the button’s range and I’ll be out of position postflop; a light 3-bet also risks isolation or a bigger 3-bet from Elon. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — fold to a substantial 3-bet preflop, defend/passively versus the big blind’s cold-call, c-bet selectively on favorable textures (when I connect or gain fold equity), and control the pot on dry boards.

Preflop in the big blind with 5d-2c, one of the worst possible hands (52 offsuit). Facing a button raise to 300 and small blind call, the pot is 700 and I need to call 200 for implied odds that don't justify it given my poor equity against likely calling/raising ranges. Post-flop playability is terrible with no straight potential, weak flush draw potential, and dominated low cards. Stacks are deep except mine at 6400 (~64BB), but folding trash preserves stack for better spots. No value in calling or raising.

Check. The flop (Jh Jc 5s) is very unfriendly to Q♠8♣ — I have no pair and almost no realistic draw (only a runner-runner straight possibility). Flash opened wide from the button and checked, so his range contains a lot of Jx, pocket pairs and broadways that will either call a probe or check‑raise/continue for value. Betting as a bluff here has poor fold equity and will often get called by worse hands, so risking chips is unnecessary with deep stacks and a 700‑chip pot. I’ll control the pot and see a turn for free: if I pair a Q (or hit a very favorable runner) I can start betting for value; if the turn bricks I’ll give up to significant aggression and only call small probes if pot odds justify it.

I raised pre-flop with 97s and was called by Minni in the small blind. The flop comes with a pair of Jacks (Jh Jc 5s), which is a very dry board that generally favors the pre-flop aggressor. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a standard continuation bet of 50% of the pot to represent a strong range (including Jacks or pocket pairs) and attempt to win the pot immediately. I also have a backdoor club flush draw for additional equity if called.

Flash opened wide from the button and I defended with Q♠8♣ planning to keep the pot small and see a turn. On Jh Jc 5s he bets 350 into 1050 (~1/3 pot). Calling 350 gives ~25% pot odds (350/(1050+350)=25%). My chance to pair a Q or 8 by the river is ~24% (plus small backdoor possibilities) — borderline but justified given the cheap price to see the turn and the chance to extract value if I pair. Raising OOP as a bluff here has poor fold equity and will be called too often by worse hands or crushed by Jx/pocket pairs, so I won't bluff-raise. Plan: call the 350 to see the turn, re-evaluate on the turn — if I hit a Q (or pick up clear equity) I'll start betting for value; if the turn bricks and Flash applies substantial pressure (a raise or a large barrel), I’ll fold. I will fold to significant immediate aggression (e.g., a raise to the min-raise 700 or larger).

Board is Jh Jc 5s 2d and I hold Qs8c — I completely missed. I have only 6 weak outs (three Qs, three 8s) and essentially no backdoor equity worth building a pot with. Flash opened wide and barreled the flop, so his continuing range contains lots of Jx and medium/strong pocket pairs; I have very poor fold equity OOP. With a 1,400-chip pot and deep stacks, the correct line is pot control: check the turn and keep the pot small. Plan for the river: if Flash checks I’ll check down; if he fires a small probe I’ll only call with a clear pair (or if the price is correct) and fold to sizeable raises — I will not bluff-raise OOP with Q8o. Preserve stack and look for a better, cheaper spot.

On a paired board of Jacks, I have no showdown value with 9-high. I c-bet the flop and Minni called, which suggests she has a piece of the board, a pocket pair, or a decent Ace-high. The turn 2d is a complete brick. By betting again, I am continuing to represent a strong range, specifically a Jack or a high pocket pair, which should put significant pressure on her marginal hands like small-to-medium pocket pairs or overcards. If she calls here, I will likely check back the river unless a very favorable card arrives.

Board: Jh Jc 5s 2d. I hold Q♠8♣. Pot is 2250 and it costs 850 to call (pot odds = 850 / (2250+850) ≈ 27.4%). I have only ~6 clean outs (three Qs + three 8s) to pair on the river → equity ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13%, and several of those pairs still lose to Jx or bigger pairs. Flash’s line (wide button open, small flop c-bet, sizeable turn barrel) is consistent with Jx/strong pairs/value hands and gives me very little fold equity OOP. A call is negative EV given the pot odds vs actual equity, and a raise as a bluff is unlikely to work. Fold and preserve my 18k+ stack for a better spot.

I'm on the Button in a 3-handed pot with a very weak hand (2h6c). With only the blinds to beat and a large positional advantage, this is a textbook steal spot. I want to maximize fold equity while keeping risk controlled, so I open to 3x the big blind (300 total). If I get folded I pick up the blinds; if called I plan to continuation-bet selectively (probe on dry/high boards, check/ pot-control on coordinated boards) and concede to a significant 3-bet from either opponent since my hand has poor showdown value. Raising to 300 balances fold equity and keeping stacks manageable against potential resistance.

4h7c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position. Facing a button raise from Minni, who frequently raises from late position based on hand history, this is a clear fold. Pot odds don't justify calling (needing ~37% equity which I don't have vs a raising range), and with ~20BB effective stack, preserve chips for better spots near the end of the tournament (hand 130/150). No 3-bet value or bluff equity here.

KQo is a premium hand in a three-handed game. Since I am in the Big Blind and Minni is raising from the button, I will 3-bet to 1000 to take the initiative and protect my blind. This sizing puts pressure on wide opening ranges while still allowing me to play a pot if called.

I opened to 300 as a button steal, but Flash’s sizable 3‑bet to 1000 takes away most of my fold equity. My hand (2♥6♣) has almost no showdown value and is heavily dominated by a typical BB 3‑bet range. Calling 700 to play a speculative, low‑equity hand is not profitable (it requires ~34% equity vs the current pot) and a small 4‑bet/raise would simply build a big pot with a poor hand or get called by better hands. Preserve my ~18k stack, wait for a much stronger holding or a clearer spot to apply pressure — fold and move on.

Preflop on the button with 8c4s offsuit after presumed SB fold. Effective heads-up vs BB with dead money in pot (150). Position is excellent, and with deep stacks, button raising range is very wide (~60-70% of hands). 84o has decent equity (~38%) against a typical BB defending range, plus significant fold equity against Minni who has shown aggression but also folds preflop often. Raising builds the pot when ahead postflop and takes down blinds frequently. Standard size 3x BB to 300. Folding too tight; limping exploitable and weak.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 6s. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While 7-6 offsuit is a connector, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a raise, especially with a short-stacked opponent on the button who may be looking to commit. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and raising as a bluff is unnecessary given my current stack size and the stage of the tournament. The most disciplined play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Elon opened to 300 from the button; SB folded and I’m in the BB with Tc3c. Pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total). I’ll call (300 total). Rationale: T3s is marginal but defensible vs a wide button opening in 3-handed — the suited ten gives backdoor flush/straight potential and reasonable equity versus a wide range. The call requires ~30.8% equity (200 into a 650 pot) which is attainable vs a loose BTN open; folding here is too tight and a light 3‑bet OOP with this hand bloats the pot and is spewy. Postflop plan: play cautiously out of position — fold to large, polarized pressure when I miss; continue and build the pot when I hit a pair, two pair, or a strong club/straight draw; use pot control and pick spots to extract value or fold to heavy aggression.

Check. I called preflop with Tc3c as a speculative defense, but this flop (Qh 6c 4h) completely misses me — no pair and only a very remote backdoor club (would need both turn and river clubs). Elon (button) has a wide C-bet range here and will continuation-bet often; I have poor equity vs that range. With the pot at 650 and deep stacks, priority is pot control and avoiding unnecessary bloating with air. Plan: check to see his action, fold to meaningful pressure, and only continue if I pick up a pair or a real draw (or if he checks back). If he fires a very small probe I may call to see the turn, but otherwise preserve chips.

Heads-up on a Qh-6c-4h flop after I raised preflop from button and Minni called from BB. I hold 84o, flopping middle pair with 8 kicker (pairs the 4h), no draws. Board is draw-heavy with heart flush draw and possible gutshots (e.g., 53, 75). Minni checks, which reps weakness or traps, but her range is wide/capped vs button open (pairs, draws, Ax, suited connectors). My range hits top/middle pairs, overpairs, Qx, flush draws frequently. With position and ~40-50% equity vs her range (blocks low pairs/straights), betting denies equity to her draws and extracts value from worse pairs/A-high. Half-pot sizing builds pot without overbetting into potential monsters. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or check-raise bluffs. Deep stacks allow postflop playability. Standard continuation bet.

Hand: Tc3c on 6c Qh 4h. I missed the flop — only a very remote backdoor club/straight potential and two overcards (T/3). Elon opened preflop from the button (wide in 3-handed) and now fires a modest-sized flop bet to 400 into a 1050 pot (~38% pot). Pot odds for a call are 400 / (1050+400) ≈ 27.6% equity required. My realistic equity vs a wide c-bet range (chance to pair T or 3 by river ~24% plus ~4% for backdoor club flush and some additional value vs villain bluffs) is around the break‑even point or slightly better, and Elon’s small sizing indicates a fairly wide/loose continuation-bet range. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so a flat call is the best line: it buys a cheap turn to realize equity and keeps bluffs/weak holdings in. Plan: call 400, fold to heavy turn pressure unless I improve (pair, two pair, or a real club/straight draw), value/raise if I pick up meaningful equity.

Turn pairs my 3 (Tc3c -> board 6c Qh 4h 3d), so I improved to a very weak bottom pair and my backdoor club draw is dead. Elon’s line — open pre, modest c-bet on the flop, then checking the turn — looks like either pot-control with a real hand (Qx/top pairs) or giving up with a wide missed c-bet range. Betting here risks a check-raise or a shove from a short/aggressive button and only gets thin value from worse (A/K highs) while bloating a pot I don’t want to face pressure in. I’ll control the pot and see the river for free: check. If Elon fires a small river probe I’ll consider a call; I’ll fold to large aggression and preserve chips for better spots.

Heads-up on the turn with bottom pair (4s) and an 8 kicker after c-betting flop and getting called. Board is Qh-6c-4h-3d: draw-heavy with live heart flush draw, gutshots (5x now has straight 3-4-5-6), possible two-pair combos. Minni's BB calling range vs button open includes Qx, Ax, pairs 77-JJ, draws (hearts, 53/75/25 for str). She checked flop (weak/trap), called c-bet, checked turn (polarized: strong or weak). My range reps top pairs, sets, draws strongly. Pair of 4s has ~35-45% equity vs her range, blocks some str draws (holds 4). With position and SPR ~4:1, betting ~55% pot (800) extracts thin value from worse (A-high, 5s, missed draws), protects vs gutshots/hearts, has fold equity vs floats. Checking risks free river to 9/10 outs for her draws (~20% equity gain), pot control unnecessary as hand caps my range somewhat. Deep stacks, late tournament: apply pressure to build stack. Minni history shows calling stations but folds to repeated aggression.

Turn summary: I checked the turn after pairing my 3 (Tc3c) and Elon fired a small bet of 800 into a 2250 pot (to-call 800). That sizing is a probe/continuation from a wide button range in 3‑handed play. I now have a weak bottom pair (3s) with a T kicker — behind any Qx/6x/4x or better, ahead of pure air and many missed overcards. Calling requires ~800/(2250+800) ≈ 26% equity, which is reasonable vs a wide barreling range, and folding would be too tight. Raising is unattractive: my hand has poor playability and Elon is short enough to shove, which would put me in a tough spot with only bottom pair. Plan: call 800 to see the river and keep the pot manageable; on the river I will re-evaluate — fold to large shove unless I improve to two pair/trips or the bet is small enough to justify a call, and only raise for value with a much stronger holding.

Board: 6c Qh 4h 3d 2s; I hold Tc3c — a weak bottom pair (trip? no, pair of 3s) with a T kicker. Elon showed aggression earlier (preflop raise, flop bet, turn barrel) but checked the river, which looks like weakness or pot control rather than a made value hand. Betting here is thin: most hands worse than mine (pure air/missed floats) likely folded earlier and better hands (Qx, pocket pairs) either beat me or will raise/call — and Elon can shove his ~4.8k stack, which I don’t want to face with a single weak pair. Checking preserves my stack and gets to showdown to realize equity against his wide barreling range. Therefore I check and see the showdown rather than bluffing or bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

River board 6c-Qh-4h-3d-2s completes nut low straight for 5x hands (2-3-4-5-6), which were open-enders on turn and crush my bottom pair of 4s (weak kicker). Hearts bricked. Minni's BB calling range vs my button aggression: Qx, 6x, Ax suited, pairs 55-99/JJ, former draws (hearts, 5x). She triple-checked, called flop/turn c-bets, suggesting medium strength or trap, but unlikely super nuts (sets/two-pair would raise sometimes). My range polarized: strong value (Qx+, straights, two-pair) bets river; bluffs less since turn bet reps strength. Bottom pair bluff-catcher, but poor vs value range (~30-40% equity). Pot 3050, SPR ~1.6:1; betting ~1500 risks raise/fold equity low after her calls. Check preserves stack, realizes equity vs worse (A-high, missed draws, trapped pairs <4). Consistent with exploitative history: Minni calls light but river bet may induce her fold only weaker or raise better. Deep stacks late (131/150), avoid variance, check showdown maximizes EV.

I am on the button with 9-8 offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening range hand for this position. Raising allows me to take control of the pot and leverage my position throughout the hand against the blinds.

I have pocket 3s in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must add 250). With deep stacks (I ~164BB, Button ~153BB, BB ~78BB) the correct +EV play is to set-mine: call and see a flop. The immediate break-even equity to call is ~35.7% (250/(450+250)), and while a flop set only hits ~11.8% on the flop, the implied odds here are excellent — if I hit a set I can win a big pot from either opponent. Raising to isolate would commit more chips out of position, invite 3-bets, and reduce my implied odds; I’d rather keep the pot controlled and see a flop. Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit a set or favorable texture I’ll build the pot, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to large aggression on hostile boards or 3-bets that price out set-mining. Therefore I will call (put a total of 300 into the pot).

AJs is a premium suited broadway hand with excellent playability and equity against a button open range, which is likely wide and stealing-oriented given Flash's aggressive history. Minni's flat-call from SB suggests a weaker speculative hand rather than strength. Facing a 3bb raise and call into a 700 pot, calling 200 is passive and allows multiway pot OOP. Raising to 1000 (about 3.3x) applies pressure, isolates the button, potentially folds out Minni, and builds the pot with position postflop if called. My stack of 7800 supports this sizing without overcommitting. Folding is too tight preflop.

I opened 9-8 offsuit from the button, which is a standard wide opening range in a 3-handed game. Minni called from the small blind, and Elon has now 3-bet to 1000 from the big blind. While I would have position for the rest of the hand, 9-8 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, particularly when another player (Minni) has already shown interest in the pot. Elon's sizing suggests strength or a desire to isolate, and my hand does not have enough playability to justify calling 700 more. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I was planning to set-mine with pocket 3s by calling the button open. Now Elon 3-bet to 1,000 (Flash folded), so I’m heads-up with deep stacks. The pot is 1,600 and I must add 700 more, giving ~30% immediate break-even equity — my flop set frequency (~11.8%) is low but the implied odds versus a single opponent with deep stacks make calling +EV. A 4-bet would reduce implied odds and commit more chips out of position with a marginal hand, so I’ll call the 3-bet and see a flop. Post-flop plan: if I hit a set or a very favorable texture I’ll build the pot; if I miss I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to large, sustained aggression or an all-in that prices out set-mining.

I missed my set — I have pocket 3s (3♦3♣) on K♦2♦5♦. I’m out of position vs Elon (the preflop 3‑better). I don’t have a made hand but I do have a strong drawing package: 9 diamonds left to make a flush on the turn + 2 remaining threes to hit a set = 11 clean outs (~41–42% to improve by the river). Pot is 2,300 and Elon has ~6,900 behind.

With this combination (an underpair + a very playable draw) the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and realize equity rather than barreling OOP into a 3‑better’s likely strong c‑betting range. Checking lets me call reasonable continuation bets and fold to large, polarized aggression that would price me poorly. Rough threshold math: a bet up to roughly ~1,645 chips (~70% of the pot) is profitable to call given my ~41% raw equity; bigger, committed bets I’ll respect and fold unless the pot odds / reads change. If the turn gives me a diamond or a 3 I’ll start building the pot; if Elon checks back I get a free card and can proceed. Therefore: check (0).

Preflop, my 3bet with AhJs was standard aggression from the BB facing a button open and SB call, isolating with blockers and playability. Flop Kd2d5d hits speculative hands Minni might call with (suited connectors, Adx), but my range as 3bettor includes overpairs, AK/AQ, sets that crush her. AhJs has an ace blocker (key on this Ax heavy board), ace-high equity vs draws/floats (~60%+ vs typical calling range), and position. Board is extremely draw-heavy (obvious flush draw, wheel straight draws), so cbetting denies equity, extracts from trapped value, and applies fold equity to missed broadways/pairs. Minni's check is standard OOP weakness signal. Bet 60% pot (1400 into 2300) balances value (overpairs crush her) and bluffs, maintains SPR ~3.5:1 for turn flexibility with 6900 stack. Checking risks free cards to 30+ outs of draws that beat us. Opponents' history shows passivity postflop, favoring our aggression.

I call 1400. I have pocket 3s (3♦3♣) on K♦-2♦-5♦: I missed a set but hold a strong drawing package — 9 diamonds + 2 threes = 11 outs (~41.7% to improve to turn/river). Pot is 3700 after Elon’s 1400 bet; calling 1400 commits for pot odds of ~1400 / 5100 ≈ 27.5%, well below my ~41.7% raw equity, so a call is +EV. I’m out of position and Elon 3‑bet preflop, so raising here would be high variance and often incorrect; I’ll realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: if the turn is a diamond or a 3 (I complete a strong draw or a set) I’ll start building the pot and extract value; if the turn is a blank and Elon fires a very large, polarized bet (>~70% pot or an all‑in), I’ll give credit and fold. Otherwise I’ll continue to call reasonable bets to see to river and use my outs.

I called preflop and the flop as a set-mining + diamond draw. Turn Kc is a blank — I missed both a 3 and a diamond. I still have 11 outs (9 diamonds + 2 treys) — ~11/46 ≈ 23.9% to hit by the river. Pot is 5100 and I’m out of position vs a preflop 3‑better who barreled the flop, so betting as a bluff has poor fold equity on a paired‑King board and invites a raise that I should fold to. Checking keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize my equity; by pot‑odds alone I can profitably call a reasonable turn bet up to roughly ~2300, but I’ll fold to very large overbets or a shove. Plan: check here, call small/medium bets to see the river, fold to heavy aggression, and if I hit a diamond or a 3 on the river I’ll start building for value.

Preflop 3bet with AhJs was standard isolation from BB with ace-high playability and blockers against loose button open and SB call. Flop Kd2d5d is draw-heavy favoring our aggressive range; c-bet sizing extracted value from draws/pairs and built pot. Minni's check-call flop shows strength (likely Adx flush draw, pair+draw, or trapped value like 55/22/K5s) but weakness OOP. Turn Kc pairs top card, bricking most non-diamond straight draws, improving our perceived range (KK, AK), while keeping diamond flush draw live (~18% equity). AhJs holds ace-high with backdoor potential, solid blockers (A), and no showdown value, making it a strong bluff candidate in low-SPR spot (pot 5100, stack 5500). Checking risks giving free river card to draws or check-behind value. Opponents passive postflop historically (many check-folds/checks). Barrel ~2/3 pot (3500) denies equity (~35% pot denies ~20% equity), applies max fold equity vs her capped range (no sets likely flat pre/call flop), balances with KK/AK value. Her 14k stack allows fold; our 55bb stack justifies pressure late tournament to maximize EV.

Pot is 8,600 and I must call 3,500 (min‑raise to 5,500). I hold 3♦3♣ and have missed my set on K♦‑2♦‑5♦ / K♣. I only have 11 clean outs to the river (9 diamonds + 2 treys) → ~11/46 ≈ 23.9% equity. If I call 3,500 the total pot if I call becomes 12,100, so the break‑even equity needed is about 3,500/12,100 ≈ 28.9%. My raw equity (~24%) is well below the required break‑even point, so a pure call is -EV. 

Beyond the math, Elon's line is polarizing and strong: preflop 3‑bet, a flop continuation bet on a three‑diamond flop, then a very large turn bet once the board paired. That sequence is consistent with very strong holdings (Kx turning trips, a flopped nut/near‑nut diamond, or other strong/committed hands) — hands that either already beat me or make many of my diamond outs turn into losing flushes. Raising to 5,500 as a bluff only risks an extra 2,000 and is unlikely to get folds from the hands that dominate me, so it’s a high‑variance, thin play. 

Given the pot odds, my limited equity, and the opponent’s line, the correct low‑variance +EV decision is to fold and preserve a healthy stack for better spots.

Button with Q♠ J♥ is a standard open-raise: good playability and position against the blinds. Pot is 150 and the call is 100; I want to take the initiative, build the pot when in position, and generate fold equity against both blinds. A 3x BB sizing (raise to 300 total) is standard here — big enough to pressure marginal blind defenses but not so large as to commit my stack if Elon (SB) 3-bets. If I face a small-to-medium 3-bet I can call and play postflop in position; versus a large 3-bet I will re-evaluate (likely fold unless the sizing and reads justify a call). Postflop plan: continue as aggressor with top-pair/strong draws, pot-control with weaker holdings. Raise to 300.

Hand 133: In SB with 2c3h, absolute trash offsuit. Pot 450, to call 250 for ~1.8:1 odds, but equity vs Minni's button raise range (which is wide but still crushes this) is under 20%. Poor postflop playability OOP, no fold equity on 3bet as stacks deep. Standard fold to preserve stack with 18BB effective (10550/50=211BB actually wait no, blinds 50/100 so ~105BB, deep but still fold weak hands pre. History shows Minni aggressive but profitable folds elsewhere. Maximize EV by waiting for better spots near end of tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d4d, which is a weak suited connector, but I'm getting decent pot odds to call (200 to call for a 450 pot). However, against a button open from Minni, who has been relatively active, this hand is a bit too weak to defend profitably, especially out of position in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy (15,225) and I want to wait for better spots to commit chips, especially as we approach the final hands of the tournament.

K2 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability, dominated by any king or pair, and little high card strength. Facing a limped pot from SB (Flash completing to 100), calling 100 into 250 effective pot (after call) gives poor implied odds even in position on button. Raising would require folding out BB frequently, but fold equity is low with trash range, and we'd be OOP if called. Deep stacks, near tournament end (134/150), better to wait for premium spots to build stack. Opponents show aggression historically but limping suggests weakness. Standard fold to preserve stack.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). In blind-vs-blind situations, a King-high hand is quite strong. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and try to win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand.

I have 8♦3♣ — a very weak, unplayable hand. Flash opened from the small blind to 300; if I call I’ll be out of position postflop (SB is effectively the button) and will face difficult decisions with almost no equity or blocker value. A 3‑bet bluff is possible sometimes, but 8‑3o has poor blockers and terrible playability, so it’s a poor candidate to apply aggression with deep stacks. Although the call is cheap (200 into a 400 pot), the hand performs poorly OOP and I don’t want to invest chips in marginal spots with 16 hands left. I’ll fold and wait for a better situation.

Js 5s is a speculative suited hand that is worth opening on the button in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy, and raising here allows me to take control of the hand in position against the blinds.

Facing a button open to 300 from the BTN with the big blind yet to act. I hold 7♣4♦ in the small blind — a very weak, easily dominated hand. Calling 250 into a 450 pot gives ~36% break-even equity, but 7-4o’s actual equity vs a BTN open (and likely continuing BB) is well below that, and I’ll be out of position for the whole hand. A min‑raise here would be a fancy bluff but is unnecessary and risky with two players to act. With deep stacks late in the tournament I’d rather preserve chips for better spot. Fold.
