Pocket eights is a premium hand facing a button raise from Flash, who tends to open wide from late position based on hand history (frequent raises, steals blinds). 3-betting for value and fold equity: gets worse hands like Ax suited, broadways to fold or call light; our hand plays well postflop OOP with set-mining potential and blocker to sets. Deep stacks (~100bb effective), so 3x sizing to 900 is standard - builds pot, maintains pressure without overcommitting. Calling risks multiway pots or postflop OOP disadvantage; folding is too tight.

I opened the button with Js 5s for 300. Elon has 3-bet from the big blind to 900. While Js 5s has some playability due to being suited and connected, calling a 3-bet out of position against Elon (who has shown aggression in previous hands) is generally not profitable with such a weak hand. I have a comfortable stack and there is no need to defend this speculative hand against a significant raise. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed pot with 4♥6♦ — a weak offsuit holding but I have position and deep stacks. In 3-max play I should open a wide range from the button to steal the blinds and play postflop in position. Raising to 300 (≈3x BB) applies pressure, denies correct pot odds to the blinds, and is a standard exploitative sizing here. Plan: if I get one caller I'll play the flop in position and c-bet favorable textures; if I face a 3-bet I will usually fold this hand (4-6o has poor equity vs a 3-bettor's value range) unless the 3-bet is tiny and I have a clear postflop plan. This raise balances aggression and pot control to maximize EV over the remaining hands.

T5o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind facing a button raise to 3x from Minni. Minni's button range is wide, but T5o has low equity against it (~30-35% vs typical button open) and terrible post-flop potential OOP on deep stacks. Calling commits chips poorly and risks difficult spots; folding preserves my healthy 108BB stack for better spots late in the tournament (hand 136/150). No value to raising as a bluff here either.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s8s, a strong speculative hand. Minni raised to 300 from the Button. This is a standard opening range, and 9s8s plays very well multi-way or heads-up in position, although here I will be out of position. Given the stack depths and the pot odds (I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 650), this is a mandatory call. Folding is too tight for a suited connector, and raising is unnecessary as I want to keep the pot manageable and realize my equity post-flop.

I am first to act on the flop after calling from the Big Blind. The board is 6h-2h-4d, which does not connect with my 9s8s. Standard play for the Big Blind defender is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's bet sizing, though with only overcards and no real draws, I am likely folding to any significant pressure.

I flopped two pair (6s and 4s) on 6♥ 2♥ 4♦. Flash checked; as button I have position and should both extract value from weaker one-pair hands and protect against heart flush draws. Pot is 650 — a caller with a 4-to-a-heart draw (9 outs) has ~35% to make by the river, so a flop bet needs to be >≈350 to deny correct pot odds. I size to 400 (~60% pot): large enough to charge/marginalize draws and get value from worse pairs/heart+overcards, but not so large as to overcommit if I face a big check-raise. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue for value/protection on safe cards; if he check-raises big I’ll give credit to sets/trips and proceed cautiously.

On a flop of 6h-2h-4d, my 9s8s has two overcards but no direct draws to the nuts. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has a range that includes overpairs, high cards, and some straight draws. Her bet of 400 into a 650 pot represents about 60% of the pot. Given that I have zero showdown value right now and my overcards are likely not clean outs (especially with the heart draw present), calling would be a 'hope' play. I don't have enough equity to float this bet out of position against a player who has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving move here.

AhTh is a premium suited ace hand on the button with position on the big blind. SB likely folded given no actions and standard pot size. Raising here applies pressure, builds the pot with a strong hand, and takes advantage of position for post-flop play. Standard raise size of 3x the BB to 300 total. Pot odds favorable, high equity against BB calling range.

8d 5h is a very weak starting hand, especially out of position in the Small Blind. Elon raised from the Button, which is often a wide range, but 85o lacks the equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet. With only 13 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack, I should wait for better spots rather than defending with marginal air.

I’m on the big blind with 9d-2d — a very weak, non-connecting suited hand. Elon opened to 300 from the button; the pot is 450 and I must call 200, which gives ~2.25:1 pot odds (≈30.8% equity needed). Against a standard button opening range my raw equity with 9♦2♦ is well below that and I’ll be out of position postflop, so my realized equity will be even worse. A tiny 3-bet bluff to 500 would be overly spewy and often commit me to tough spots. Preserve the stack and fold this marginal holding.

I am on the button with a strong pocket pair, Eight of Spades and Eight of Diamonds. This is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. I want to build the pot and take control of the hand. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard and effective opening size here.

I’m in the small blind (50) facing a button open to 300. I hold Qh4d — a weak, offsuit hand that’s often dominated by the raiser’s BTN range and plays very poorly out of position against both the button and the big blind. Pot is 450 and it’s 250 to call (calling would give ~35–36% immediate pot odds), but I won’t have fold equity and I’ll struggle to realize equity OOP in a multiway pot. A 3-bet bluff is thin here (not enough blockers and stacks are deep so BB can call or cold-4bet). Better to preserve my 14k+ stack for a stronger spot in position. Fold.

7s2h is one of the worst possible hands with no connectivity, suitedness insignificant (different suits actually offsuit), and dominated by any raising range. Flash's button raise after SB fold is a standard steal attempt with a wide range. Defending BB with this hand risks spew; pot odds of calling 200 into 650 (post-call) are poor given my <20% equity vs typical button range. Deep stacks mean no need to gamble; fold preserves stack for better spots in late tournament.

Button with QQ into two players (SB Elon 10,850, BB Flash 14,725). This is a premium hand — raise for value and to isolate. The current pot is tiny (150) and the minimum raise is 200, so a standard 3x BB open to 300 builds the pot, keeps dominated hands in, and applies pressure without overcommitting a large portion of my 14,275 stack. If I face a small 3-bet (typical 900–1,500 range) I will re-evaluate (mostly call or 4-bet depending on opponent and sizing); fold to an extreme shove only if the price and reads make QQ a coinflip or worse. Primary plan: raise to 300 to get value and initiative on the button.

Pocket deuces is a speculative hand with strong set-mining potential and good implied odds given the deep effective stacks (around 100BB+). Minni's button raise to 3BB is likely wide, including many hands we dominate or have live cards against. Folding preflop would be too tight in this spot 3-handed. 3-betting out of position risks playing a small pair postflop with initiative lost if shoved on or flat-called, and our stack isn't suited for a light 3-bet bluff here. Calling is best: excellent pot odds (250 to call into 450, ~35% equity needed but we have ~50%+ vs typical button range), BB still to act which may close the action or inflate the pot further, allowing us to realize equity cheaply OOP and stack off profitably if we hit a set on a favorable board. Consistent with GTO play in deep-stack 3-handed scenarios.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Three offsuit. Minni opened to 300 from the Button, and Elon called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 3.5:1. In a three-handed game, King-high hands have decent playability, and given the excellent price, I should call to see a flop, even though I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand.

Pocket 2s missed the flop entirely on a Jd As 5s board, placing us behind any Ax, Jx, or paired overcards that hit. The board features a strong ace-high card, spade flush draw, and gutshots/straight draws (e.g., QT, KT for broadways). Preflop, we called a wide button raise for set-mining value deepstacked 3-handed, but now OOP first to act multiway into 900 pot. Our equity is low (~10-20% vs combined ranges heavy on Ax/broadways), no draws, and betting is a pure bluff with poor playability post-bet (reverse SPR issues if called). Checking realizes fold equity from weak villain ranges if they check behind, controls pot OOP, and allows cheap fold to aggression. Consistent with GTO multiway dynamics: don't bluff OOP without equity here. Future plan: fold to most bets, consider floating tiny ones only if villain-specific reads suggest weakness.

I'm in the Big Blind with King-high on a flop of Jack of Diamonds, Ace of Spades, and Five of Spades. Minni, who opened pre-flop, is yet to act, and Elon has already checked. This board strongly favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which likely includes many Ace-X and Jack-X hands. My hand has no direct connection to the board and very little potential to improve to the best hand, especially out of position against two opponents. Checking is the most standard and prudent move here, as it allows me to see what Minni does without committing more chips with a weak hand.

I opened to 300 preflop with QQ. Flop Jd As 5s — the Ace is an overcard, but both opponents checked to me. With two players behind I want to take the initiative: a continuation bet extracts value from worse pairs and Jx/Kx hands, charges backdoor draws, and folds out many marginal hands that can outdraw me later. I size to ~2/3 pot (600) — large enough to apply pressure and deny equity to draws/overcards but not so large that I overcommit if I face a strong check‑raise. If I face a pot‑sized check‑raise or shove I will strongly suspect an Ace or better and re‑evaluate (likely fold); if called I'll proceed cautiously on the turn.

Pocket 22 completely whiffed the Jd As 5s flop: no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw. Equity is dismal (~10-15% vs Minni's button c-bet range after two checks, which favors Ax, Jx, sets, two-pair combos, and some flush/straight draws like KsQs/KTs). Pot odds are ~3.5:1 (600 into 1500), requiring 22% equity to call, but we have far less and no additional equity to realize OOP multiway. Calling bloats pot OOP with air, vulnerable to turn aggression from Flash or further Minni bets. Raising is a desperate bluff OOP 3-ways deepstacked, folding out better but getting called by value too often. Folding preserves stack for better spots; consistent with prior plan to fold to flop aggression as a set-mining hand that missed. Minni's history shows aggression but value-oriented postflop here.

On this Ace-high board, my King-high has virtually no showdown value and no significant draws. Minni's 600 continuation bet into two players after raising pre-flop shows significant strength or a strong semi-bluff on an Ace-heavy board. Elon has already folded, and I have nothing to continue with. Continuing would be a waste of chips given the lack of equity and position.

Preflop on the button with T4o offsuit, facing a raise (to call 100 into pot of 150). T4o is a weak hand with poor playability: offsuit, disconnected cards, low kicker on the ten. Even in position with deep stacks (~100BB effective), standard defending range vs a raise doesn't include T4o, as equity is insufficient (~30% vs typical raising range) and postflop potential is limited without suited/connectors value. Opponents Flash and Minni are aggressive raisers from blinds/history, so folding preserves stack for better spots with only 10 hands left in the tournament. No need to speculate here.

I have Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) in the Small Blind. This is a very strong hand for three-handed play, especially when the button (Elon) has folded. Minni is in the Big Blind and has a stack comparable to mine. While I could just call the 50 more to see a flop, raising is generally more effective to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to exert pressure and build the pot with a hand that likely leads Minni's range.

I’m in the big blind with pocket fives against a small‑blind open to 300. Heads‑up this pair is a strong, playable holding — it wants either to take the pot down preflop or go to the flop with fold equity and set‑mining potential. Flash has shown a willingness to open wide from the SB, so a 3‑bet here will (1) isolate him, (2) get value from worse pairs, A‑x and many broadways, and (3) generate fold equity vs his weaker holdings.

Sizing: I’ll raise to 1,000 total. That’s a healthy 3x+ 3‑bet that applies pressure without bloating the pot unnecessarily given deep stacks. If he folds we pick up the pot; if he calls we play for position/postflop with a clear plan: slow‑play sets, c‑bet favourable textures, and exercise caution (likely fold or proceed very carefully) to a large 4‑bet that represents a heavier made hand. With only a few hands left in the tournament I want to accumulate chips but avoid committing too much with a medium pocket pair vs heavy preflop aggression.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o). I raised to 300, and Minni in the Big Blind 3-bet to 1000. In a three-handed game, A8o is a respectable hand, but it performs poorly against a 3-bet when out of position. Minni's 3-bet sizing (3.3x) is substantial and suggests a strong range. Given that we are on hand 140 of 150 and I currently hold a slight chip lead, I want to avoid high-variance situations where I am likely dominated or forced to play a large pot out of position. Calling 700 more to see a flop with a marginal Ace is risky, and 4-betting would likely commit too much of my stack. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to protect my lead in the final 10 hands.

I am on the button with K6o. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game, but Minni has been very active from the blinds. However, given the stage of the tournament (hand 141 of 150), I want to maintain my stack and look for better spots, or attempt to steal the blinds. Raising here to 300 is a standard move to take control of the pot. If reraised, I can easily fold.

I’m in the small blind with 5♦4♦ facing a button open to 300. I’ve already posted 50, so calling costs 250 more (300 total committed). Pot is 450; calling 250 gives ~2.8:1 reward:risk (break-even equity ~35–36%). With deep stacks (15k) and speculative suited connectors, the implied odds to hit straights/flushes make a call profitable enough here. I’m out of position, so a min-raise to 500 would bloat a pot OOP with little fold equity; folding is too tight given the small cost. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously — continue only with strong draws or made hands, and fold to large preflop 3-bets or heavy postflop aggression.

AJo is a strong hand facing a button raise from Flash, who tends to open wide from the button based on history, and a small blind call from Minni, which could be speculative or medium strength. The pot odds to call 200 into 700 are good, but calling OOP potentially multiway is suboptimal. A 3-bet isolates the button, applies pressure, and leverages my stack depth. Raising to 1200 (4x the open) is a standard sizing to fold out worse aces, broadways, and pairs while getting value from worse. Late in the tournament, building the stack aggressively with premium hands is key.

I am on the button with K6o and opened to 300. Minni called from the small blind, but Elon has now squeezed to 1200 from the big blind. Elon's stack is relatively short at 9400, and this 3-bet indicates significant strength or a high-frequency squeeze. K6o does not play well against a 3-bet range, especially out of position relative to the big blind if I were to call, although I have position here. However, calling 900 more with a weak King-high hand in a bloated pot is not profitable. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack for the final few hands of the tournament.

Flash opened to 300 and Elon 3‑bet to 1200. Pot is 1800 and I must call 900 more (total commitment to 1200) — break‑even equity ≈ 33.3%. I have 5♦4♦: a small suited connector with limited equity and very poor position against a sizable 3‑bet. Elon has been an active/aggressive 3‑bettor and his range here will be weighted toward strong hands (and some polarized bluffs), so my equity is likely below the required threshold. 4‑betting as a bluff is thin (min raise is 2100) and I don’t have the blockers or position to make that profitable. I already planned to fold to large preflop aggression. Preserve the healthy 14.9k stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I’m on the button with K♠2♣. Pot is 150 and I need to put in 100 to see the flop (min-raise to 200). K2o is a marginal hand to open-raise with — it’s often dominated and Elon (SB) has shown frequent aggression, so a raise risks a 3-bet and a difficult postflop spot. Calling keeps me in position vs both opponents, gets a cheap look at the flop with a playable hand (occasional K/2 connections and backdoor draws), and preserves stack depth to exploit postflop. Plan: see the flop, proceed depending on texture (value/continue with top pair or good draws; fold to large show of strength).

82o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing a button limp into a limped pot, completing the SB for 50 into 300 (pot will be ~300 after call) gives terrible implied odds and equity multiway (postflop likely 3-handed). No reason to raise this speculative hand for value or bluff. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament with deep stacks (111 BB). Opponent history shows loose play but doesn't justify defending this.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of fives. Minni has limped from the button, and Elon has folded from the Small Blind. Given the current stage of the tournament and the fact that I can see the flop for free, checking is a safe, low-variance play. While pocket fives are a decent hand, they are vulnerable post-flop to many overcards, so keeping the pot small and evaluating the flop is a prudent strategy.

I have a low pocket pair (5s) on a board with two overcards (Jack and Nine). Since the pot is small and I'm out of position against a button limper, checking is the most appropriate move to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. My hand has some showdown value but is very vulnerable to further action or overcards on later streets.

I called preflop with K♠2♣ to see a flop and keep position. Flop is 4♠-9♣-J♣. I have no pair but I do have a King (the highest card) and one club (a very weak backdoor two-card club draw). Flash checked, indicating a wide, often missed BB defending range (lots of Kx/Qx, Jx, small pairs, and club draws). In position I should take the initiative — a c-bet will fold many hands that beat my King-high (weak overcards, unpaired hands) and charge draws. I don't want to overcommit with a mediocre hand, so a half-pot-ish c-bet is best: it applies pressure, keeps control of the pot if called, and leaves room to fold to heavy aggression or continue if I improve. Bet 150 into the 250 pot; if called I'll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue aggressively with improvement or a clear read that Flash is calling light.

I am holding a low pocket pair on a board that contains two overcards, a Jack and a Nine. While I checked the flop to control the pot size, Minni's bet of 150 into the 250 pot puts me in a difficult position. Calling out of position with an underpair is generally a losing strategy, as I will likely face further pressure on the turn and river with very few ways to improve my hand. Given the late stage of the tournament and the need to preserve my stack, I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Preflop on the button with Q8o, a speculative hand with queen blocker and backdoor potential. Position is ideal for stealing blinds in a 3-handed spot with deep stacks. Pot is 150 (blinds), to call 100. Opponents have shown aggression in history but also frequent folds to raises. Standard play is to raise to ~3x the BB to attempt a steal, as folding range is wide here late in the tournament. Raising to 300 puts pressure and builds pot in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o. Elon, who is on the Button, has raised to 300. K3o is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise, especially with a chip leader like Minni still to act in the Big Blind. Calling 250 more into a pot of 450 with a marginal hand and being out of position for the rest of the hand is generally not profitable. Given we are near the end of the tournament (hand 143 of 150), preserving my stack (currently 13,775) is more important than defending with a weak King in this spot.

Elon opened to 300 from the button and the SB folded. I’m in the big blind with 8s7s — a suited connector that plays very well heads-up. Calling (matching his 300) is the best option: I get favorable pot odds (calling 200 into a 450 pot, ~2.25:1) and good implied odds with deep stacks (14925 vs 10850). I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a speculative hand — that surrenders position postflop and invites 4-bets or costly decisions. Plan is to call, see a flop, proceed aggressively on favorable boards (flush/straight/paired with backdoor equity) and control the pot on dry boards.

Flop: Qc 4c Qs — I have 8s7s: no pair, only backdoor spade/straight potential. Elon was the preflop raiser and checked the flop; that check could be weakness or a trap, but I shouldn’t bloat the pot out of position with virtually no equity. Pot is small (650) relative to our deep stacks, so a bluff here risks committing a lot on later streets if called. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity and see a turn — if I pick up a spade or meaningful straight draw I can bluff/semi-bluff on the turn or river; if Elon leads out with significant aggression I’ll fold this unimproved hand. Checking preserves stack and keeps options open.

Flopped top set with Qd on a Qc 4c Qs board, an absolute monster disguising as possible top pair or flush draw. Paired board reduces straight draws but flush draw is live (clubs). Minni called preflop raise from BB after check-fold from SB, so her range includes suited connectors, Ax suited (esp clubs), broadway hands, mid pairs. She checked flop, which is standard but invites a bet. Need to bet for value from worse made hands (KQ, AJ, smaller pairs) and protection vs. draws. Avoid check-raise traps or free cards. Pot 650, bet ~2/3 pot (450) balances value and fold equity while keeping stack deep. Builds pot for turn/river value extraction.

Call. The pot is 1,100 and Elon’s bet is 450, so calling commits 450 into a 1,550 pot (I need roughly ~29% equity to break even). I have 8s7s on Qc-4c-Qs: no pair but two spades in my hand + the Qs on board gives backdoor spade potential (low by itself) and modest pair/straight outs by the river. Elon was the preflop raiser and this size looks like a standard continuation bet — he can be betting a wide range here. With deep stacks I prefer to realize equity and see the turn rather than try a risky OOP bluff/raise on a paired queen board. Strategy: call to keep the pot manageable and float — fold to heavy turn aggression unless I pick up a meaningful draw (spade, 8/7 or open-ender) or a cheap card that allows a profitable bluff on the river. If the turn blanks and Elon barreled large, I’ll fold; if I improve or see weakness I’ll look to apply pressure.

Board: Qc 4c Qs 9h. I hold 8s7s — I have no pair, no flush (backdoor flush was killed on the turn) and no realistic straight draw with just one card to come. My only clean river outs are three 8s and three 7s (6 outs ≈ 13%), and those pairs still lose to any Queen or many hands in Elon’s raising/c-betting range. Elon was the preflop raiser and continued on the flop, so his range is weighted toward Qx/strong broadways/overpairs — not a spot where I can expect reliable fold equity by barreling out of position.

Given the weak equity, pot control is the correct line: check the turn and go to the river. Betting here bloats the pot out of position with very little fold equity and turns difficult decisions into costly mistakes. Plan: check to see the river; if Elon fires a large bet on the river I will fold unless I pair up to a convincing two-pair or the bet is trivially small. If the river bricks and he checks, I can consider a small bluff on the river, or a small value bet only if I pair and think it can get called.

Action: check to control the pot, realize equity, and avoid committing chips OOP with a marginal hand.

Turn card 9h is a relatively blank for most of villain's range, bringing possible heart draws but not completing the primary club flush draw. I have top set of queens (trips) on a paired queen board, which is one of the best possible hands here—strong against any single queen (only Qh left in deck), overpairs, mid pairs like 99/JJ/TT which improved or not, flush draws (esp clubs), and straight draws (limited). Villain defended BB vs button raise pre (wide range: suited aces/broadways, suited connectors, pairs), checked flop and called 450 into ~650 (shows strength but not trapping with nuts), checks turn again suggesting no strong made hand or cautious with draw. With position and deep stacks (my 10400 vs her 14275), continue value betting to extract from worse made hands (KQ/AQ without Q kicker, 44 set, overpairs), draws chasing, and thin value. Bet sizing ~2/3 pot (1550) at 1000 builds pot while denying equity to draws and keeps fold equity vs. stubborn bluffs. Avoid checking as it gives free cards to clubs/hearts/straights and misses value. Plan to barrel river unless obvious scare.

Board: Qc 4c Qs 9h. I hold 8s7s: no pair, no realistic draw. My only clean river outs are three 8s and three 7s (6 outs ≈ 12% equity). The pot is 2550 and Elon’s turn bet is 1000, so a call requires roughly 28% equity to be break-even. My ~12% raw equity is well below the pot odds, and Elon’s line (preflop raise, sizable flop c‑bet and a second barrel on the turn) is consistent with a value-weighted range (Qx, strong broadways, pocket pairs/full houses) — not a line I can profitably chase with one-pair outs. Raising or shoving OOP as a bluff here is too high-variance and unlikely to fold a large enough portion of his range. Pot control and surviving to better spots three-handed is the right long-term play. I’ll fold and preserve my 14,275 stack for better equity situations.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at 13,775 chips. Standard play from the button is to raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. A raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) is consistent with my previous aggressive patterns and serves to either win the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop situation where I have both the initiative and position.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind with 3♠5♦. Pot is 450 and I must call 250 (or raise to at least 500). This hand is very weak and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. Immediate pot odds for a call (250 into a 700 pot after I call) require ~35.7% equity — 3‑5o has far less against a typical button opening range, and if the big blind (Elon) gets involved I’ll be OOP in a multiway pot with almost no fold equity. A reraise here would be a thin bluff that’s likely to be called or 3‑bet by better hands. With ~142 BBs and only a few hands left in the tournament, there’s no need to gamble with junk — preserve the stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Pocket fives is a strong hand for set mining against a button raise, which is likely wide-ranging given position. Pot is 450, calling 200 more for odds of about 3.25:1, excellent for implied odds with deep stacks (118 BB effective). Flash is aggressive from history, but no fold equity needed; prefer calling to realize equity post-flop OOP. 3-bet risks too much without premium hand. Late tournament, preserve stack but defend value hands.

Pocket 5s is a solid underpair on a dry Q-T-2 rainbow flop. Limited draws (spade flush draw, few straights like J9/KJ). Flash's button range is wide (Ax, KQ, suited broadways, connectors), often missing. Checking first OOP promotes pot control, induces bluffs/checks behind, realizes equity (around 35-40% vs wide range). Betting risks no value (few worse hands call), vulnerable to check-raise, larger pots OOP bad. If bets, call small/medium sizes leveraging implied odds for set/straight potential turns. Deep stacks (116BB), no need to force action on favorable board for passive play. Late tournament, prioritize equity realization over thin value.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Seven offsuit and was called by Elon in the Big Blind. The flop comes Queen-Ten-Deuce, which contains two broadway cards. While this board can connect with a Big Blind defending range (e.g., J-T, T-9, K-J), as the pre-flop aggressor, I still hold the range advantage with more high pairs and strong Queens in my range. I will place a standard continuation bet of roughly half the pot to put pressure on his marginal hands and potentially take the pot down immediately. If he calls, I have some showdown value with Ace-high and can check back on many turn cards.

Pocket 5s remains a strong hand on this dry Q-T-2 rainbow flop facing Flash's 1/2 pot c-bet from the button. Flash's range is wide due to position: includes Ax, KQ, suited broadways, connectors, and bluffs. 55 has excellent equity (~35-40% vs. polarized c-bet range), crushes bluffs, set mines effectively, and has straight potential (34,67). Pot odds are favorable: 300 to call into 950 (pot after call 1250), requiring ~19% equity which we exceed. Deep stacks (116BB effective) provide massive implied odds for sets/trips. Check-raising risks bloating pot OOP vs. aggressive opponent without premium; folding surrenders too much equity. Calling induces bluffs on later streets, controls pot size OOP, and realizes equity best long-term. Late in tournament (144/150), prioritize equity over forcing action on favorable board.

Pocket 5s remains a strong speculative hand heads-up OOP vs Flash's aggressive button range on this Qs-Td-2c-7d board. The turn 7d adds a diamond draw but doesn't complete strong hands like straights (possible 98, J9 draw incomplete). My equity holds at ~30-35% vs Flash's polarized range (value: Qx, Tx, sets like QQ/TT/22; bluffs: Ax, KQ, suited connectors missing). Pot 1250, deep stacks (~110BB effective), checking maintains pot control OOP, induces bluffs/checks behind (Flash often checks turns after c-bet), realizes equity best without bloating pot. Betting risks check-raise from value or folds bluffs, bad with no showdown value/disguise. Late tournament (144/150), prioritize survival/equity over forcing thin value on drawy board. Will call medium bets (~500-800) leveraging implied odds; fold to large overbets signaling nuts.

I paired my Seven on the turn, which gives me decent showdown value against Elon's range. However, the board still features a Queen and a Ten, both of which are likely to have connected with his Big Blind defending range. Since Elon has checked to me for a second time, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the size of the pot with a medium-strength hand, avoid a potential check-raise, and see the river for free to evaluate whether my pair of Sevens is good enough to win at showdown.

Pocket 5s on this Qs-Td-2c-7d-Ks river board remains a medium-strength bluff-catcher OOP vs. Flash's aggressive button range. The river King of spades completes the spade flush draw (Qs-Ks) and adds backdoor straight possibilities (e.g., J9), strengthening Flash's value range (KQ, AK, QJ, Tx good, sets like QQ/TT/22) while his bluffs (missed draws like J9s, 98s spade/diamond draws, A-high blockers) remain live. My check-call flop + mutual check turn history assigns Flash a polarized range: strong value that checks thin air/weak or traps, and bluffs checking back. Equity ~25-30% vs. this range (beats bluffs/A-high, loses to pairs+). Deep stacks (113BB effective), pot 1250 offers no great bet sizing for thin value (small bets called by better, large induce raises); betting OOP risks bloating pot or folding out better. Checking induces bluffs/value bets I can hero-call profitably (pot odds ~3:1 on medium bets), controls pot OOP, realizes showdown value cost-free, and avoids tough spots late in tournament (144/150 hands). Flash aggressive post-flop but checks turns often behind (per history), likely to bluff-catch or value bet thinly. Prioritize survival/equity over forcing action with marginal hand.

On the river, the King of Spades (Ks) completes the board. My hand is a pair of Sevens with an Ace kicker. Elon has checked to me for the third time (flop, turn, and river) after calling my flop continuation bet. The King is a significant card that could have improved Elon's range (specifically hands like K-J or K-9) or completed a straight if he held A-J or J-9. While I have improved to a pair of Sevens, it remains a relatively weak showdown hand on this board. Betting here is unlikely to get better hands to fold and will almost certainly only be called by hands that beat me (like a Ten or a Queen). Therefore, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most optimal and safest play to conclude the hand.

On the button with 8♥5♠ — a very weak offsuit hand. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop, so I need roughly 40% equity to break even (100 / (150+100)). 8‑5o performs poorly multiway and is easily dominated; raising to 200 as a steal is mediocre with two players to act and risks a 3‑bet from an aggressive SB (Elon) or a defended BB (Flash). With deep stacks and only a few hands left in the tournament, I’ll preserve my stack for better spots rather than limp or open with junk. Fold.

Hand 145, SB with Js9c vs BB (Flash) after BTN (Minni) folds. Heads-up, deep stacks (~113BB). J9o is within SB stealing range due to playability (straight potential) and fold equity. Limping is too passive; folding too tight late tournament. Raise to ~2.5x BB total (250) to apply pressure, build pot OOP with decent hand, and exploit BB's wide defending range. Goal to maximize stack by accumulating small pots frequently.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine of hearts, a strong suited king. The action folded to Elon in the Small Blind who raised to 250. This is a standard small blind open. My hand is well above the range required to defend the big blind, especially against a small blind open which is typically very wide. I have the option to call or 3-bet. Given the stack depths and the late stage of the tournament, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that plays well post-flop while keeping the pot manageable. Calling 150 more into a pot of 350 is a very good price.

Flop: Q♦5♦4♦ is extremely coordinated and draw-heavy (flush draws, open-enders like A3, 63, 76; combo draws). My Js9c is pure air: no pair, no flush draw (offsuit non-diamond), gutshot unlikely (T7 for straight, but blocks nothing major). OOP vs BB caller who defends wide. Pre-flop raise was a steal attempt, but range doesn't connect well here. Betting risks getting called/raised by any draw/value (Qx, Ax suited, suited connectors) while gaining little fold equity on this texture where villain floats or has equity. Check preserves stack, induces bluffs on later streets if villain bets weak, or takes free card. Late tournament (145/150), deep stacks (~110BB), prioritize survival and selective aggression. Check to pot control/exploit.

The flop is monotone diamonds (Qd 5d 4d) and I hold Kh 9h, meaning I have no diamonds and no piece of the board. Elon checked from the Small Blind after a pre-flop raise. This board is dangerous because any diamond in his hand has significant equity. Since I have no showdown value and no draw, betting is risky as I would be forced to fold to any resistance. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free and potentially see if Elon shows further weakness on a non-diamond turn.

Turn board pairs the Queen (Q♦ Q♥), making the texture even drier for my complete air (Js9c: jack-high, no pair, no draws, no blockers to strong hands like Qx, sets, two-pair, straights). Preflop as SB raiser, my range is polarized (value: AQs+, pairs; bluffs: suited connectors, Ax suited), but flop check caps my range somewhat OOP vs BB defender who calls wide and checks back (likely weak pairs, stubborn draws, floats). Pot 500, deep stacks (111BB), 145/150 hands: prioritize survival and selective spots over spewing chips into a paired board where villain has ample equity/SDV (e.g., any Q, 65s for str8, diamond draws with showdown). Betting risks calls/raises from better (minimal fold equity as turn slows action); induces no bluffs likely. Check induces villain bluffs (missed draws now bricked), controls pot, takes free river card (to villain-lead or check down), exploits history of check-check pots. Maximizes EV long-term by avoiding variance late.

Elon raised pre-flop from the small blind and has now checked both the flop and the turn on a paired, monotone board (Qd 5d 4d Qh). I hold Kh 9h, which has no diamonds and only King-high showdown value. While Elon's checks suggest weakness, betting into this board is risky because any diamond in his hand holds significant equity or a made flush. By checking back, I realize my equity for free and can potentially win at showdown against his total air or improve to a winning pair on the river without risking a check-raise.

River J♥ pairs the board's lower end (Jacks), giving my Js9c second pair (Jacks) with a weak 9 kicker on a paired Queens board (Q♦Q♥) from a scary diamond flop (5♦4♦Q♦). Preflop as SB raiser OOP, my range is polarized (strong value like AQ/QJ/KQ pairs/set mines; bluffs suited connectors/Ax), but double check-back (flop/turn) caps me somewhat while villain's BB call-check range includes medium strength (weak Qx, stubborn diamond draws like A♦x/K♦x that bricked, floats, small pairs). Pot 500 tiny vs deep stacks (111BB, 145/150 hands), survival key late tournament. Betting thin value risks: folds from worse (missed draws, ace-high bluffs I beat) while called/raised by better (top pair Qx, two-pair QJ/Q5, full houses QQ/QJ, sets). Minimal fold equity as villain unlikely to have total air after checking back twice on draw-heavy board. Checking maximizes EV: induces bluffs from missed draws (now bricked harder), controls pot (no spew), showdowns profitably vs villain's capped range (beats floats/medium missed hands, loses to value but frequency balanced). Exploits history of check-check pots, avoids variance, prioritizes stack preservation/accumulation in final hands.

Elon has checked three streets after opening from the Small Blind. The board is quite dangerous with a possible flush and a pair of Queens. My King-high has some showdown value against his total bluffs and missed lower straight draws. While a bet might force a fold from Ace-high or a small pocket pair, it is safer to check and take the free showdown in a small pot, especially this late in the tournament.

3-handed on the button pre-flop with blinds posted (pot 150, to call BB 100). Standard first-in raise from BTN in short-handed play is very wide, around 70-80% of hands, due to excellent position post-flop and fold equity against blinds. Tc3h offsuit is marginal but playable for thin value/steal, with some backdoor straight potential and live cards. Opponents have shown passive tendencies in history with many checks and small pots. Raise to 2.5x BB (250 total) to build pot or take it down immediately. Deep stacks allow maneuvering post-flop if called.

I have been dealt pocket kings (Kd Kh), the second strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With only five hands remaining in the tournament and being in the Small Blind, I need to maximize the value of this premium hand. Elon, on the button, has opened for 250. My goal is to build a large pot now and potentially get all the chips in, especially given the aggressive play we've seen from both Elon and Minni throughout the session. A standard 3-bet from the Small Blind here would be roughly 3 to 4 times the open, but I want to induce a call or a 4-bet shove from Elon. I will raise to 900 to put pressure on both Elon and the Big Blind, Minni.

Facing a button open to 250 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 900. I’m in the big blind with 9♦5♠ (offsuit). Pot is 1250 and it costs 800 to call — I’d need ~39% equity to break even (800 / (1250+800) ≈ 39%). This hand is very weak, dominated by nearly all reasonable 3‑bet ranges, and I’ll be out of position multiway if I continue. A small 4‑bet bluff to 1550 isn’t credible here and shoving is too risky with such a marginal holding. With deep stacks and better spots ahead, the correct play is to preserve chips and fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash in the SB after my BTN open-raise with T♣3♥ offsuit, a complete trash hand with no blockers, low equity, and poor playability. Flash's 3-bet sizing (3.6x) suggests strength or positionally exploiting my wide BTN range; history shows Flash aggressive with frequent raises but profitable. Pot odds ~2:1 (650 to call into 1250) require ~25% equity, but this hand realizes <15% vs typical 3-bet range (pairs, broadways, suited aces, suited connectors). No fold equity on further raise without position advantage post-flop. Deep stacks, but folding marginal open is +EV long-term to preserve stack for superior spots in final hands of tournament. Immediate fold maximizes EV.

I have KTs on the button, which is a strong starting hand for three-handed play. I have the opportunity to raise and take control of the pot. Minni and Elon have been somewhat active, but KTs is well ahead of their likely calling ranges in this position. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the blinds while remaining efficient.

Facing a standard button open to 300 from the Button while I'm in the small blind with 3♦2♥: this is one of the weakest non-pair hands. Call would cost 250 to play out of position against two opponents (I'll be first to act postflop and Elon in the BB can still 3‑bet), and a raise (to 500+) with 3‑2o is pure bluff with poor blocker value and likely to be punished by a caller or 3‑bettor given deep stacks. Preserving stack and waiting for a better spot is +EV here. Fold and conserve chips for stronger situations.

Preflop in BB with 4d5h suited connectors against a button raise to 300 after SB folds. Pot is 450, calling 200 more for ~22% pot odds (pot will be ~650 after call). 45s has excellent implied odds potential with straight and flush possibilities, good playability multiway or heads-up, and solid equity (~38-42%) against a typical wide button opening range (25-40% of hands). Folding is too tight OOP vs BTN; 3-betting is suboptimal as it's not strong enough for value and has poor post-flop playability if called. Calling keeps the pot small, realizes equity, and allows post-flop maneuvering. Late tournament but stacks deep (~110BB effective), so speculative hands like this defend profitably. Opponents' history shows Flash aggressive from late position, increasing fold equity post-flop if needed.

Flop Kh9dAs completely misses my 4d5h. No pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (gutshot to 67 or wheel but unlikely). Pure air against Flash's button opening range, which includes many Ax, Kx, 98, broadways that connect well with this Ace-high board. OOP as BB, standard play is check-fold to any reasonable bet size. Donk betting is -EV with zero equity and poor playability; better to realize we have nothing and pot control/minimize losses. Deep stacks but no fold equity advantage first in. If miracle check-behind, we get free turn, but expect bet/call-off range too strong.

I have middle pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board. Elon checked to me after calling from the Big Blind. While I have a piece of the board, the presence of the Ace makes this a marginal situation for value betting. Checking back is a solid play here to control the pot size and realize my equity. Betting might force out hands I already beat while getting called primarily by better hands like Aces. This move keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see a turn card for free.

Turn pairs my 4d for bottom pair with a terrible 5h kicker on an A-K-9-4 board. While the 4h brings some heart draws into play, my hand is still very weak and vulnerable to any Ax, Kx, 98, or better pairs in Flash's wide button range. Flash checked behind on the dangerous A-high flop, suggesting possible medium strength, missed draws, or traps, but doesn't change that OOP with this holding, betting risks getting called/raised by better and playing river OOP is miserable. Checking keeps pot small, realizes any showdown value if he has worse (e.g., pure air/draws), and avoids bloating pot late in tournament (hand 147/150) where stack preservation matters with deep effective stacks (~110BB). If he bets, fold to avoid tough spots; free river card is fine as board doesn't complete much scary for him.

On this Ace-high board, my pair of Kings is a strong second-best hand but remains vulnerable. Since the flop went check-check, it's unlikely Elon has a strong Ace, but he could be trapping or holding a marginal 9. By checking back on the turn, I continue to control the pot size and ensure I reach the river to see if he checks again. This allows me to either pick up a value bet on the river if he checks a third time or call a reasonable lead-out bet from him, while avoiding a potential check-raise that would force me off my equity.

River 9c pairs the board's 9d, creating two pair possibilities (A9, K9, 99) and completing some straight draws (e.g., T8), while bringing a club that doesn't complete obvious flushes. My 4d5h holds bottom pair with a terrible 5 kicker on an A-K-9-9-4 board, vulnerable to any Ace, King, better 9, or two-pair combos in Flash's wide button opening range (~25-40% hands). Flash's double check-behind (flop and turn) on a scary A-high board suggests he may have missed draws, medium pairs (e.g., JJ-TT, QJ), air (suited connectors), or is trapping a monster (unlikely given aggression history). Betting OOP risks raising from better hands, thin value calls from worse (e.g., pure air unlikely to call), and bloating pot unnecessarily late (hand 147/150) with deep stacks (~110BB). Checking maximizes showdown value against bluffs/missed hands, induces bets from worse (which I can call small or fold), preserves stack for final hands where ICM/ survival matters more. No strong blockers; pot control optimal with marginal hand.

I have Two Pair, Kings and Nines, with an Ace kicker on a board of Kh-9d-As-4h-9c. After checking both the flop and the turn to control the pot and underrepresent my hand, Elon has checked for a third time on the river. While my hand is relatively strong, the presence of the Ace and the paired Nine on the board makes betting for value marginal. If I bet, I am likely only getting called by an Ace, a Nine, or perhaps a King. Checking back allows me to safely realize my showdown value and win the pot against his weaker hands, pocket pairs, or missed draws without risking a check-raise near the end of the tournament.

Button in a three-handed pot — I can open a wide range. Q6♦ is marginal but suited and very playable in position. Standard, table-consistent sizing is to raise to ~3x the big blind; 300 is a clean, familiar open that pressures the blinds and denies correct odds to defend. If I get a single caller I’ll play postflop aggressively (c‑bet on dry boards, float/call with backdoor diamonds or Q‑pair equity). If SB (Elon) responds with a large 3‑bet I’ll give it up (he’s shown the ability to 3‑bet heavy); versus a small 3‑bet or isolating call I can continue to play for stacks given deep effective stacks. Raise to 300 to steal/define the pot and use position.

9h3s is a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness advantage (offsuit), poor connectivity, and dominated by any raising range from Minni on the button. Facing a standard 3x raise to 300 (pot now 450, to call 250), calling OOP from SB commits 2.3% of my 11k stack for a speculative hand with negligible equity against her wide button range. Postflop OOP, this hand plays terribly on most boards. With only 3 hands left in the tournament (148/150), stack preservation is key; no need to gamble with garbage. Immediate fold maximizes EV by avoiding tough spots and waiting for premium hands.

I am currently the chip leader with only three hands remaining in the tournament. My hand, 4c 3c, is quite weak, and although it is suited, playing it out of position against an active player like Minni is risky. With such a slim lead (only 1,000 chips over Minni), I prefer to fold here and maintain my lead rather than risk a significant portion of my stack in a marginal situation. I will have the button on the final hand, which is a much better position to exert pressure if needed.

3-handed on button with A4o facing a SB limp (Flash checked) and BB (Minni). Deep stacks (~110BB effective). Excellent position. A4o has decent blocker and plays well postflop in position if called. SB limps are often weak; raising steals blinds frequently and builds pot with ace high. Opponents aggressive but fold to button raises often in history. Min-raise too small; size to 3x BB (300) for value/steal. Late in tournament, apply pressure to maximize stack.

We are currently the chip leader in hand 149 of 150. Holding 63o in the Small Blind against a button raise from Elon, the hand is simply too weak to defend out of position. With only two hands remaining in the tournament, my priority is to protect my stack and maintain the chip lead rather than entering a pot with a marginal-to-poor hand. Folding is the safest and most mathematically sound decision here.

I’m in the big blind with 2♥7♠ — the single worst starting hand. Elon opened to 300 from the button and the small blind folded, so it’s heads-up. Pot is 450 and it’s 200 to call, which requires ~30.8% equity (200/(450+200)). 2-7 offsuit has far less equity vs a standard BTN opening range and has essentially no playability postflop. Three-bet bluffing here is unnecessary risk (would be dominated often and invites calls/4-bets). With a healthy stack and one hand left in the tourney, the lowest-variance / +EV line is to fold and avoid getting involved with a near-lock losing holding.

It is the final hand of the tournament. I am on the button with Q-5 offsuit. While not a strong hand, I have a comfortable stack and need to decide whether to enter the pot or fold. Given this is hand 150 of 150, my objective is to protect my current stack and solidify my position. Raising here could lead to a confrontation I don't need, and calling with a weak hand like Q-5 offsuit is generally unprofitable. I will fold and secure my current chip count.

Heads-up in the SB with 7♦5♠ (marginal but playable). When the button has folded we effectively have the button postflop (we act last after the flop), so opening wide is correct to seize initiative and use positional advantage. A minimum raise to 200 maximizes fold equity (can pick up the blinds immediately), keeps the pot controllable against bigger stacks, and allows us to see a flop in position if Elon calls. If Elon 3-bets large I’ll concede this marginal hand and fold; if he calls I’ll c-bet or check depending on the flop texture and his reaction. Limping/calling gives him cheap equity and less fold equity, so I prefer the small open-raise.

Preflop in BB facing a small raise to 200 from SB Minni after BTN fold. Hand is AhJc, a premium holding (AJo) in top ~6% of hands, excellent for defense and value 3-betting from BB vs wide SB opening range. Minni has been aggressive preflop throughout history but often folds to 3-bets or aggression. Deep stacks (~110BB), heads-up likely postflop where I have position advantage (act last OOP actually wait no BB is OOP but small pot OOP fine). Calling is passive and allows multiway if Flash wakes up but he's folded; 3-bet sizes to ~3.5x the raise (700 total) builds pot with strong hand, takes initiative, and pressures her wide range. Folding never. Avoid larger 3bet to prevent overcommitment early. Last hand of tournament, maximize EV by playing strong hands aggressively.

I opened to 200 from the SB with 7♦5♠ to steal and play the hand in position. Elon’s 3‑bet to 700 forces me to call 500 into a 900 pot — I’d need roughly 500/(900+500) ≈ 35.7% equity to justify a call. 7♦5♠ offsuit has poor equity and is frequently dominated by a typical 3‑bet range of big cards and pairs. A 4‑bet (min to 1200 or larger) would be overcommitting with no real fold equity or showdown value. Calling to play postflop is marginal and +EV only against an unusually wide, loose 3‑bet range; given Elon's sizing and tendencies I’m better folding and waiting for a clearer spot. Fold to preserve my stack and pick better spots in position.
